nodes.label: Abbas Abdi
nodes.role: Editor-in-chief, Salam and Khordad; former US Embassy hostage-taker turned reformist

data.role: Editor-in-chief, Salam and Khordad; former US Embassy hostage-taker turned reformist
data.bio: Abbas Abdi's personal trajectory encapsulates the contradictions of Iran's reform generation: he was one of the students who stormed the US Embassy in November 1979, and later became editor-in-chief of the reformist [Salam](/orgs/salam-newspaper) newspaper and [Khordad](/orgs/khordad-newspaper), both eventually closed by the judiciary he had once served. He was arrested in 1993 for his reformist writings at Salam and spent eight months in prison. The July 1999 closure of Salam — triggered by his publication of a leaked MOIS document — directly ignited the student uprising. He was a founder of Khatami's Islamic Iran Participation Party. In 2002 he was arrested again after Salam's successor Ayandeh published a public opinion poll showing majority Iranian support for resuming US-Iran talks; this constituted 'illegal' contact with American pollsters. He was sentenced to multiple years in prison.

nodes.label: Abbas-Ali Kadkhodaei
nodes.role: Jurist member of the Guardian Council; former spokesman

data.role: Jurist member of the Guardian Council; former spokesman
data.bio: Abbas-Ali Kadkhodaei is a jurist member of the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council) who served as the Council's public face as spokesman for many years before [Hadi Tahan Nazif](/people/hadi-tahan-nazif) took on that role. Appointed by Secretary [Ahmad Jannati](/people/ahmad-jannati) to the Central Committee for Election Supervision, he was the primary official voice defending the Council's candidate disqualifications. Before the [2020 parliamentary election](/events/parliamentary-election-iran-2020), he warned that 'insults or inflammatory remarks towards the Supreme Leader' would be considered during candidate vetting. He was sanctioned by the US Treasury in February 2020.

nodes.label: Abbas Amir-Entezam
nodes.role: Deputy Prime Minister (1979); Iran's longest-held political prisoner

data.role: Deputy Prime Minister (1979); Iran's longest-held political prisoner
data.bio: Head of Political Office of the [Freedom Movement of Iran](/orgs/freedom-movement-of-iran) (1978–1979). Served as deputy prime minister and government spokesman in [Bazargan](/people/mehdi-bazargan)'s provisional government. Arrested in 1981 on espionage charges widely considered politically motivated. Spent over 20 years in prison, making him Iran's longest-held political prisoner. Released on medical leave in 2010. Died 11 August 2018.

nodes.label: Abbas Araghchi
nodes.role: Minister of Foreign Affairs (2024–present); lead nuclear negotiator

data.role: Minister of Foreign Affairs (2024–present); lead nuclear negotiator
data.bio: Abbas Araghchi is Iran's current Foreign Minister under [Masoud Pezeshkian](/people/masoud-pezeshkian). A career diplomat, he served as Deputy Foreign Minister for Legal and International Affairs and was Iran's lead technical nuclear negotiator during the JCPOA talks under [Mohammad Javad Zarif](/people/javad-zarif). He was one of the architects of the 2015 nuclear deal. After the deal's collapse he remained at the [Ministry of Foreign Affairs](/orgs/ministry-of-foreign-affairs) as a senior official. His appointment as FM under Pezeshkian was seen as a signal of continuity with the reformist-era foreign policy school. His early tenure has been shaped by the post-2026 succession crisis and Iran's recalibrated position under [Mojtaba Khamenei](/people/mojtaba-khamenei).

nodes.label: Abbas Sheibani
nodes.role: Physician; founding member of the Freedom Movement; Minister of Agriculture (1979–1980); five-term member of parliament

data.role: Physician; founding member of the Freedom Movement; Minister of Agriculture (1979–1980); five-term member of parliament
data.bio:
Abbas Sheibani was a physician and political figure who traversed the full arc of post-revolution Iranian politics, from nationalist-religious activism to the conservative establishment. A founding member of the [Freedom Movement of Iran](/orgs/freedom-movement-of-iran) alongside [Mehdi Bazargan](/people/mehdi-bazargan), [Mahmoud Taleghani](/people/mahmoud-taleghani), and [Yadollah Sahabi](/people/yadollah-sahabi) in 1961, he had previously been active in the [National Front](/orgs/national-front-iran). He spent 13 years in the Shah's prisons across nine separate sentences for political activism.

After the [Iranian Revolution](/events/iranian-revolution-1979), Sheibani broke with the Freedom Movement and joined the [Islamic Republican Party](/orgs/islamic-republican-party), aligning with the clerical establishment. He served as a member of the [Islamic Revolutionary Council](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-council) and was elected to the [Assembly of Experts for the Constitution](/orgs/assembly-of-experts-for-constitution) from Tehran Province in 1979. He served as Minister of Agriculture from November 1979 to August 1980.

Sheibani ran for president twice — finishing second to Mohammad-Ali Rajai in July 1981, and serving as the sole approved rival to [Akbar Hashemi Rafsanjani](/people/akbar-hashemi-rafsanjani) in the 1989 election. He served five terms in the [Islamic Consultative Assembly](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly) representing Tehran (1984–2000) and later served three terms on the Tehran City Council. He also served as president of the [University of Tehran](/orgs/university-of-tehran) (1983–1984). [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) personally led funeral prayers at his death in December 2022, calling him a 'long-time friend and sincere believer.'

nodes.label: Abdolali Ali-Asgari
nodes.role: Head of IRIB (2016–2021)

data.role: Head of IRIB (2016–2021)
data.bio: Abdolali Ali-Asgari served as head of [IRIB](/orgs/irib) from 2016 to 2021, appointed by Supreme Leader [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei). His tenure covered a period of major unrest including the December 2017–January 2018 economic protests and the November 2019 protests, during which at least 1,500 people were killed according to Reuters. During the November 2019 internet shutdown, IRIB was the sole source of broadcast news and aired exclusively the government's narrative, characterising protesters as rioters and foreign agents. He was replaced by [Peyman Jebelli](/people/peyman-jebelli) in 2021.

nodes.label: Abdolali Bazargan
nodes.role: Deputy leader of Freedom Movement of Iran; Islamic thinker; reformist politician

data.role: Deputy leader of Freedom Movement of Iran; Islamic thinker; reformist politician
data.bio: Abdolali Bazargan is an Iranian liberal politician, writer, and Islamic intellectual, and the son of [Mehdi Bazargan](/people/mehdi-bazargan), Iran's first post-revolution prime minister. He joined the National Front of Iran in 1961 before moving to his father's [Freedom Movement of Iran](/orgs/freedom-movement-of-iran) in 1977, where he currently serves as deputy leader. He co-authored the January 2010 [Green Movement](/events/green-movement-protests-2009) manifesto with [Abdolkarim Soroush](/people/abdolkarim-soroush), [Mohsen Kadivar](/people/mohsen-kadivar), [Akbar Ganji](/people/akbar-ganji), and [Ataollah Mohajerani](/people/ataollah-mohajerani), calling for the resignation of President Ahmadinejad and free elections under independent supervision.

nodes.label: Abdolkarim Mousavi-Ardabili
nodes.role: Chief Justice of Iran (1981–1989); co-founder of the Islamic Republican Party

data.role: Chief Justice of Iran (1981–1989); co-founder of the Islamic Republican Party
data.bio:
Grand Ayatollah Abdolkarim Mousavi-Ardabili was a senior Shia cleric who served as the second Chief Justice of the Islamic Republic. A longtime supporter of [Ruhollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini), he was selected as a member of the [Islamic Revolutionary Council](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-council) in 1979, was elected to the [Assembly of Experts for the Constitution](/orgs/assembly-of-experts-for-constitution), and was a co-founder of the [Islamic Republican Party](/orgs/islamic-republican-party) alongside [Mohammad Beheshti](/people/mohammad-beheshti), [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei), and [Akbar Hashemi Rafsanjani](/people/akbar-hashemi-rafsanjani).

Following Beheshti's assassination in the [Hafte Tir bombing](/events/hafte-tir-bombing-1981) on 28 June 1981, Khomeini appointed Mousavi-Ardabili as head of the [Judiciary of Iran](/orgs/judiciary-of-iran), a position he held until 1989. As Chief Justice, he oversaw the revolutionary courts and the judicial apparatus during the [Iran-Iraq War](/events/iran-iraq-war). He also served as Tehran's Friday Prayer Imam and was a founding member of the [Expediency Discernment Council](/orgs/expediency-discernment-council) in 1988.

During the [1988 mass executions](/events/mass-executions-1988), Mousavi-Ardabili was the country's highest judicial authority. He publicly advocated for harsh treatment of MEK prisoners, stating at Friday prayers on 5 August 1988 that the judiciary was 'under strong pressure from public opinion' demanding their execution. As head of the Supreme Judicial Council, he oversaw the establishment and operation of the Death Commissions that carried out the killings. [Hossein-Ali Montazeri](/people/hossein-ali-montazeri), then Khomeini's designated successor, wrote letters criticizing Mousavi-Ardabili's role in pressuring authorities to carry out the executions. He was considered a candidate for Supreme Leader in 1989 but [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) was selected instead. He participated in the [Iranian Revolution](/events/iranian-revolution-1979) as a pro-Khomeini activist. In later years, he distanced himself from hardline positions and expressed support for the [Green Movement protests](/events/green-movement-protests-2009) in 2009. He died on 23 November 2016 in Tehran.

nodes.label: Abdolkarim Soroush
nodes.role: Philosopher; religious intellectual; former member of the Cultural Revolution Committee; exile scholar

data.role: Philosopher; religious intellectual; former member of the Cultural Revolution Committee; exile scholar
data.bio:
Abdolkarim Soroush (born Hossein Haj Faraj Dabbagh, 16 December 1945) is the most influential religious intellectual of post-revolutionary Iran, whose theory of the 'Contraction and Expansion of Religious Knowledge' laid the philosophical foundations for the reformist movement of the 1990s. He studied pharmacy in Tehran, then earned a master's degree in analytical chemistry from the University of London before spending five years at Chelsea College studying history and philosophy of science, where he was deeply influenced by Karl Popper's critical rationalism.

As a young intellectual, Soroush was shaped by two towering pre-revolutionary figures. He approached [Morteza Motahhari](/people/morteza-motahhari) at the University of Tehran for philosophical instruction; Motahhari's commentary on Mulla Sadra's philosophy left a lasting impression, and it was reportedly through Motahhari's encouragement that Ayatollah Khomeini first read Soroush's early work. Soroush was also close to [Ali Shariati](/people/ali-shariati) — he was among the first people to travel to Southampton after Shariati's death in 1977. However, Soroush would later sharply criticise Shariati's 'ideologisation' of Islam, arguing that Shariati's turn to Marxist historicism reduced Shiite thought to political ideology, whereas Soroush sought to free religious understanding from ideological rigidity altogether.

Soroush returned to Iran during the [1979 Revolution](/events/iranian-revolution-1979) and was appointed by [Ayatollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini) to the seven-member Cultural Revolution Committee — the body charged with closing Iranian universities and purging them of Western and secular influence during the [Iranian Cultural Revolution](/events/iranian-cultural-revolution-1980) beginning in June 1980. His role on the committee remains contested: conservative critics accused him of obstructing the Islamisation of the human sciences, while opposition figures blamed him for the dismissal of professors and students. Soroush denied that the committee directly ordered purges, maintaining that dismissal decisions were made at the university level by pre-existing purge committees, though he conceded the committee had the power to intervene and had done so in some cases. In 1983 he resigned from the committee, citing disillusionment with the revolution's direction, and never held another official government position. He later acknowledged the 'horrifying summer of 1988' — the [mass executions of political prisoners](/events/mass-executions-1988) — but when challenged about his silence, he responded: 'To have done more would have been a virtue, but not to have done so is no vice,' questioning what other academics, Majlis deputies, and clerics had done to stop the killings.

Through the late 1980s and 1990s Soroush developed his theory that while divine revelation is absolute, human understanding of religion is historically contingent and fallible — a direct challenge to the absolutist claims underlying velayat-e faqih. He lectured regularly at [the University of Tehran](/orgs/university-of-tehran) to large student audiences who would later form the core of [Mohammad Khatami](/people/mohammad-khatami)'s 2nd of Khordad reform movement. Weekly sessions known as the 'Wednesday Meetings' brought together many future reformists around Soroush's ideas, including [Saeed Hajjarian](/people/saeed-hajjarian), [Akbar Ganji](/people/akbar-ganji), and [Emadeddin Baghi](/people/emadeddin-baghi). He co-founded [Kian](/orgs/kian-magazine) magazine in 1991 with [Mashallah Shamsolvaezin](/people/mashallah-shamsolvaezin), which became the most important forum for religious intellectualism in Iran. The magazine published his articles on religious pluralism, hermeneutics, tolerance, and clericalism until it was shut down by direct order of [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) in 1998. He also published his articles in [Kayhan](/orgs/kayhan-newspaper)'s cultural supplement *Kayhan-e Farhangi* until Khatami's 1992 resignation from the Ministry of Culture forced his circle out.

In the mid-1990s, both Soroush and his lecture audiences were repeatedly assaulted by [Ansar-e Hezbollah](/orgs/ansar-e-hezbollah) vigilantes. At a 1995 event commemorating the US Embassy seizure, Supreme Leader Khamenei devoted more time berating Soroush than condemning the United States. Soroush was stripped of three senior academic appointments, banned from publishing, subjected to intelligence interrogations, and had his passport confiscated. He left Iran around 2000 and has since held visiting positions at Harvard, Yale, Princeton, Columbia, Georgetown, and the University of Maryland.

Soroush was an active supporter of the [2009 Green Movement](/events/green-movement-protests-2009). Together with [Mohsen Kadivar](/people/mohsen-kadivar), [Akbar Ganji](/people/akbar-ganji), [Ataollah Mohajerani](/people/ataollah-mohajerani), and [Abdolali Bazargan](/people/abdolali-bazargan), he co-authored a manifesto in January 2010 calling for the resignation of President Mahmoud Ahmadinejad, abolition of the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council)'s vetting power, release of all political prisoners, and freedom of press and media. He described the Green Movement as 'a pluralistic movement, including believers and non-believers, socialists and liberals.' He also signed an invitation for a three-day hunger strike in front of the United Nations in July 2009 to demand the release of those arrested during the protests.

During the [Mahsa Amini protests](/events/mahsa-amini-protests-2022) of 2022, Soroush declared himself 'among the supporters of the blessed Woman, Life, Freedom uprising and long-time fighters against religious despotism.' However, his subsequent gender-related remarks criticising a meeting of Islamic Republic opponents in Washington — directed at one of the participants — drew widespread condemnation. Critics argued that a former member of the Cultural Revolution Committee had 'the least connection' with the Woman, Life, Freedom movement, and that his comments contradicted the movement's core demand for women's autonomy. The controversy underscored an ongoing tension between the older generation of religious reformist intellectuals and the secular, feminist character of the 2022 uprising.

Soroush was named one of Time magazine's 100 most influential people (2005), won the Erasmus Prize (2004), and was ranked among the world's top public intellectuals by Prospect magazine (2008). His major works include *The Theoretical Contraction and Expansion of Religion* (1994) and *Expansion of Prophetic Experience* (1999).

nodes.label: Abdollah Javadi Amoli
nodes.role: Grand Ayatollah; Shia philosopher and marja'

data.role: Grand Ayatollah; Shia philosopher and marja'
data.bio: Grand Ayatollah Abdollah Javadi Amoli is a prominent Shia scholar, philosopher, and marja' (source of emulation) based in Qom. He was elected to the [Assembly of Experts for the Constitution](/orgs/assembly-of-experts-for-constitution) in 1979 and participated in drafting the Islamic Republic's constitution. A professor at the Qom seminary and a member of the [Society of Seminary Teachers of Qom](/orgs/society-of-seminary-teachers-of-qom), he is known for his extensive works on Islamic philosophy, ethics, and Quranic exegesis. He has also served in the [Assembly of Experts](/orgs/assembly-of-experts) for Leadership. In 1989, he was part of the delegation that delivered [Ruhollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini)'s message to Soviet leader Mikhail Gorbachev.

nodes.label: Abdolnaser Hemmati
nodes.role: Former Finance Minister (2024, briefly); former Central Bank Governor (2018–2021)

data.role: Former Finance Minister (2024, briefly); former Central Bank Governor (2018–2021)
data.bio: Abdolnaser Hemmati is an economist who served as Governor of the Central Bank of Iran (2018–2021) and ran as a presidential candidate in the 2021 election. [Masoud Pezeshkian](/people/masoud-pezeshkian) appointed him Finance Minister in August 2024, but he resigned in March 2025. As Central Bank Governor he navigated the severe economic pressure of US sanctions after 2018. His 2021 presidential candidacy was approved by the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council), making him one of the more moderate candidates allowed to run.

nodes.label: Abdul Rahman Ghassemlou
nodes.role: Leader of the Democratic Party of Iranian Kurdistan (PDKI)

data.role: Leader of the Democratic Party of Iranian Kurdistan (PDKI)
data.bio: Abdul Rahman Ghassemlou was the leader of the [Democratic Party of Iranian Kurdistan](/orgs/democratic-party-of-iranian-kurdistan) (PDKI) and a prominent advocate for Kurdish autonomy within Iran. An economist educated in Paris and Prague, he led the PDKI from 1973 until his assassination. In the August 1979 election for the [Assembly of Experts for the Constitution](/orgs/assembly-of-experts-for-constitution), he won over 80% of the vote in West Azerbaijan Province, but [Ruhollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini) personally ordered his credentials rejected. The Islamic Republic subsequently banned the PDKI and launched a military campaign against Kurdish regions. Ghassemlou sought negotiations with Tehran in the late 1980s, but was assassinated on 13 July 1989 in Vienna by Iranian intelligence agents during what he believed were peace talks.

nodes.label: Abolhassan Banisadr
nodes.role: 1st President of the Islamic Republic of Iran (1980–1981)

data.role: 1st President of the Islamic Republic of Iran (1980–1981)
data.bio: Abolhassan Banisadr was the first president of the Islamic Republic, elected in January 1980 with 76% of the vote. An economist and political thinker who had been close to [Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini) in exile, he had previously served as a member of the [Assembly of Experts for the Constitution](/orgs/assembly-of-experts-for-constitution) in 1979, where he was the second-highest vote-getter in Tehran Province. He quickly came into conflict with the clerical establishment and the [Islamic Republican Party](/orgs/islamic-republican-party). He was impeached by parliament in June 1981 and fled to France, where he lived in exile until his death in 2021.

nodes.label: Adnan Tabatabai
nodes.role: German-Iranian academic and analyst

data.role: German-Iranian academic and analyst
data.bio: Adnan Tabatabai is a German-Iranian political analyst and founder of the Center for Applied Research in Partnership with the Orient (CARPO) in Bonn. He was identified as a member of the [Iran Experts Initiative](/events/iei-email-leak-2023). Leaked emails revealed that in May 2014 he offered to ghostwrite op-eds for the Iranian [Foreign Ministry](/orgs/ministry-of-foreign-affairs), writing to [IPIS](/orgs/ipis) head [Mostafa Zahrani](/people/mostafa-zahrani) with the proposal. He attended the inaugural IEI meeting at Vienna's Palais Coburg in May 2014.

nodes.label: Ahmad Alam al-Huda
nodes.role: Friday Prayer Imam of Mashhad; representative of Supreme Leader in Razavi Khorasan

data.role: Friday Prayer Imam of Mashhad; representative of Supreme Leader in Razavi Khorasan
data.bio: Ahmad Alam al-Huda is the hardline Friday Prayer Imam of Mashhad, appointed by [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) as his personal representative in Razavi Khorasan Province. He is the father-in-law of [Ebrahim Raisi](/people/ebrahim-raisi) — Raisi married his daughter Jamileh Alamolhoda. He supervised Saeed Jalili's doctoral dissertation at Imam Sadeq University, and his sermons from Mashhad's Friday prayer platform have long served as a barometer of the hardliner wing's positions on domestic and foreign policy.

nodes.label: Ahmad Ali Masoud Ansari
nodes.role: Iranian economist; former Pahlavi court confidant and financial adviser; memoirist

data.role: Iranian economist; former Pahlavi court confidant and financial adviser; memoirist
data.bio:
Ahmad Ali Masoud Ansari is an Iranian economist and the brother of historian [Ali M. Ansari](/people/ali-m-ansari). Through his father, Mohammad Ali Massoud Ansari, he is a first cousin of [Farah Pahlavi](/people/farah-pahlavi), the last Empress of Iran — a connection that placed him in the innermost circle of the Pahlavi court.

During the 1978–1979 Islamic Revolution, Ansari served as a secret emissary between the Shah and Ayatollah Shariatmadari. After the Shah's fall, he remained with the royal family in exile as a trusted companion, consultant, and emissary — liaising with General Oveissi, Hushang Ansary, and [Shapour Bakhtiar](/people/shapour-bakhtiar) on behalf of the Shah until the monarch's death in Cairo in July 1980. He continued as a financial adviser to the Pahlavi family after the Shah's death, though this relationship eventually ended amid legal and financial disputes.

Ansari later published two memoirs — including *Nagofteh-ha-ye az Tarikh-e Mo'aser* (Untold Stories of Contemporary History) — in which he alleged fraud, feuds, and moral scandals within the royal family. He claimed that [Reza Pahlavi](/people/reza-pahlavi) and his aides were funded by Saudi Arabia and the CIA, and that [Farah Pahlavi](/people/farah-pahlavi) maintained leftist political views that undermined the Shah's rule. These allegations, made through books and interviews on Washington-based Mardom TV and Roodast, have not been publicly addressed by the Pahlavi family.

nodes.label: Ahmad Hosseini Khorasani
nodes.role: Clerical member of the Guardian Council

data.role: Clerical member of the Guardian Council
data.bio: Ahmad Hosseini Khorasani is a clerical member of the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council), appointed by [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei). He is one of the six Islamic jurists (faqihs) on the Council responsible for assessing legislation's conformity with Islamic law.

nodes.label: Ahmad Jannati
nodes.role: Secretary of the Guardian Council (1992–present); former Chairman of Assembly of Experts

data.role: Secretary of the Guardian Council (1992–present); former Chairman of Assembly of Experts
data.bio: Ahmad Jannati has been a member of the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council) since its founding in 1980, and its secretary since 1992 — the longest-tenured figure in any constitutional body of the Islamic Republic. A founding member of the hardline [Haghani Seminary](/orgs/haghani-seminary) in Qom alongside [Mohammad-Taqi Mesbah Yazdi](/people/mohammad-taqi-mesbah-yazdi), he is among the most extreme voices in Iranian politics, repeatedly calling for the execution of political opponents and systematically disqualifying reformist candidates. He served concurrently as Chairman of the [Assembly of Experts](/orgs/assembly-of-experts). His continuation in office into his late nineties has been attributed to political calculation over capacity.

nodes.label: Ahmad Khatami
nodes.role: Member of Guardian Council; substitute Friday Prayer Imam of Tehran (2005–present)

data.role: Member of Guardian Council; substitute Friday Prayer Imam of Tehran (2005–present)
data.bio: Ahmad Khatami is a hardline Shia cleric and member of the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council) and the [Assembly of Experts](/orgs/assembly-of-experts). He shares a surname with reformist President [Mohammad Khatami](/people/mohammad-khatami) but is entirely unrelated — a fact that gave him national prominence in 1997. He was appointed substitute Friday Prayer Imam of Tehran in 2005 by [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei), becoming the youngest person to hold that position. During the [2009 Green Movement protests](/events/green-movement-protests-2009) he declared the protesters to be engaging in *mohareb* (war against God), a capital offence, and called on the judiciary to execute opposition leaders [Mir-Hossein Mousavi](/people/mir-hossein-mousavi) and [Mehdi Karroubi](/people/mehdi-karroubi) as 'leaders of sedition'. Appointed to the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council) by [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) in 2020 to replace Mohammad Yazdi. He was mentioned as a candidate for Supreme Leader succession but reportedly opposed [Mojtaba Khamenei](/people/mojtaba-khamenei)'s elevation in the Assembly of Experts.

nodes.label: Ahmad Khomeini
nodes.role: Son of Ayatollah Khomeini; political liaison; died 1995 under suspicious circumstances

data.role: Son of Ayatollah Khomeini; political liaison; died 1995 under suspicious circumstances
data.bio: Ahmad Khomeini was the son of [Ruhollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini) and served as the key liaison between the founder of the Islamic Republic and the political establishment. After his father's death in 1989, he remained an influential figure who maintained access to Khomeini's archives and political legacy. On 17 March 1995, Ahmad Khomeini died of an apparent heart attack at the age of 49. The death is widely believed to have been an assassination — reportedly by cyanide poisoning carried out by Saeed Emami — as part of the broader [chain murders](/events/chain-murders-iran-1998) campaign. Ahmad had reportedly been collecting documents that could have been embarrassing to the hardline establishment, and his death conveniently removed a potential rival centre of revolutionary legitimacy.

nodes.label: Ahmad-Reza Radan
nodes.role: Commander-in-Chief, Law Enforcement Command of Iran (2022–present)

data.role: Commander-in-Chief, Law Enforcement Command of Iran (2022–present)
data.bio: Ahmad-Reza Radan is the Commander-in-Chief of the [Law Enforcement Command of Iran](/orgs/law-enforcement-command), appointed in January 2023 by [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei). A veteran security official, Radan previously served as deputy commander of Iran's police force during the [2009 Green Movement protests](/events/green-movement-protests-2009), where he oversaw the violent crackdown on demonstrators. He is under EU and US sanctions for his role in the suppression of protests. During the [January 2026 massacres](/events/january-2026-massacres), police forces under his command used live ammunition against protesters as part of the coordinated security response ordered by the Supreme National Security Council.

nodes.label: Ahmad Tafazzoli
nodes.role: Scholar of ancient Iranian literature; Tehran University professor; assassinated 1997

data.role: Scholar of ancient Iranian literature; Tehran University professor; assassinated 1997
data.bio: Ahmad Tafazzoli was a prominent scholar of ancient Iranian literature and a professor at Tehran University. On 15 January 1997 he was found dead in a suburb of Tehran — his skull and bones had been crushed, and his body was stuffed into the trunk of his car. His murder was later attributed to the same [Ministry of Intelligence](/orgs/ministry-of-intelligence) unit responsible for the broader [chain murders](/events/chain-murders-iran-1998) campaign. Tafazzoli's killing was one of the most brutal of the earlier (pre-1998) chain murders, which only came to light after the high-profile assassinations of late 1998.

nodes.label: Ahmad Tavakkoli
nodes.role: Conservative politician, MP, founder of Resalat newspaper

data.role: Conservative politician, MP, founder of Resalat newspaper
data.bio:
Ahmad Tavakkoli was an Iranian conservative principlist politician. He earned a PhD in economics from the University of Nottingham. He served as Minister of Labour (1981–1983) under Prime Minister [Mir-Hossein Mousavi](/people/mir-hossein-mousavi) and was a member of the [Islamic Consultative Assembly](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly) representing Tehran (2004–2016) and Behshahr (1980–1981).

Tavakkoli founded [Resalat](/orgs/resalat-newspaper), one of Iran's leading conservative newspapers, and managed the Alef news website. He ran for president in 1993 and 2001, finishing second both times. He served on the [Expediency Discernment Council](/orgs/expediency-discernment-council) from 2017 until his death. He was a member of the [Islamic Republican Party](/orgs/islamic-republican-party) and the [Mojahedin of the Islamic Revolution Organization](/orgs/mojahedin-of-islamic-revolution-organization).

He was a cousin of the Larijani brothers, including [Ali Larijani](/people/ali-larijani) and [Sadegh Larijani](/people/sadegh-larijani). He was a fierce critic of [Mahmoud Ahmadinejad](/people/mahmoud-ahmadinejad)'s economic policies. He also founded Justice and Transparency Watch, an anti-corruption NGO.

Tavakkoli died of a heart attack in Tehran on 23 July 2025. He was co-father-in-law with [Sadegh Zibakalam](/people/sadegh-zibakalam).

nodes.label: Ahmad Vahidi
nodes.role: Former Minister of Interior (2021–2024); former Minister of Defense (2009–2013)

data.role: Former Minister of Interior (2021–2024); former Minister of Defense (2009–2013)
data.bio: Ahmad Vahidi is one of the most internationally wanted figures in the Iranian establishment. A founding member of the [IRGC Quds Force](/orgs/irgc-quds-force) and its early commander, he was later appointed Defense Minister under [Mahmoud Ahmadinejad](/people/mahmoud-ahmadinejad) (2009–2013). Under [Ebrahim Raisi](/people/ebrahim-raisi) he became Interior Minister (2021–2024), supervising Iran's domestic security apparatus and election administration. Argentina issued an Interpol red notice against Vahidi for alleged planning of the 1994 AMIA bombing in Buenos Aires that killed 85 people. He is subject to US and EU sanctions. He is a Brigadier General of the IRGC.

nodes.label: Akbar Ganji
nodes.role: Investigative journalist; chain murders exposé author; political dissident

data.role: Investigative journalist; chain murders exposé author; political dissident
data.bio: Akbar Ganji (born 31 January 1960) is described as 'Iran's preeminent political dissident' — a journalist and writer whose investigative work on the [chain murders of 1998](/events/chain-murders-iran-1998) made him a national figure and whose subsequent imprisonment transformed him into an international human rights symbol. He grew up in a poor, religious family in south Tehran; as a young man he joined the IRGC and the Ministry of Culture during the early Islamic Republic's consolidation. By the mid-1990s he had become disillusioned with what he called 'the rise of Fascism.' He wrote for [Kian](/orgs/kian-magazine), [Sobh-e Emroz](/orgs/sobh-e-emroz-newspaper), [Fath](/orgs/fath-newspaper), and [Asr-e Azadegan](/orgs/asr-e-azadegan-newspaper). His chain murders investigation used code names — 'Excellency Red Garmented' for Rafsanjani, 'Master Key' for [Ali Fallahian](/people/ali-fallahian) — later published in the books *Dungeon of Ghosts* (1999) and *The Red Eminence and the Grey Eminences* (2000), the latter described by the Washington Post as 'the Iranian equivalent of the Gulag Archipelago.' Arrested 22 April 2000 at the Berlin Conference, sentenced to 6 years in Evin Prison. He went on multiple hunger strikes lasting over 80 days, sustaining permanent neurological damage. Released 18 March 2006, he went into exile and continues writing from Europe and North America.

nodes.label: Akbar Hashemi Rafsanjani
nodes.role: 4th President of Iran (1989–1997); Chairman of Expediency Discernment Council (1989–2017)

data.role: 4th President of Iran (1989–1997); Chairman of Expediency Discernment Council (1989–2017)
data.bio: Akbar Hashemi Rafsanjani was one of the most powerful figures in post-revolutionary Iran. He served as Speaker of the [Islamic Consultative Assembly](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly) from 1980 to 1989 and as president from 1989 to 1997. A cleric and confidant of [Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini), he is associated with the pragmatist wing of Iranian politics and championed post-war economic reconstruction. He chaired the [Expediency Discernment Council](/orgs/expediency-discernment-council) until his death in January 2017.

nodes.label: Ali Akbar Mohtashamipur
nodes.role: Founder of Hezbollah; Iran's ambassador to Syria (1982–1986); Minister of Interior (1985–1989)

data.role: Founder of Hezbollah; Iran's ambassador to Syria (1982–1986); Minister of Interior (1985–1989)
data.bio: Ali Akbar Mohtashamipur was a Shia cleric who accompanied [Ruhollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini) during his exile in Iraq and France. After the [Iranian Revolution](/events/iranian-revolution-1979), he served as Iran's ambassador to Syria from 1982 to 1986. In that capacity he played a pivotal role in the creation of [Hezbollah](/orgs/hezbollah), working within the framework of the [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) Department for Islamic Liberation Movements. He actively supervised the merger of existing radical Shia factions — including Islamic Amal, the Lebanese branch of the Islamic Dawa Party, and other groups — into the unified Hezbollah organization. He later served as Iran's Minister of Interior from 1985 to 1989. In 1984, while serving as ambassador in Damascus, he survived an Israeli assassination attempt via a parcel bomb that severed his hand. He died on 7 June 2021 from COVID-19.

nodes.label: Ali Akbar Nategh-Nouri
nodes.role: Speaker of Parliament (1992–2000); Head of Supreme Leader's Inspection Office

data.role: Speaker of Parliament (1992–2000); Head of Supreme Leader's Inspection Office
data.bio: Ali Akbar Nategh-Nouri served as Speaker of Parliament for two full terms, 1992 to 2000. He was the establishment-backed frontrunner in the 1997 presidential election before being unexpectedly defeated by reformist [Mohammad Khatami](/people/mohammad-khatami) in a landslide. He subsequently served as head of the Inspection [Office of the Supreme Leader](/orgs/office-of-the-supreme-leader)'s office. He represents the traditional conservative wing of Iranian politics.

nodes.label: Ali Akbar Saidi Sirjani
nodes.role: Writer; poet; journalist; died in MOIS custody 1994

data.role: Writer; poet; journalist; died in MOIS custody 1994
data.bio: Ali Akbar Saidi Sirjani was one of Iran's most distinguished writers, poets, and cultural commentators. Arrested on 14 March 1994 by agents of the [Ministry of Intelligence](/orgs/ministry-of-intelligence), he was announced dead eight months later of an alleged 'heart attack.' The true cause of death, later revealed by journalist Emadeddin Baghi, was a potassium suppository administered in custody. Saidi Sirjani had been a member of the [Iranian Writers' Association](/orgs/iranian-writers-association) and was an outspoken critic of censorship. His killing was one of the earlier victims of the [chain murders](/events/chain-murders-iran-1998) campaign carried out by rogue units within MOIS.

nodes.label: Ali Akbar Salehi
nodes.role: Former Minister of Foreign Affairs (2010–2013); former head of Atomic Energy Organisation (2009–2013, 2015–2021)

data.role: Former Minister of Foreign Affairs (2010–2013); former head of Atomic Energy Organisation (2009–2013, 2015–2021)
data.bio: Ali Akbar Salehi is a nuclear physicist and diplomat who served as [Mahmoud Ahmadinejad](/people/mahmoud-ahmadinejad)'s Foreign Minister (2010–2013) after [Manouchehr Mottaki](/people/manouchehr-mottaki) was abruptly dismissed. He simultaneously headed the Atomic Energy Organisation of Iran (AEOI). He led Iran's nuclear delegation in early negotiations with world powers. He was Iran's ambassador to the International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA) 2006–2009. Under [Hassan Rouhani](/people/hassan-rouhani) he returned as AEOI head (2015–2021), overseeing the nuclear programme through the JCPOA period and its collapse. He is sanctioned by the US.

nodes.label: Ali Akbar Velayati
nodes.role: Foreign Policy Adviser to the Supreme Leader (1997–present); former Foreign Minister (1981–1997)

data.role: Foreign Policy Adviser to the Supreme Leader (1997–present); former Foreign Minister (1981–1997)
data.bio: Ali Akbar Velayati is a physician-turned-diplomat who served as Iran's Foreign Minister for over fifteen years — the longest tenure in the Islamic Republic's history — spanning the prime ministership of [Mir-Hossein Mousavi](/people/mir-hossein-mousavi), the presidency of [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei), and two terms of [Akbar Hashemi Rafsanjani](/people/akbar-hashemi-rafsanjani). Since 1997 he has been [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei)'s personal adviser on international affairs, making him arguably more influential than many sitting foreign ministers. An Argentine court issued an international arrest warrant against him in 2006 for his alleged role in planning the 1994 AMIA bombing in Buenos Aires that killed 85 people, and the US Treasury sanctioned him in 2019. He is a member of the [Expediency Discernment Council](/orgs/expediency-discernment-council) and head of the board of the Islamic Azad University.

nodes.label: Ali Ansari
nodes.role: Founder of Ayandeh Bank and developer of Iran Mall; UK-sanctioned businessman alleged to have financed the IRGC

data.role: Founder of Ayandeh Bank and developer of Iran Mall; UK-sanctioned businessman alleged to have financed the IRGC
data.bio:
Ali Ansari is an Iranian banker and businessman born in December 1962 in Mehrabad, southern Tehran. He served in the [Iran-Iraq War](/events/iran-iraq-war) before entering business, establishing a wholesale iron distribution centre in Tehran's Shadabad district in 1993.

In the 2000s he built a conglomerate spanning Tehran's mobile market, furniture retail, and real estate. He founded Tat Bank around 2009 and orchestrated its merger with Salehin Credit Institution and ATI Credit Institution to form [Ayandeh Bank](/orgs/ayandeh-bank) in 2012. Despite regulatory caps of 25 percent per shareholder, Ansari reportedly controlled approximately 65 percent of Ayandeh Bank's shares through affiliated companies.

His signature project was [Iran Mall](/orgs/iran-mall), the world's largest shopping mall by area, located in northwest Tehran. Approximately 70 percent of Ayandeh Bank's lending — some 97 thousand billion tomans — was directed to Iran Mall and related entities, the bulk of which became non-performing loans.

Ansari is considered close to former president [Mahmoud Ahmadinejad](/people/mahmoud-ahmadinejad), and Ayandeh Bank's licence was widely seen as a political gift to a campaign supporter. Reports have also linked the Iran Mall development to a financial network around [Mojtaba Khamenei](/people/mojtaba-khamenei), son of Supreme Leader [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei), though Ansari's lawyers have denied any relationship with the [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps).

In October 2025, the [Central Bank of Iran](/orgs/central-bank-of-iran) revoked Ayandeh Bank's operating licence after finding $5.1 billion in accumulated losses, $2.9 billion in overdrafts, and a capital adequacy ratio of negative 600 percent. The bank's assets and deposits were absorbed into [Bank Melli Iran](/orgs/bank-melli-iran). One week later, on 30 October 2025, the United Kingdom sanctioned Ansari for financially enabling the IRGC, imposing an asset freeze, travel ban, and director disqualification. Investigations revealed a European property portfolio worth approximately €400 million, including luxury hotels in Mallorca, Kitzbühel, and Frankfurt.

nodes.label: Ali-Asghar Badizadegan
nodes.role: Co-founder of Mojahedin-e Khalq (MEK)

data.role: Co-founder of Mojahedin-e Khalq (MEK)
data.bio: Ali-Asghar Badizadegan was an engineer and one of the three co-founders of the [Mojahedin-e Khalq](/orgs/mojahedin-e-khalq) alongside [Mohammad Hanifnejad](/people/mohammad-hanifnejad) and [Saeed Mohsen](/people/saeed-mohsen). He helped establish the MEK's intellectual foundations, blending progressive Islamic thought with revolutionary theory. He was arrested by SAVAK in 1971 and executed on 25 May 1972.

nodes.label: Ali Fadavi
nodes.role: Former IRGC Deputy Commander-in-Chief (2019–2025); former IRGC Navy Commander (2010–2018)

data.role: Former IRGC Deputy Commander-in-Chief (2019–2025); former IRGC Navy Commander (2010–2018)
data.bio: Ali Fadavi commanded the IRGC Navy from 2010 to 2018, then became Deputy Commander-in-Chief of the entire [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) from 2019. A veteran of the Iran–Iraq War with an intelligence background, he was known for bellicose rhetoric against the US Navy and repeatedly threatened to destroy American vessels in the Persian Gulf. In January 2016 he received the Fath Medal from [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) for arresting ten US Navy sailors in the Persian Gulf. He was replaced as IRGC Navy commander by [Alireza Tangsiri](/people/alireza-tangsiri) in 2018. He was sanctioned by the US, EU, and multiple allied governments.

nodes.label: Ali Fallahian
nodes.role: Former Minister of Intelligence (1989–1997)

data.role: Former Minister of Intelligence (1989–1997)
data.bio: Ali Fallahian served as Iran's Minister of Intelligence under [Akbar Hashemi Rafsanjani](/people/akbar-hashemi-rafsanjani) for the full eight years of his presidency (1989–1997). He is widely believed to have directed Iran's covert assassination programme against dissidents abroad during this period, including the 1992 Mykonos restaurant murders in Berlin — for which a German court in 1997 issued a warrant naming Fallahian — and the 1994 AMIA bombing in Buenos Aires. A second German arrest warrant was issued in 1996. He was replaced when [Mohammad Khatami](/people/mohammad-khatami) took office. He later served in the [Assembly of Experts](/orgs/assembly-of-experts) and has been subject to international sanctions by the EU and US.

nodes.label: Ali Golzadeh Ghafouri
nodes.role: Progressive cleric; member of the Assembly of Experts for the Constitution

data.role: Progressive cleric; member of the Assembly of Experts for the Constitution
data.bio: Ali Golzadeh Ghafouri was a progressive Shia cleric who was elected to the [Assembly of Experts for the Constitution](/orgs/assembly-of-experts-for-constitution) in 1979 as an independent from Tehran Province with over 1.5 million votes. He opposed the doctrine of velayat-e faqih and the concentration of power in the hands of a Supreme Leader. After the consolidation of clerical power, he withdrew from politics in 1981. His family suffered severe repression: two of his sons were executed in 1981, and his daughter and son-in-law were executed during the [1988 mass executions](/events/mass-executions-1988).

nodes.label: Ali Hashemi Bahramani
nodes.role: IRGC officer; Iranian contact in the Iran-Contra affair's 'second channel'

data.role: IRGC officer; Iranian contact in the Iran-Contra affair's 'second channel'
data.bio: Ali Hashemi Bahramani was a nephew of [Akbar Hashemi Rafsanjani](/people/akbar-hashemi-rafsanjani) and an officer in the [Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps). In August 1986, he was established as an alternative Iranian contact in the [Iran-Contra affair](/events/iran-contra-affair-1985) after American frustration with [Manucher Ghorbanifar](/people/manucher-ghorbanifar)'s unreliability. Known to U.S. officials as 'the Relative' or the 'second channel,' Bahramani met frequently with Oliver North in West Germany during the summer and autumn of 1986 to discuss arms sales, hostage releases, and potential U.S.-Iranian cooperation against Iraq during the [Iran-Iraq War](/events/iran-iraq-war). North was reportedly so impressed with him that he arranged a midnight tour of the White House for Bahramani. He was approximately 20 years old at the time of these negotiations. In October 1986, Bahramani informed North that Rafsanjani had decided to involve all Iranian factions in the talks, not just the 'moderate' group — a signal that the deal's premises were unraveling.

nodes.label: Ali Khamenei
nodes.role: 2nd Supreme Leader of the Islamic Republic of Iran (1989–2026)

data.role: 2nd Supreme Leader of the Islamic Republic of Iran (1989–2026)
data.bio: Ali Khamenei served as Iran's third president from 1981 to 1989, then succeeded [Ruhollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini) as Supreme Leader following Khomeini's death. As Supreme Leader for nearly four decades, he exercised ultimate authority over all branches of government, the military, and the judiciary. He oversaw Iran's nuclear programme, the crackdown on the [Green Movement](/events/green-movement-protests-2009) in 2009, and the response to the Mahsa Amini protests in 2022. He died on 28 February 2026.

nodes.label: Ali Larijani
nodes.role: Speaker of Parliament (2008–2020); Former chief nuclear negotiator (d. 2026)

data.role: Speaker of Parliament (2008–2020); Former chief nuclear negotiator (d. 2026)
data.bio: Ali Larijani was the longest-serving speaker in the history of the Islamic Republic, holding the position from 2008 to 2020. Before entering politics, he served as head of [IRIB](/orgs/irib) (1994–2004), appointed by [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) as a hardline counterweight to the reformist press during the Khatami era. He subsequently served as Iran's chief nuclear negotiator (2005–2007) and secretary of the [Supreme National Security Council](/orgs/supreme-national-security-council). He was disqualified from the 2021 presidential election by the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council). His brother [Sadegh Larijani](/people/sadegh-larijani) served as head of the judiciary. He was killed on 17 March 2026 in an [Israeli airstrike on Tehran](/events/larijani-assassination-2026), along with his son Morteza Larijani.

nodes.label: Ali M. Ansari
nodes.role: Professor of Iranian History at University of St Andrews; founding director, Institute for Iranian Studies; President, British Institute of Persian Studies

data.role: Professor of Iranian History at University of St Andrews; founding director, Institute for Iranian Studies; President, British Institute of Persian Studies
data.bio:
Ali Massoud Ansari is a British-Iranian historian and one of the leading academic voices on modern Iran in the English-speaking world. Born in Rome in 1967, he is a member of the extended Pahlavi family — his father, Mohammad Ali Massoud Ansari, is a cousin of [Farah Pahlavi](/people/farah-pahlavi), the last Empress of Iran.

Ansari was educated at University College London (BA), King's College London (MA), and the School of Oriental and African Studies (PhD, 1998), where his doctoral dissertation examined 'Shah Mohammad Reza Pahlavi and the Myth of Imperial Authority.' He is Professor of Modern History with reference to the Middle East at the University of St Andrews, where he founded and directs the [Institute for Iranian Studies](/orgs/institute-for-iranian-studies-st-andrews). He also serves as President of the [British Institute of Persian Studies](/orgs/british-institute-of-persian-studies), Senior Associate Fellow at [RUSI](/orgs/royal-united-services-institute), and Associate Fellow at [Chatham House](/orgs/chatham-house). In 2016 he was elected a Fellow of the Royal Society of Edinburgh.

His extensive bibliography includes *Modern Iran Since 1921: The Pahlavis and After* (2003), *Confronting Iran* (2006), *The Politics of Nationalism in Modern Iran* (2012), *Iran: A Very Short Introduction* (2014), and *Iran, Islam and Democracy* (3rd ed., 2019). He is a regular commentator on Iranian affairs for the BBC, The Guardian, and The Independent.

His brother, [Ahmad Ali Masoud Ansari](/people/ahmad-ali-ansari), was a confidant of the Pahlavi court who later published controversial memoirs alleging fraud and feuds within the royal family, including claims that [Reza Pahlavi](/people/reza-pahlavi) was funded by Saudi Arabia and the CIA.

nodes.label: Ali Meshkini
nodes.role: Chairman of the Assembly of Experts for Leadership (1983–2007)

data.role: Chairman of the Assembly of Experts for Leadership (1983–2007)
data.bio: Ayatollah Ali Meshkini was a senior cleric who served as the first and longest-serving chairman of the [Assembly of Experts](/orgs/assembly-of-experts) for Leadership from its establishment in 1983 until his death in 2007. He was elected to the [Assembly of Experts for the Constitution](/orgs/assembly-of-experts-for-constitution) in 1979, representing East Azerbaijan Province. A loyal supporter of [Ruhollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini) and later [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei), he was based in the Qom seminary and was a member of the [Society of Seminary Teachers of Qom](/orgs/society-of-seminary-teachers-of-qom). His long chairmanship of the Assembly of Experts gave him a pivotal role in the supervision of the Supreme Leader's office, though the assembly rarely exercised its oversight powers during his tenure.

nodes.label: Ali Motahari
nodes.role: Second Deputy Speaker of Parliament, 10th Majlis (2016–2019); MP for Tehran (2008–2020)

data.role: Second Deputy Speaker of Parliament, 10th Majlis (2016–2019); MP for Tehran (2008–2020)
data.bio: Ali Motahari is the son of the martyred Islamic scholar [Morteza Motahhari](/people/morteza-motahhari) and the brother-in-law of former [Majlis](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly) Speaker [Ali Larijani](/people/ali-larijani). He represented Tehran for three consecutive terms (8th–10th Majlis, 2008–2020), serving as Second Deputy Speaker in the 10th Majlis (2016–2019). A principlist by formal affiliation, he was widely regarded as a maverick who publicly challenged [Ahmadinejad](/people/mahmoud-ahmadinejad), criticised [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) political interference, and called for the release of [Green Movement](/events/green-movement-protests-2009) leaders. He was disqualified by the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council) from the 2020 elections. He holds a PhD in philosophy from the University of Tehran.

nodes.label: Ali Nikzad
nodes.role: Second Deputy Speaker, 11th and 12th Majlis (2020–present)

data.role: Second Deputy Speaker, 11th and 12th Majlis (2020–present)
data.bio: Ali Nikzad is a conservative civil engineer and politician from Ardabil Province who has served as Second Deputy Speaker of the [Majlis](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly) under Ghalibaf across two consecutive sessions (2020–present). He previously served as Minister of Roads and Urban Development under [Ahmadinejad](/people/mahmoud-ahmadinejad) (2011–2013) and has been a member of the [Expediency Discernment Council](/orgs/expediency-discernment-council).

nodes.label: Ali Reza Pahlavi
nodes.role: Prince of Iran; second in line of Pahlavi succession until his death

data.role: Prince of Iran; second in line of Pahlavi succession until his death
data.bio:
Ali Reza Pahlavi was the second son of [Mohammad Reza Shah Pahlavi](/people/mohammad-reza-shah-pahlavi) and [Farah Pahlavi](/people/farah-pahlavi), and the younger brother of [Reza Pahlavi](/people/reza-pahlavi). He was second in line to the Pahlavi succession.

After the 1979 Islamic Revolution, the family went into exile. Ali Reza studied at Princeton University and later pursued graduate studies at Harvard and Columbia, focusing on ancient Iranian history and philology.

He died by suicide on 4 January 2011 in Boston, Massachusetts. His death was a significant personal blow to the Pahlavi family and the Iranian exile community.

nodes.label: Ali Shamkhani
nodes.role: Former SNSC Secretary (2013–2023); former Defense Minister (1997–2005)

data.role: Former SNSC Secretary (2013–2023); former Defense Minister (1997–2005)
data.bio: Ali Shamkhani is an ethnic Arab from Khuzestan who rose through the [Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) Navy to become one of Iran's most senior security officials. He commanded the IRGC Navy during the Iran–Iraq War and later served as combined IRGC/Regular Navy commander. [Mohammad Khatami](/people/mohammad-khatami) appointed him Defense Minister in 1997 — a moderate appointment widely seen as signalling detente — and he served for the full eight years. [Hassan Rouhani](/people/hassan-rouhani) appointed him Secretary of the [Supreme National Security Council](/orgs/supreme-national-security-council) in 2013, where he served until 2023. He brokered the 2023 China-mediated Iran–Saudi Arabia normalisation agreement. He was replaced as SNSC Secretary by Ali Akbar Ahmadian in 2023.

nodes.label: Ali Shariati
nodes.role: Sociologist; revolutionary Islamic thinker

data.role: Sociologist; revolutionary Islamic thinker
data.bio: Influential sociologist and Islamic thinker who helped organize the [Freedom Movement of Iran](/orgs/freedom-movement-of-iran) from France. His lectures at the [Hussainiyyeh Ershad](/orgs/hussainiyyeh-ershad) in Tehran in the early 1970s shaped a generation of revolutionary youth. Advocated for a progressive, anti-imperialist Islam. Imprisoned twice under the Pahlavi regime (3.3 years total). Died under suspicious circumstances in Southampton, England on 18 June 1977 — many believe he was killed by [SAVAK](/orgs/savak).

nodes.label: Ali Vaez
nodes.role: Iran Project Director, International Crisis Group

data.role: Iran Project Director, International Crisis Group
data.bio: Ali Vaez is the Iran project director at the [International Crisis Group](/orgs/international-crisis-group) and a protégé of [Robert Malley](/people/robert-malley). He was identified as a member of the [Iran Experts Initiative](/events/iei-email-leak-2023). Leaked emails showed he shared a draft nuclear policy article with [IPIS](/orgs/ipis) head [Mostafa Zahrani](/people/mostafa-zahrani) before publication; the article was subsequently published in the National Interest in June 2014 under a modified title. He attended the inaugural IEI meeting at Vienna's Palais Coburg in May 2014 and advised Malley's Iran negotiating team.

nodes.label: Ali Younessi
nodes.role: Former Minister of Intelligence (1999–2005)

data.role: Former Minister of Intelligence (1999–2005)
data.bio: Ali Younessi served as Iran's Minister of Intelligence for most of [Mohammad Khatami](/people/mohammad-khatami)'s presidency (1999–2005), replacing [Qorbanali Dorri-Najafabadi](/people/qorbanali-dorri-najafabadi) who resigned over the 1998 chain murders of dissidents. A cleric and jurist, Younessi was seen as a reformist appointment and took steps to investigate intelligence operations that had targeted journalists and intellectuals. He was later appointed as [Hassan Rouhani](/people/hassan-rouhani)'s special assistant for ethnic minorities and religious affairs (2014–2021). In 2020 he was arrested on charges of espionage but released.

nodes.label: Alireza Arafi
nodes.role: Guardian Council member (2019–present); member of post-Khamenei interim leadership council

data.role: Guardian Council member (2019–present); member of post-Khamenei interim leadership council
data.bio: Alireza Arafi is a senior cleric and [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council) member appointed by Khamenei in 2019. He has served as president of the Islamic Seminaries of Iran (Hawza). Following Khamenei's death in February 2026, he was appointed as the Guardian Council's representative on the three-member interim leadership council, alongside [President Pezeshkian](/people/masoud-pezeshkian) and Chief Justice Mohseni-Ejei, pursuant to Article 111 of the constitution. He has been mentioned as a potential future Supreme Leader.

nodes.label: Alireza Tangsiri
nodes.role: Former Commander, IRGC Navy (2018–2026); killed in Israeli airstrike March 2026

data.role: Former Commander, IRGC Navy (2018–2026); killed in Israeli airstrike March 2026
data.bio: Alireza Tangsiri commanded the IRGC Navy from August 2018, personally appointed by [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) to replace [Ali Fadavi](/people/ali-fadavi). Born in a Persian Gulf fishing community, he built the IRGC Navy's 'swarm tactics' doctrine — coordinated attacks by hundreds of fast-attack craft and missiles designed to overwhelm US carrier groups. He was the public face of Iran's de facto blockade of the Strait of Hormuz during the 2026 Iran war. He was killed on 26 March 2026 in an Israeli airstrike at the port of Bandar Abbas, alongside other senior IRGC Navy commanders. The US Treasury had designated him a Specially Designated Global Terrorist in 2019.

nodes.label: Amin Hossein Rahimi
nodes.role: Minister of Justice (2021–present)

data.role: Minister of Justice (2021–present)
data.bio: Amin Hossein Rahimi has served as Iran's Minister of Justice since [Ebrahim Raisi](/people/ebrahim-raisi)'s cabinet in 2021, retained by [Masoud Pezeshkian](/people/masoud-pezeshkian) in 2024. He is a career jurist who began working in Iran's judiciary at age 18. He served as Chief of the General and Revolutionary Courts in Tehran and as Political and Security Deputy of the Prosecutor General before his ministerial appointment. He is regarded as a loyalist of [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) and a product of the hardliner judicial network associated with [Ebrahim Raisi](/people/ebrahim-raisi).

nodes.label: Amir Ali Hajizadeh
nodes.role: Former Commander, IRGC Aerospace Force (2009–2025); killed in Israeli airstrike June 2025

data.role: Former Commander, IRGC Aerospace Force (2009–2025); killed in Israeli airstrike June 2025
data.bio: Amir Ali Hajizadeh commanded the [IRGC Aerospace Force](/orgs/irgc-aerospace-force) from 2009 until his death in an Israeli airstrike on 13 June 2025, as part of the large-scale Israeli operation that also killed [Hossein Salami](/people/hossein-salami) and other senior commanders. He joined the [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) in 1980 as a sniper during the Iran–Iraq War and was mentored by Hassan Tehrani Moghaddam — the 'Father of Iran's Missile Program' — who brought him into the nascent missile programme. He organised Iran's first missile unit 'Hadid' and rose to command Iran's entire ballistic missile and UAV arsenal. He directed the January 2020 missile strikes against US bases in Iraq following [Qasem Soleimani](/people/qasem-soleimani)'s assassination, and oversaw Iran's April and October 2024 direct missile and drone strikes against Israel. He was in command when IRGC missiles shot down Ukrainian flight PS752 on 8 January 2020, killing all 176 aboard — he admitted 'full responsibility' three days later. [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) awarded him the Order of Fath in October 2024 for the strikes on Israel.

nodes.label: Amir-Hossein Ghazizadeh Hashemi
nodes.role: First Deputy Speaker, 11th Majlis (2020–2024); 2021 presidential candidate

data.role: First Deputy Speaker, 11th Majlis (2020–2024); 2021 presidential candidate
data.bio: Amir-Hossein Ghazizadeh Hashemi is a hardline politician from Mashhad who served as First Deputy Speaker of the 11th [Majlis](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly) under Ghalibaf (2020–2024). He was one of only seven candidates approved by the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council) from 585 applicants in the 2021 presidential election, finishing fourth. A physician by training, he represents the [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps)-aligned principlist networks that swept the 11th Majlis.

nodes.label: Amir-Hossein Sabeti
nodes.role: MP for Tehran, 12th Majlis (2024–present); former state television host

data.role: MP for Tehran, 12th Majlis (2024–present); former state television host
data.bio: Amir-Hossein Sabeti is a young conservative activist and state television anchor who placed second in the Tehran ballot in the 2024 parliamentary election, making him one of the most prominent new faces in the 12th [Majlis](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly). Born in 1989, he represents the post-war generation of [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps)-aligned conservatives who entered politics via media rather than clerical routes. He is a member of the [Front of Islamic Revolution Stability](/orgs/front-of-islamic-revolution-stability). In 2012 he received a suspended sentence from a Revolutionary Court for protesting [Ali Larijani](/people/ali-larijani)'s parliamentary policies on universities.

nodes.label: Ariane Tabatabai
nodes.role: Pentagon Chief of Staff for Asst. Secretary of Defense for Special Operations

data.role: Pentagon Chief of Staff for Asst. Secretary of Defense for Special Operations
data.bio: Ariane Tabatabai is a German-Iranian political scientist who served as chief of staff to the Assistant Secretary of Defense for Special Operations and Low-Intensity Conflict at the Pentagon. She was recruited in 2014 by [Saeed Khatibzadeh](/people/saeed-khatibzadeh) as a founding member of the [Iran Experts Initiative](/events/iei-email-leak-2023), a covert [Iranian Foreign Ministry](/orgs/ministry-of-foreign-affairs) influence operation coordinated through [IPIS](/orgs/ipis). Leaked emails showed she consulted IPIS head [Mostafa Zahrani](/people/mostafa-zahrani) about conference attendance — including an Israeli conference invitation — and informed him of opportunities to testify before Congress. In February 2021, [Robert Malley](/people/robert-malley) hired her to join his Iran team at the State Department, and she subsequently moved to the Pentagon. After the [IEI leak](/events/iei-email-leak-2023) in September 2023, the Pentagon launched a security review of her clearance; she was ultimately retained and her top-secret SCI clearance maintained.

nodes.label: Ashraf Rabiei
nodes.role: Early MEK leader; first wife of Massoud Rajavi

data.role: Early MEK leader; first wife of Massoud Rajavi
data.bio: Ashraf Rabiei studied physics at Aryamehr University (now Sharif University) and joined the [Mojahedin-e Khalq](/orgs/mojahedin-e-khalq) in the early 1970s. She married [Massoud Rajavi](/people/massoud-rajavi) in 1980. In the 1980 parliamentary election, she received over 300,000 votes but did not win a seat. She was killed alongside [Moussa Khiabani](/people/moussa-khiabani) on 8 February 1982 when the [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) raided their safehouse in Tehran. Camp Ashraf in Iraq was named in her honor.

nodes.label: Ataollah Mohajerani
nodes.role: Former Minister of Culture and Islamic Guidance (1997–2000); writer; reformist politician

data.role: Former Minister of Culture and Islamic Guidance (1997–2000); writer; reformist politician
data.bio: Ataollah Mohajerani served as [Mohammad Khatami](/people/mohammad-khatami)'s Minister of Culture and Islamic Guidance from 1997 to 2000, presiding over the most open period for the Iranian press since the revolution. Under his stewardship, hundreds of new newspapers and magazines were licensed, including the reformist dailies that employed journalists like [Akbar Ganji](/people/akbar-ganji) and [Mashallah Shamsolvaezin](/people/mashallah-shamsolvaezin). He survived a parliamentary impeachment vote in 1999 with Khatami's backing but resigned in 2000 under mounting pressure from hardliners. He co-authored the 2010 [Green Movement](/events/green-movement-protests-2009) manifesto with [Abdolkarim Soroush](/people/abdolkarim-soroush), [Mohsen Kadivar](/people/mohsen-kadivar), and [Akbar Ganji](/people/akbar-ganji). He has lived in exile in London since 2009.

nodes.label: Aziz Nasirzadeh
nodes.role: Minister of Defense (2024–present)

data.role: Minister of Defense (2024–present)
data.bio: Aziz Nasirzadeh is Iran's Minister of Defense under [Masoud Pezeshkian](/people/masoud-pezeshkian), appointed in August 2024. A senior air force commander, he previously served as commander of the Iranian Air Force and as deputy chief of staff of the armed forces. His appointment to the defense portfolio followed the tenure of [Mohammad Reza Ashtiani](/people/mohammad-reza-ashtiani) under [Ebrahim Raisi](/people/ebrahim-raisi).

nodes.label: Babak Zanjani
nodes.role: Convicted sanctions-evasion tycoon; founder of Sorinet Group; orchestrated billions in illicit Iranian oil sales

data.role: Convicted sanctions-evasion tycoon; founder of Sorinet Group; orchestrated billions in illicit Iranian oil sales
data.bio:
Babak Zanjani is an Iranian billionaire businessman convicted of large-scale embezzlement and sanctions evasion. Between 2010 and 2013, operating through his UAE-based Sorinet Group and a network of over 60 shell companies, he sold millions of barrels of Iranian crude oil on behalf of the [Ministry of Petroleum](/orgs/ministry-of-petroleum) in violation of international sanctions, generating an estimated $17.5 billion in revenue for the Iranian government.

His operations were authorised by senior officials during [Mahmoud Ahmadinejad](/people/mahmoud-ahmadinejad)'s second presidential term, including Oil Minister [Rostam Ghasemi](/people/rostam-ghasemi), formerly the head of [Khatam al-Anbiya](/orgs/khatam-al-anbiya). Zanjani was accused of withholding $2.7 billion owed to the state.

The European Union sanctioned Zanjani in December 2012 as a key facilitator of Iranian oil deals, and the United States designated him in April 2013 under Executive Order 13382. He was arrested on 30 December 2013 and sentenced to death on 6 March 2016 for 'spreading corruption on earth.' The Supreme Court confirmed the sentence in December 2016.

In April 2024, his death sentence was commuted to 20 years' imprisonment after he returned approximately $2.1 billion in assets, with clemency approved by [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei). By early 2025, Zanjani was conditionally released and resumed business activity through the newly registered Avan Group (DotOne Group), which quickly expanded into rail transport, crypto trading, and other sectors. In 2025, the United States and United Kingdom imposed fresh sanctions on Zanjani and his crypto exchanges for continued [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) financing.

nodes.label: Behzad Nabavi
nodes.role: First Deputy Speaker of Parliament, 6th Majlis (alternating 2000–2004); Co-founder, OMIIR

data.role: First Deputy Speaker of Parliament, 6th Majlis (alternating 2000–2004); Co-founder, OMIIR
data.bio: Behzad Nabavi is a senior figure of the Islamic left turned democratic reformist. An electrical engineer, he fought against the Shah and was imprisoned before the revolution. He co-founded the Islamic Revolution Committees (komiteh) and a forerunner to the Ministry of Intelligence. Under PM Mousavi he served as minister for executive affairs and minister of heavy industry. He was also Iran's chief negotiator in the 1981 [Algiers Accords](/events/algiers-accords-1981) that ended the hostage crisis. In the 6th [Majlis](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly) he alternated as First Deputy Speaker with [Mohammad-Reza Khatami](/people/mohammad-reza-khatami). He was arrested after the 2009 elections and sentenced to six years.

nodes.label: Bijan Namdar Zanganeh
nodes.role: Former Minister of Petroleum (1997–2005, 2013–2021); longest-serving Oil Minister in the Islamic Republic

data.role: Former Minister of Petroleum (1997–2005, 2013–2021); longest-serving Oil Minister in the Islamic Republic
data.bio: Bijan Namdar Zanganeh served as Iran's Minister of Petroleum under both [Mohammad Khatami](/people/mohammad-khatami) (1997–2005) and [Hassan Rouhani](/people/hassan-rouhani) (2013–2021) — twelve years in total, the longest oil ministerial tenure in the Islamic Republic's history. He is closely associated with the technocratic and pragmatist camp, and with Iran's efforts to attract foreign investment and maintain OPEC standing. His nomination by Rouhani was criticised by hardline newspapers. He is regarded as one of the most capable technocrats in Iranian economic history, having overseen Iran's oil expansion during the Khatami years and the post-JCPOA production recovery. He was replaced by [Javad Owji](/people/javad-owji) under [Ebrahim Raisi](/people/ebrahim-raisi).

nodes.label: Dariush Forouhar
nodes.role: Secretary-general of Nation of Iran Party; former Minister of Labour; assassinated 1998

data.role: Secretary-general of Nation of Iran Party; former Minister of Labour; assassinated 1998
data.bio: Dariush Forouhar was the leader of the [Nation of Iran Party](/orgs/nation-of-iran-party) (Hezb-e Mellat-e Iran), one of the oldest nationalist-democratic parties in Iran. A veteran of the 1953 oil nationalisation movement, he briefly served as Minister of Labour in the provisional government after the 1979 revolution. He became one of the most outspoken critics of the Islamic Republic, openly calling for free elections and the rule of law from his Tehran home, which served as an informal political salon. On the night of 22 November 1998, agents of the [Ministry of Intelligence](/orgs/ministry-of-intelligence) entered his home and stabbed him 26 times. His wife [Parvaneh Forouhar](/people/parvaneh-forouhar) was stabbed 25 times the same night. Their murders, along with the killings of Mohammad Mokhtari and Mohammad Jafar Pouyandeh weeks later, became the catalyst for the public exposure of the [chain murders](/events/chain-murders-iran-1998). The home had been under 24-hour MOIS surveillance. Lawyer [Shirin Ebadi](/people/shirin-ebadi) represented the Forouhar family in the subsequent proceedings. Forouhar's killers were sentenced to death but the sentences were commuted at the family's request.

nodes.label: Dina Esfandiary
nodes.role: Senior Advisor, International Crisis Group

data.role: Senior Advisor, International Crisis Group
data.bio: Dina Esfandiary is a political analyst who served as a senior advisor at the [International Crisis Group](/orgs/international-crisis-group). In March 2014, she was recruited alongside [Ariane Tabatabai](/people/ariane-tabatabai) by [Saeed Khatibzadeh](/people/saeed-khatibzadeh) at a meeting in Prague to form the core group of the [Iran Experts Initiative](/events/iei-email-leak-2023), a covert [Iranian Foreign Ministry](/orgs/ministry-of-foreign-affairs) influence operation. Khatibzadeh reported to [IPIS](/orgs/ipis) head [Mostafa Zahrani](/people/mostafa-zahrani) that 'we three agreed to be the core group of the IEI.' She attended the inaugural IEI meeting at Vienna's Palais Coburg hotel in May 2014. She was hired by ICG during [Robert Malley](/people/robert-malley)'s presidency and advised his Iran negotiating efforts.

nodes.label: Ebrahim Raisi
nodes.role: 8th President of Iran (2021–2024)

data.role: 8th President of Iran (2021–2024)
data.bio: Ebrahim Raisi was a hardline cleric who served as head of the judiciary from 2019 before being elected president in 2021 in an election with record-low turnout. He is alleged to have been a member of the 'Death Commission' that ordered the mass execution of political prisoners in 1988. He was subject to US Treasury sanctions since 2019. His presidency was dominated by the Mahsa Amini protests of 2022. He died on 19 May 2024 when his helicopter crashed in foggy conditions near the Azerbaijan border.

nodes.label: Ebrahim Yazdi
nodes.role: Secretary-general of Freedom Movement of Iran (1995–2017); former Foreign Minister

data.role: Secretary-general of Freedom Movement of Iran (1995–2017); former Foreign Minister
data.bio: Pharmacologist and political activist. Organized [Freedom Movement of Iran](/orgs/freedom-movement-of-iran) activities in the United States during exile. Served as foreign minister in [Bazargan](/people/mehdi-bazargan)'s provisional government (1979). Second secretary-general of the Freedom Movement (1995–2017). Repeatedly arrested and imprisoned by the Islamic Republic. Participated in the [1979 revolution](/events/iranian-revolution-1979). Died 27 August 2017.

nodes.label: Ebrahim Zalzadeh
nodes.role: Editor; publisher; owner of Ebtekar publishing house; assassinated 1997

data.role: Editor; publisher; owner of Ebtekar publishing house; assassinated 1997
data.bio: Ebrahim Zalzadeh was the editor of the literary magazine *Me'yar* and owner of Ebtekar publishing house. On 24 February 1997, he was arrested by agents of the [Ministry of Intelligence](/orgs/ministry-of-intelligence). His body was found half-buried outside Tehran on 29 March 1997. His murder was part of the [chain murders](/events/chain-murders-iran-1998) campaign targeting Iranian intellectuals and publishers.

nodes.label: Ellie Geranmayeh
nodes.role: Senior Policy Fellow, European Council on Foreign Relations

data.role: Senior Policy Fellow, European Council on Foreign Relations
data.bio: Ellie Geranmayeh is a senior policy fellow and deputy head of the Middle East and North Africa programme at the [European Council on Foreign Relations (ECFR)](/orgs/european-council-on-foreign-relations). She was identified as a member of the [Iran Experts Initiative](/events/iei-email-leak-2023), attending the inaugural meeting at Vienna's Palais Coburg in May 2014 alongside other Western-based Iranian academics recruited by the Iranian [Foreign Ministry](/orgs/ministry-of-foreign-affairs) through [IPIS](/orgs/ipis) to promote Tehran's nuclear negotiating positions.

nodes.label: Emadeddin Baghi
nodes.role: Investigative journalist; human rights activist; writer for Neshat and Fath on chain murders

data.role: Investigative journalist; human rights activist; writer for Neshat and Fath on chain murders
data.bio: Emadeddin Baghi is an investigative journalist and human rights defender who co-authored, alongside [Akbar Ganji](/people/akbar-ganji), the landmark chain murders investigations in [Sobh-e Emroz](/orgs/sobh-e-emroz-newspaper), [Neshat](/orgs/neshat-newspaper), and [Fath](/orgs/fath-newspaper). He was a key source alongside [Saeed Hajjarian](/people/saeed-hajjarian). His September 1999 article in Neshat criticising capital punishment was the stated pretext for that paper's closure and for Shamsolvaezin's prosecution. Baghi himself was arrested on 29 May 2000 and sentenced to five and a half years in prison for 'undermining national security,' later reduced on appeal to three years. He went on to found the Society for the Defense of Prisoners' Rights in Iran, becoming a leading human rights NGO figure.

nodes.label: Eskandar Momeni
nodes.role: Minister of Interior (2024–present)

data.role: Minister of Interior (2024–present)
data.bio: Eskandar Momeni is Iran's Minister of Interior under [Masoud Pezeshkian](/people/masoud-pezeshkian), appointed in August 2024. A senior security official with a military background, he previously served in senior positions in law enforcement and security agencies. He superseded [Ahmad Vahidi](/people/ahmad-vahidi)'s hardline tenure at the Interior Ministry.

nodes.label: Esmaeil Khatib
nodes.role: Minister of Intelligence (2021–present)

data.role: Minister of Intelligence (2021–present)
data.bio: Esmaeil Khatib is Iran's Minister of Intelligence, appointed by [Ebrahim Raisi](/people/ebrahim-raisi) in 2021 and retained by [Masoud Pezeshkian](/people/masoud-pezeshkian) in 2024. He joined the [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) intelligence wing in 1980 and built a career spanning the Ministry of Intelligence, the [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) intelligence apparatus, and the [Office of the Supreme Leader](/orgs/office-of-the-supreme-leader). Under [Ebrahim Raisi](/people/ebrahim-raisi) as Judiciary chief, Khatib headed the Judiciary's Protection and Intelligence Centre. He studied jurisprudence under [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) himself, giving him direct personal ties to the Supreme Leader network. The Intelligence Ministry under Khatib has overseen intensified suppression of protest movements including the [2022 Mahsa Amini protests](/events/mahsa-amini-protests-2022).

nodes.label: Esmail Qaani
nodes.role: Commander of the IRGC Quds Force (2020–2025)

data.role: Commander of the IRGC Quds Force (2020–2025)
data.bio: Esmail Qaani succeeded [Qasem Soleimani](/people/qasem-soleimani) as [Quds Force](/orgs/irgc-quds-force) commander on the day of Soleimani's assassination, appointed immediately by Khamenei. A veteran of the [Iran-Iraq War](/events/iran-iraq-war) and longtime Quds Force deputy, his tenure was marked by criticism that he lacked Soleimani's strategic flair and public profile. He oversaw Quds Force operations during the Gaza conflict from 2023 and the broader escalation with Israel. Iranian sources reported he was killed in Israeli strikes on Iran in June 2025.

nodes.label: Ezzatollah Sahabi
nodes.role: Freedom Movement activist; journalist

data.role: Freedom Movement activist; journalist
data.bio: Son of [Yadollah Sahabi](/people/yadollah-sahabi). Co-founder of the [Freedom Movement of Iran](/orgs/freedom-movement-of-iran). He was elected to the [Assembly of Experts for the Constitution](/orgs/assembly-of-experts-for-constitution) in 1979, representing Tehran Province, where he opposed the concentration of power in the velayat-e faqih provisions. Imprisoned 3 times under the Pahlavi regime (10.8 years total) and twice under the Islamic Republic (1.3 years). Active in nationalist-religious opposition politics. Co-founded the [Council of Nationalist-Religious Activists](/orgs/council-of-nationalist-religious-activists) in 2000.

nodes.label: Ezzatollah Zarghami
nodes.role: Head of IRIB (2004–2014); Minister of Cultural Heritage (2021–2024)

data.role: Head of IRIB (2004–2014); Minister of Cultural Heritage (2021–2024)
data.bio: Ezzatollah Zarghami is a former [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) member who served as head of [IRIB](/orgs/irib) for a decade, appointed by Supreme Leader [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) in 2004 after [Ali Larijani](/people/ali-larijani)'s departure.
