His tenure covered the controversial [2009 presidential election](/events/presidential-election-iran-2009) and the [Green Movement](/events/green-movement-protests-2009), during which IRIB declared [Mahmoud Ahmadinejad](/people/mahmoud-ahmadinejad) the winner before official certification and broadcast no honest coverage of the massive street protests. Under Zarghami, IRIB expanded its international satellite channels including [Press TV](/orgs/press-tv) and aired forced confessions of detained reformist activists and journalists. He was personally sanctioned by the European Union in 2012 for IRIB's role in broadcasting coerced confessions. After leaving IRIB, he was appointed Minister of Cultural Heritage and Tourism by President [Ebrahim Raisi](/people/ebrahim-raisi) in 2021.

nodes.label: Farah Pahlavi
nodes.role: Last Empress (Shahbanou) of Iran (1959–1979); exiled opposition figurehead

data.role: Last Empress (Shahbanou) of Iran (1959–1979); exiled opposition figurehead
data.bio:
Farah Pahlavi (née Diba) is the widow of Mohammad Reza Shah Pahlavi and the last Empress of Iran. Born into a prominent family — her father Captain Sohrab Diba was an army officer, her maternal family the Ghotbis were of Gilak origin — she studied architecture at the École Spéciale d'Architecture in Paris before marrying the Shah in 1959.

As Shahbanou she was a patron of the arts, culture, and education, overseeing numerous museums and cultural institutions. After the 1979 Islamic Revolution, she accompanied the Shah into exile, living in Egypt, Morocco, the Bahamas, Mexico, and Panama before settling in the United States after the Shah's death in Cairo in 1980.

She divides her time between Washington, D.C. and Paris, remaining active in exile politics. During the 2022–2023 Woman, Life, Freedom protests, she publicly supported the movement. Her son [Reza Pahlavi](/people/reza-pahlavi) is a prominent opposition figure who advocates for a secular democratic Iran. Her cousin, Mohammad Ali Massoud Ansari, was the father of historian [Ali M. Ansari](/people/ali-m-ansari).

nodes.label: Fatemeh Hashemi Rafsanjani
nodes.role: Women's rights activist, head of Charity Foundation for Special Diseases

data.role: Women's rights activist, head of Charity Foundation for Special Diseases
data.bio:
Fatemeh Hashemi Rafsanjani is an Iranian women's rights activist and the daughter of former President [Akbar Hashemi Rafsanjani](/people/akbar-hashemi-rafsanjani). She heads the Charity Foundation for Special Diseases and serves as secretary-general of the Women's Solidarity Association of Iran.

She is a senior member of the [Moderation and Development Party](/orgs/moderation-and-development-party). In 1995, she led Iran's delegation to the Organisation of Islamic Countries' symposium on women's role in Islamic society.

She studied under [Sadegh Zibakalam](/people/sadegh-zibakalam) at the University of Tehran.

nodes.label: Gholam-Ali Haddad-Adel
nodes.role: Speaker of Parliament (2004–2008); Head of Iranian Academy of Persian Language and Literature

data.role: Speaker of Parliament (2004–2008); Head of Iranian Academy of Persian Language and Literature
data.bio: Gholam-Ali Haddad-Adel is a philosopher and politician who served as Speaker of Parliament from 2004 to 2008, the first non-cleric to hold the role since the 1979 revolution. He holds a PhD in philosophy from the University of Tehran and has led the Iranian [Academy of Persian Language and Literature](/orgs/iranian-academy-of-persian-language) since 1995. His daughter Zahra married [Mojtaba Khamenei](/people/mojtaba-khamenei), son of the Supreme Leader, making Haddad-Adel one of the most personally connected figures to the supreme leadership. He is a senior member of the principlist [Alliance of Builders of Islamic Iran](/orgs/alliance-of-builders-of-islamic-iran).

nodes.label: Gholam-Hossein Mohseni-Ejei
nodes.role: Chief Justice of Iran (2021–present); member of post-Khamenei interim leadership council

data.role: Chief Justice of Iran (2021–present); member of post-Khamenei interim leadership council
data.bio: Gholam-Hossein Mohseni-Ejei is a hardline jurist appointed Chief Justice by Khamenei in July 2021. He previously served as Minister of Intelligence under [Ahmadinejad](/people/mahmoud-ahmadinejad) (2005–2009), as prosecutor-general, and as first deputy chief justice. He is regarded as a reliable enforcer of the regime's security priorities. Following Khamenei's death in February 2026, he became one of three members of the constitutional interim leadership council alongside [President Pezeshkian](/people/masoud-pezeshkian) and [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council) member Arafi.

nodes.label: Gholamhossein Karbaschi
nodes.role: Mayor of Tehran 1989–1998; founder of Hamshahri and Ham-Mihan; reformist politician

data.role: Mayor of Tehran 1989–1998; founder of Hamshahri and Ham-Mihan; reformist politician
data.bio: Gholamhossein Karbaschi served as Mayor of Tehran from 1989 to 1998, transforming the city's infrastructure and cultural life — and the media landscape. He founded [Hamshahri](/orgs/hamshahri-newspaper) in 1992 as the first colour daily in Iran, which grew to 400,000 circulation within three years and became the reformists' most powerful media platform. A close Khatami ally, he managed the reformist coalition's communications strategy. In 1998–1999 he was prosecuted on graft charges widely regarded as politically motivated — part of the hardliners' strategy of destroying the reformist political machine institution by institution. He was sentenced to 18 months in prison (later suspended) and five years' ban from public office. He subsequently ran the newspaper [Ham-Mihan](/orgs/ham-mihan-newspaper). He was also the founder of the Executives of Construction Party (Hezb-e Kargozaran-e Sazandegi), one of the main reformist parties.

nodes.label: Gholamreza Molabeygi
nodes.role: Jurist member of the Guardian Council

data.role: Jurist member of the Guardian Council
data.bio: Gholamreza Molabeygi is a jurist member of the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council), nominated by the Chief Justice and confirmed by the [Islamic Consultative Assembly](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly). He is one of six constitutional law experts on the Council.

nodes.label: Gholamreza Soleimani
nodes.role: Commander of the Basij Resistance Force (d. 2026)

data.role: Commander of the Basij Resistance Force (d. 2026)
data.bio: Gholamreza Soleimani served as commander of the [Basij Resistance Force](/orgs/basij), the paramilitary volunteer militia subordinate to the [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps). Under his command, Basij forces played a central role in the violent suppression of the [2025-2026 protests](/events/january-2026-massacres), using live ammunition and other lethal force against civilian demonstrators. He was killed on 17 March 2026 in an Israeli airstrike during the [assassination of Ali Larijani](/events/larijani-assassination-2026), along with other senior officials.

nodes.label: Hadi Tahan Nazif
nodes.role: Spokesman of the Guardian Council; jurist member

data.role: Spokesman of the Guardian Council; jurist member
data.bio: Hadi Tahan Nazif is the current spokesman of the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council) and a jurist member. He serves as the Council's primary public communicator, issuing formal statements on candidate vetting decisions and electoral results. He confirmed [Siamak Rahpeyk](/people/siamak-rahpeyk)'s reappointment as deputy secretary in July 2023.

nodes.label: Hamed Esmaeilion
nodes.role: Diaspora activist; author; co-founder of Association of Families of Flight PS752 Victims

data.role: Diaspora activist; author; co-founder of Association of Families of Flight PS752 Victims
data.bio:
Hamed Esmaeilion (born 12 March 1977) is an Iranian-Canadian dentist, author, and activist who became one of the most prominent voices of the Iranian diaspora opposition after losing his wife Parisa Eghbalian and nine-year-old daughter Reera in the [Ukraine International Airlines Flight 752 shootdown](/events/ukraine-airlines-flight-752-shootdown-2020) on 8 January 2020. The [IRGC Aerospace Force](/orgs/irgc-aerospace-force) shot down the passenger aircraft with two surface-to-air missiles shortly after takeoff from Tehran, killing all 176 people aboard.

Esmaeilion co-founded the [Association of Families of Flight PS752 Victims](/orgs/association-of-families-of-flight-ps752-victims) and served as its president and spokesperson, leading legal and advocacy campaigns for accountability. He resigned from the association on 7 March 2023 to focus on broader opposition work.

During the [Mahsa Amini protests](/events/mahsa-amini-protests-2022), Esmaeilion emerged as a key organiser of diaspora solidarity rallies, coordinating demonstrations on 1 October 2022 in over 150 cities worldwide and a major Berlin rally on 22 October 2022 that drew an estimated 80,000–100,000 participants. He joined a loose coalition with [Reza Pahlavi](/people/reza-pahlavi) and [Masih Alinejad](/people/masih-alinejad) to amplify the Woman, Life, Freedom movement internationally.

Before his activism, Esmaeilion was a published novelist. His works include *Thyme is not Fair* (2008) and *Dr. Datis* (2013), both recognised by the Hooshang Golshiri Literary Awards. He emigrated from Iran to Canada in 2010 and practises dentistry in Richmond Hill, Ontario.

nodes.label: Hamid Hajizadeh
nodes.role: Poet; scholar; high school teacher; assassinated with his son 1998

data.role: Poet; scholar; high school teacher; assassinated with his son 1998
data.bio: Hamid Hajizadeh was a poet, scholar, and high school teacher in Kerman. On the night of 22 September 1998, [Ministry of Intelligence](/orgs/ministry-of-intelligence) agents entered his home and stabbed him 38 times in his bed. His nine-year-old son Karoon (Karun) was sleeping in his father's arms and was also stabbed to death. The murder of Hajizadeh and his child — one of the most horrifying acts of the [chain murders](/events/chain-murders-iran-1998) — occurred two months before the more widely publicised assassinations of the Forouhars in November 1998.

nodes.label: Hamid Rasaee
nodes.role: MP for Tehran, 12th Majlis (2024–present); publisher, Noh Dey Weekly

data.role: MP for Tehran, 12th Majlis (2024–present); publisher, Noh Dey Weekly
data.bio: Hamid Rasaee is a hardline cleric representing Tehran in the [Islamic Consultative Assembly](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly) (12th Majlis). He is a senior figure of the [Front of Islamic Revolution Stability](/orgs/front-of-islamic-revolution-stability) and publisher of the *Noh Dey Weekly*, shut down multiple times during the [Hassan Rouhani](/people/hassan-rouhani) presidency. He publicly clashed in a famous 2015 parliamentary confrontation with an aide of [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) over the [JCPOA](/events/jcpoa-nuclear-deal-2015). He was previously disqualified by the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council) from one election cycle. He served in the 8th and 9th Majlis before returning in the 12th.

nodes.label: Hamid-Reza Haji Babaee
nodes.role: First Deputy Speaker, 12th Majlis (2024–present)

data.role: First Deputy Speaker, 12th Majlis (2024–present)
data.bio: Hamid-Reza Haji Babaee is a conservative politician from Hamadan Province serving as First Deputy Speaker of the 12th [Majlis](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly) under [Speaker Ghalibaf](/people/mohammad-bagher-ghalibaf). He previously served as Minister of Education under [Ahmadinejad](/people/mahmoud-ahmadinejad) (2009–2013). A long-serving principlist MP, he represents the established traditional conservative wing of the Majlis.

nodes.label: Hamid Reza Jalalipour
nodes.role: Co-director, Jame'eh, Tous, Neshat; reformist political analyst

data.role: Co-director, Jame'eh, Tous, Neshat; reformist political analyst
data.bio: Hamid Reza Jalalipour was the co-director and publishing head of the company behind [Jame'eh](/orgs/jameeh-newspaper), [Tous](/orgs/tous-newspaper), and [Neshat](/orgs/neshat-newspaper), working alongside [Mashallah Shamsolvaezin](/people/mashallah-shamsolvaezin). He and Shamsolvaezin were jailed together without charges for 35 days after Jame'eh's closure in 1998. Jalalipour is also a sociologist and political analyst associated with Tehran University, one of the academic-reformist intellectuals who staffed the Khatami-era press.

nodes.label: Hassan Ayat
nodes.role: Secretary of the Assembly of Experts for the Constitution; IRP central committee member

data.role: Secretary of the Assembly of Experts for the Constitution; IRP central committee member
data.bio: Hassan Ayat was a lawyer and political activist who served as secretary of the [Assembly of Experts for the Constitution](/orgs/assembly-of-experts-for-constitution) in 1979. A member of the [Islamic Republican Party](/orgs/islamic-republican-party) central committee, he played a key role in inserting the doctrine of velayat-e faqih into the constitution, arguing forcefully for broad powers for the Supreme Leader. He was elected to the first [Islamic Consultative Assembly](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly) (Majles) in 1980. He was assassinated on 5 August 1981, an attack attributed to the [Mojahedin-e Khalq](/orgs/mojahedin-e-khalq).

nodes.label: Hassan Karoubi
nodes.role: Iranian cleric; intermediary in the Iran-Contra affair

data.role: Iranian cleric; intermediary in the Iran-Contra affair
data.bio: Hassan Karoubi is a Shia cleric and brother of [Mehdi Karroubi](/people/mehdi-karroubi). He served as an intermediary in the early stages of the [Iran-Contra affair](/events/iran-contra-affair-1985). In late October 1985, he met with [Manucher Ghorbanifar](/people/manucher-ghorbanifar) and U.S. consultant Michael Ledeen in Geneva, presenting himself as a member of a 'moderate' faction within the Iranian establishment and proposing a staggered exchange of American hostages for Hawk, Sidewinder, and Phoenix missiles. He had previously been reported in contact with U.S. intelligence figures as early as 1980. His exact rank within the Iranian government was uncertain to American officials at the time.

nodes.label: Hassan Rouhani
nodes.role: 7th President of Iran (2013–2021)

data.role: 7th President of Iran (2013–2021)
data.bio: Hassan Rouhani was elected in 2013 on a centrist platform promising to ease sanctions. He oversaw negotiations that led to the 2015 [JCPOA](/events/jcpoa-nuclear-deal-2015) nuclear deal, the signature achievement of his presidency. After the United States withdrew from the JCPOA in 2018, Iran's economy deteriorated sharply. Prior to the presidency he served as Iran's chief nuclear negotiator (2003–2005) and as secretary of the [Supreme National Security Council](/orgs/supreme-national-security-council) for sixteen years.

nodes.label: Heydar Moslehi
nodes.role: Former Minister of Intelligence (2009–2013)

data.role: Former Minister of Intelligence (2009–2013)
data.bio: Heydar Moslehi served as Iran's Minister of Intelligence during [Mahmoud Ahmadinejad](/people/mahmoud-ahmadinejad)'s second term (2009–2013). His appointment became the epicentre of the Ahmadinejad–Khamenei power struggle: Ahmadinejad attempted to dismiss Moslehi in 2011, but [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) publicly overruled the dismissal — prompting Ahmadinejad to boycott cabinet meetings for eleven days. This was the most visible public rupture in the Ahmadinejad–Khamenei relationship. A mid-ranking cleric, Moslehi had previously been head of the Organisation of Islamic Endowments and was an Ahmadinejad loyalist until the falling out.

nodes.label: Hossein-Ali Montazeri
nodes.role: Grand Ayatollah; designated successor to Khomeini (1985–1989); leading dissident cleric

data.role: Grand Ayatollah; designated successor to Khomeini (1985–1989); leading dissident cleric
data.bio:
Hossein-Ali Montazeri was a Grand Ayatollah and one of the architects of Iran's Islamic Republic. A student of [Ruhollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini), he was imprisoned for years under the Shah and played a central role in drafting Iran's theocratic constitution. In 1979, he was elected chairman of the [Assembly of Experts for the Constitution](/orgs/assembly-of-experts-for-constitution), the 73-member body that drafted the Islamic Republic's constitution, with [Mohammad Beheshti](/people/mohammad-beheshti) as deputy chairman. The assembly embedded the concept of velayat-e faqih (guardianship of the jurist) into the new constitution. In 1985, the [Assembly of Experts](/orgs/assembly-of-experts) designated him as Khomeini's successor.

Montazeri's path to [dismissal](/events/montazeri-dismissal-1989) began with the [Iran-Contra affair](/events/iran-contra-affair-1985): his protégé [Mehdi Hashemi](/people/mehdi-hashemi), an IRGC official, leaked details of the secret U.S. arms deals in 1986. Hashemi was arrested and executed in September 1987, weakening Montazeri's political base. Then came the [1988 mass executions of political prisoners](/events/mass-executions-1988). Montazeri wrote at least three letters to Khomeini protesting the killings and confronted the Death Committee members directly, telling them: 'the greatest crime committed during the reign of the Islamic Republic has been committed by you.' He argued that prisoners — including [Mojahedin-e Khalq](/orgs/mojahedin-e-khalq) sympathisers who had already been tried and sentenced — deserved fair treatment under Islamic law. [Ahmad Khomeini](/people/ahmad-khomeini), who controlled access to his father, and [Akbar Hashemi Rafsanjani](/people/akbar-hashemi-rafsanjani), then Speaker of Parliament, both worked to isolate Montazeri. On 28 March 1989, Khomeini formally stripped him of the succession. The subsequent [1989 constitutional revision](/events/constitutional-revision-1989) removed the requirement that the Supreme Leader be a marja, clearing the path for [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) — who was only a Hojatoleslam — to succeed Khomeini.

After Khomeini's death, Montazeri continued to challenge Khamenei's religious credentials. In November 1997 he was placed under house arrest in Qom, where he remained until approximately 2003. He maintained ties to the reformist movement, sharing common ground with [Mohammad Khatami](/people/mohammad-khatami) on civil liberties and with the [Freedom Movement of Iran](/orgs/freedom-movement-of-iran) on democratic governance. During the [2009 Green Movement](/events/green-movement-protests-2009), he issued a fatwa declaring the disputed election illegitimate and became the movement's most authoritative clerical voice, aligning with [Mir-Hossein Mousavi](/people/mir-hossein-mousavi) and [Mehdi Karroubi](/people/mehdi-karroubi). He [died on 19 December 2009](/events/death-of-montazeri-2009); his funeral and mourning ceremonies became major protest gatherings that sustained the Green Movement through its most intense period.

nodes.label: Hossein-Ali Nayyeri
nodes.role: Head, Supreme Disciplinary Court for Judges (2013–present); former Sharia judge, Death Committee (1988)

data.role: Head, Supreme Disciplinary Court for Judges (2013–present); former Sharia judge, Death Committee (1988)
data.bio: Hossein-Ali Nayyeri presided over the Tehran 'Death Committee' that in summer 1988 ordered mass executions of political prisoners pursuant to [Ruhollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini)'s fatwa — an event the UN has classified as crimes against humanity and genocide. He is explicitly named in Khomeini's written decree as the Sharia judge responsible for the Tehran operations. He personally told Ayatollah Montazeri on 15 August 1988 that 750 prisoners had already been executed in Tehran. The committee he chaired — alongside [Morteza Eshraqi](/people/morteza-eshraqi), [Ebrahim Raisi](/people/ebrahim-raisi) (deputy), and [Mostafa Pourmohammadi](/people/mostafa-pourmohammadi) (Intelligence Ministry representative) — interrogated and executed prisoners in minutes. He was subsequently appointed Deputy Chief Justice of Iran's Supreme Court (1989–2013) and has headed the Supreme Disciplinary Court for Judges since 2013, facing no legal consequences.

nodes.label: Hossein Amir-Abdollahian
nodes.role: Former Minister of Foreign Affairs (2021–2024); died in helicopter crash

data.role: Former Minister of Foreign Affairs (2021–2024); died in helicopter crash
data.bio: Hossein Amir-Abdollahian was Iran's Foreign Minister under [Ebrahim Raisi](/people/ebrahim-raisi) from August 2021 until his death in May 2024. He died alongside Raisi in the helicopter crash on 19 May 2024 in East Azerbaijan. A career diplomat who specialised in Arab and African affairs, he served as Deputy Foreign Minister for Arab and African Affairs under [Manouchehr Mottaki](/people/manouchehr-mottaki) (2011–2016) and was known for his close ties to Hezbollah, Hamas, and the [IRGC Quds Force](/orgs/irgc-quds-force). He was removed from his deputy role by [Mohammad Javad Zarif](/people/javad-zarif) in 2016, then became a senior adviser to [Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf](/people/mohammad-bagher-ghalibaf) at the Majlis. His foreign policy prioritised the 'resistance axis' and East-facing engagement with Russia and China.

nodes.label: Hossein Dehghan
nodes.role: Former Minister of Defense (2013–2017); IRGC Aerospace Force veteran

data.role: Former Minister of Defense (2013–2017); IRGC Aerospace Force veteran
data.bio: Hossein Dehghan is an IRGC brigadier general who served as Defense Minister in [Hassan Rouhani](/people/hassan-rouhani)'s first cabinet (2013–2017). He joined the IRGC at its founding and commanded contingents in Lebanon and Syria in the early 1980s. He was Deputy Defense Minister under [Mohammad Khatami](/people/mohammad-khatami). Under Rouhani's second term he was replaced by Amir Hatami. He ran as a presidential candidate in 2021. He is subject to US and EU sanctions. He was a military adviser to [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) before his cabinet appointment.

nodes.label: Hossein Mousavian
nodes.role: Research Scholar at Princeton University

data.role: Research Scholar at Princeton University
data.bio: Seyed Hossein Mousavian is a former Iranian diplomat and nuclear policy scholar. He served as Iran's Ambassador to Germany (1990–1997) and as head of the Foreign Relations Committee of the Supreme National Security Council. From 2003 to 2005, he was spokesman and member of Iran's nuclear negotiating team under [Hassan Rouhani](/people/hassan-rouhani). In 2007, he was briefly arrested on espionage charges widely viewed as politically motivated by hardliners. He subsequently relocated to the United States and became a research scholar at Princeton University's School of Public and International Affairs. He was named in the leaked [Iran Experts Initiative](/events/iei-email-leak-2023) emails published by Semafor and Iran International in September 2023 as a participant in the network coordinated by the [Iranian Foreign Ministry](/orgs/ministry-of-foreign-affairs) through [IPIS](/orgs/ipis).

nodes.label: Hossein Salami
nodes.role: IRGC Commander-in-Chief (2019–2025)

data.role: IRGC Commander-in-Chief (2019–2025)
data.bio: Hossein Salami served as Commander-in-Chief of the [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) from April 2019, appointed by Khamenei following the removal of [Mohammad Ali Jafari](/people/mohammad-ali-jafari). He joined the IRGC in 1980 at the outbreak of the [Iran-Iraq War](/events/iran-iraq-war) and rose through its Aerospace Force before appointment to the top post. He oversaw the IRGC during the Mahsa Amini crackdown of 2022, the expansion of Iran's ballistic missile and drone programmes, and the direct Iranian strikes against Israel in April 2024. He was reportedly killed in Israeli strikes on Iran in June 2025.

nodes.label: Hossein Shariatmadari
nodes.role: Managing editor of Kayhan since 1993; Khamenei appointee; former MOIS interrogator

data.role: Managing editor of Kayhan since 1993; Khamenei appointee; former MOIS interrogator
data.bio: Hossein Shariatmadari has been the managing editor of [Kayhan](/orgs/kayhan-newspaper) since 1993, appointed directly by [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei), whose views Kayhan consistently reflects. He was a student activist in the 1970s who printed leaflets denouncing the Shah and was arrested by SAVAK, tortured (including having his nails pulled out and receiving electric shocks), and sentenced to life imprisonment — freed when the Shah fell. After the revolution he worked as an interrogator for the Ministry of Intelligence and Security (MOIS), where he is reported to have tortured dissidents to extract false confessions and to have produced state television programmes broadcasting those confessions. Under his editorship Kayhan has called for the prosecution of reformist presidents, denied the Holocaust (he stated in 2020 that the Holocaust 'was falsely claimed by Zionist and Western governments'), called for taking EU nationals hostage, incited violence against Baha'is, and attacked journalists. Shamsolvaezin considers him personally responsible for Neshat's closure.

nodes.label: Hossein Taeb
nodes.role: Former head, IRGC Intelligence Organisation (2009–2022)

data.role: Former head, IRGC Intelligence Organisation (2009–2022)
data.bio: Hossein Taeb headed the [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps)'s Intelligence Organisation for over thirteen years (2009–2022), making him one of the most feared figures in Iran's security apparatus. A student of [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) and wartime associate of [Mojtaba Khamenei](/people/mojtaba-khamenei) in the Habib Battalion, he combined IRGC and MOIS experience spanning from the [1988 massacre of political prisoners](/events/mass-executions-1988) through the [chain murders of 1998](/events/chain-murders-iran-1998) to the suppression of the [2009 Green Movement](/events/green-movement-protests-2009) and [2022 Mahsa Amini protests](/events/mahsa-amini-protests-2022). He was dismissed in June 2022 following a series of Israeli intelligence successes inside Iran, including the assassination of nuclear scientists, and a foiled plot against Israeli tourists in Turkey that exposed IRGC-IO networks. He was replaced by General Mohammad Kazemi.

nodes.label: Jafar Panahi
nodes.role: Filmmaker and political dissident

data.role: Filmmaker and political dissident
data.bio: Jafar Panahi is an internationally acclaimed Iranian filmmaker and one of the Islamic Republic's most prominent cultural dissidents. Winner of the Golden Lion at the Venice Film Festival and multiple prizes at Cannes and Berlin, he was arrested in 2010 and sentenced to six years in prison and a 20-year ban on filmmaking, travel, and public speaking. He continued to make films clandestinely under the ban. He was arrested again in July 2022 during the lead-up to the Mahsa Amini protests. On 28 January 2026, he co-signed a joint statement with other prominent political prisoners and civil society figures condemning the [January 2026 massacres](/events/january-2026-massacres).

nodes.label: Jamal Abdi
nodes.role: President, NIAC & Executive Director, NIAC Action

data.role: President, NIAC & Executive Director, NIAC Action
data.bio: Jamal Abdi is the president of the [National Iranian American Council (NIAC)](/orgs/niac) and executive director of [NIAC Action](/orgs/niac-action), succeeding [Trita Parsi](/people/trita-parsi) as the organization's leader. He publicly applauded the appointment of [Robert Malley](/people/robert-malley) as Biden's Iran envoy in January 2021, calling it 'a major step in putting U.S. diplomacy back in the hands of genuine professionals.' Under his leadership, NIAC Action has continued lobbying for diplomatic engagement with Iran and against sanctions.

nodes.label: Javad Owji
nodes.role: Former Minister of Petroleum (2021–2024)

data.role: Former Minister of Petroleum (2021–2024)
data.bio: Javad Owji served as Iran's Minister of Petroleum under [Ebrahim Raisi](/people/ebrahim-raisi) from 2021 to 2024. A longtime executive in the oil and gas sector, he previously served as Deputy Oil Minister and managing director of the National Iranian Gas Company (NIGC). His appointment was influenced by First Vice President [Mohammad Mokhber](/people/mohammad-mokhber), given Owji's connections to Setad subsidiaries. He is sanctioned by the US Treasury. His predecessor [Bijan Namdar Zanganeh](/people/bijan-namdar-zanganeh) is regarded as one of Iran's most capable oil technocrats, a standard Owji found difficult to match. He was replaced by [Mohsen Paknejad](/people/mohsen-paknejad) under [Masoud Pezeshkian](/people/masoud-pezeshkian).

nodes.label: Mohammad Javad Zarif
nodes.role: Minister of Foreign Affairs (2013–2021); Vice President (2024–2025)

data.role: Minister of Foreign Affairs (2013–2021); Vice President (2024–2025)
data.bio: Mohammad Javad Zarif served as Iran's Foreign Minister throughout [Hassan Rouhani](/people/hassan-rouhani)'s presidency, leading the Iranian negotiating team that concluded the [JCPOA](/events/jcpoa-nuclear-deal-2015) nuclear deal in 2015. A US-educated career diplomat, he was Iran's ambassador to the United Nations before becoming foreign minister. He endorsed [Masoud Pezeshkian](/people/masoud-pezeshkian) in the 2024 presidential election and briefly served as vice president for strategic affairs before resigning. He was subject to US sanctions from 2019. In 2023, leaked emails revealed that his Foreign Ministry had overseen the [Iran Experts Initiative](/events/iei-email-leak-2023), a covert influence operation run through [IPIS](/orgs/ipis) that recruited Western-based Iranian academics to promote Tehran's nuclear negotiating positions.

nodes.label: Kamal Kharrazi
nodes.role: Former Minister of Foreign Affairs (1997–2005); Strategic Council for Foreign Relations chairman

data.role: Former Minister of Foreign Affairs (1997–2005); Strategic Council for Foreign Relations chairman
data.bio: Kamal Kharrazi served as Iran's Foreign Minister for the full eight years of [Mohammad Khatami](/people/mohammad-khatami)'s presidency (1997–2005). Previously Iran's Ambassador to the United Nations (1989–1997), he was a calm and relatively moderate voice on international affairs during the reform era, presiding over the diplomatic opening with Europe and the Middle East. He replaced [Ali Akbar Velayati](/people/ali-akbar-velayati) when Khatami took office. Since 2005 he has headed the Strategic Council for Foreign Relations, an advisory body to the Supreme Leader. His brother Mohsen Kharazi is connected by marriage to the Khamenei family.

nodes.label: Karim Sanjabi
nodes.role: Leader of the National Front; Foreign Minister in provisional government (1979)

data.role: Leader of the National Front; Foreign Minister in provisional government (1979)
data.bio: Leader of the [National Front of Iran](/orgs/national-front-iran). Served briefly as Foreign Minister in [Bazargan](/people/mehdi-bazargan)'s provisional government in 1979, before being succeeded by [Ebrahim Yazdi](/people/ebrahim-yazdi). Went into exile and died in 1995.

nodes.label: Kaveh Afrasiabi
nodes.role: Iranian political scientist; convicted unregistered foreign agent

data.role: Iranian political scientist; convicted unregistered foreign agent
data.bio: Kaveh Afrasiabi is an Iranian political scientist and US permanent resident who was arrested in Watertown, Massachusetts in January 2021 for acting as an unregistered agent of the Iranian government in violation of the Foreign Agents Registration Act (FARA). He was paid approximately $265,000 by Iran's Permanent Mission to the United Nations to promote Iranian government positions while presenting himself as an independent expert in American media, including the New York Times, Washington Post, and Huffington Post. His case is closely related to the broader pattern of Iranian influence operations exposed by the [Iran Experts Initiative](/events/iei-email-leak-2023) leak. He was released to Iran in a prisoner swap in September 2023.

nodes.label: Kazem Rajavi
nodes.role: Human rights advocate; Iran's first post-revolution ambassador to UN in Geneva

data.role: Human rights advocate; Iran's first post-revolution ambassador to UN in Geneva
data.bio: Kazem Rajavi was a professor of political science at the University of Geneva and elder brother of [Massoud Rajavi](/people/massoud-rajavi). He played a crucial role in saving Massoud's life by lobbying internationally to commute his death sentence under the Shah. After the 1979 revolution, he briefly served as Iran's ambassador to the UN in Geneva before breaking with the regime. He became a leading human rights advocate, documenting abuses by the Islamic Republic. On 24 April 1990, he was [assassinated](/events/assassination-of-kazem-rajavi-1990) near his home in Coppet, Switzerland by Iranian intelligence agents who had entered the country on diplomatic passports. [Ali Fallahian](/people/ali-fallahian) was charged by a Swiss court with masterminding the operation.

nodes.label: Kazem Sami
nodes.role: Physician; former Minister of Health; Islamic nationalist; assassinated 1988

data.role: Physician; former Minister of Health; Islamic nationalist; assassinated 1988
data.bio: Kazem Sami was a physician, member of the [Freedom Movement of Iran](/orgs/freedom-movement-of-iran), and Islamic nationalist political figure who served as Iran's Minister of Health in the early post-revolution provisional government. A follower of [Mohammad Mossadegh](/people/mohammad-mossadegh)'s nationalist tradition, he became increasingly critical of the consolidation of clerical power. On 23 November 1988, Sami was attacked with an axe in his Tehran medical clinic and killed. His murder is considered likely the first victim of the [chain murders](/events/chain-murders-iran-1998) — the decade-long campaign of assassinations carried out by rogue units within the [Ministry of Intelligence](/orgs/ministry-of-intelligence). The connection was only established years later when the 1998 murders brought the entire chain to light.

nodes.label: Kazem Shariatmadari
nodes.role: Grand Ayatollah; critic of Velayat-e Faqih

data.role: Grand Ayatollah; critic of Velayat-e Faqih
data.bio: Grand Ayatollah Kazem Shariatmadari was one of Iran's most senior Shia clerics and a major religious authority. He initially supported the [1979 revolution](/events/iranian-revolution-1979) but became one of the most prominent critics of [Ruhollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini)'s doctrine of Velayat-e Faqih (Guardianship of the Islamic Jurist). He advocated for a more democratic interpretation of Islamic governance. In 1982, he was implicated in an alleged coup plot involving [Sadegh Ghotbzadeh](/people/sadegh-ghotbzadeh) and was stripped of his Grand Ayatollah title — an unprecedented act in Shia history. He was placed under house arrest and died on 3 April 1986.

nodes.label: Kheyrollah Parvin
nodes.role: Jurist member of the Guardian Council

data.role: Jurist member of the Guardian Council
data.bio: Kheyrollah Parvin is a jurist member of the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council), nominated by the Chief Justice and confirmed by the [Islamic Consultative Assembly](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly). He is one of six constitutional law experts on the Council responsible for vetting legislation and candidates.

nodes.label: Leila Pahlavi
nodes.role: Princess of Iran; youngest daughter of Mohammad Reza Shah Pahlavi

data.role: Princess of Iran; youngest daughter of Mohammad Reza Shah Pahlavi
data.bio:
Leila Pahlavi was the youngest daughter of [Mohammad Reza Shah Pahlavi](/people/mohammad-reza-shah-pahlavi) and [Farah Pahlavi](/people/farah-pahlavi), and the sister of [Reza Pahlavi](/people/reza-pahlavi) and [Ali Reza Pahlavi](/people/ali-reza-pahlavi).

She left Iran as a child following the 1979 Islamic Revolution. She studied at Brown University in the United States. She struggled with depression and eating disorders in exile.

She died on 10 June 2001 in London from an overdose of barbiturates. Her death deeply affected the Pahlavi family.

nodes.label: Lotfollah Safi Golpaygani
nodes.role: Grand Ayatollah; first Secretary of the Guardian Council (1980–1988)

data.role: Grand Ayatollah; first Secretary of the Guardian Council (1980–1988)
data.bio: Grand Ayatollah Lotfollah Safi Golpaygani was a senior Shia scholar and one of the longest-lived marjas of the twentieth and twenty-first centuries. He was elected to the [Assembly of Experts for the Constitution](/orgs/assembly-of-experts-for-constitution) in 1979, representing Markazi Province. Following the constitution's ratification, he was appointed the first Secretary of the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council), serving from 1980 to 1988. In this role, he oversaw the council's vetting of legislation for compliance with Islamic law and the constitution during the critical early years of the Islamic Republic. Based in Qom, he was a student of Grand Ayatollah Borujerdi and became a marja' in his own right. He died on 15 January 2022 at the age of 102.

nodes.label: Mahmoud Ahmadinejad
nodes.role: 6th President of Iran (2005–2013)

data.role: 6th President of Iran (2005–2013)
data.bio: Mahmoud Ahmadinejad served as mayor of Tehran from 2003 before his presidential election victory in 2005. His two-term presidency was defined by confrontational nuclear rhetoric, Holocaust denial, economic mismanagement, and the disputed 2009 election that triggered the [Green Movement](/events/green-movement-protests-2009). He significantly expanded the [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps)'s economic and political power. After leaving office he fell out with Khamenei and was disqualified from subsequent presidential races.

nodes.label: Mahmoud Alavi
nodes.role: Former Minister of Intelligence (2013–2021)

data.role: Former Minister of Intelligence (2013–2021)
data.bio: Mahmoud Alavi served as Iran's Minister of Intelligence for both terms of [Hassan Rouhani](/people/hassan-rouhani) (2013–2021). A cleric with a military background, he was previously head of the Political and Ideological Organisation of the Iranian Army — a post to which [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) had appointed him directly. He had also served as a deputy Defense Minister. As Intelligence Minister he navigated the tension between Rouhani's détente agenda and the hardline security establishment, and managed the period of the JCPOA negotiations and their aftermath.

nodes.label: Mahmoud Nabavian
nodes.role: MP for Tehran, 12th Majlis (2024–present); member, Front of Islamic Revolution Stability

data.role: MP for Tehran, 12th Majlis (2024–present); member, Front of Islamic Revolution Stability
data.bio: Mahmoud Nabavian is a Shia cleric who topped the Tehran ballot in the 2024 parliamentary election with the highest vote count in the capital. A student of [Mohammad-Taqi Mesbah Yazdi](/people/mohammad-taqi-mesbah-yazdi) and a senior member of the [Front of Islamic Revolution Stability](/orgs/front-of-islamic-revolution-stability), he has been a fierce opponent of Iran's nuclear diplomacy, comparing the [JCPOA](/events/jcpoa-nuclear-deal-2015) to Iran's humiliating 1828 territorial concessions to Russia. He attacked [Mohammad Javad Zarif](/people/javad-zarif) over the nuclear deal and was accused of lobbying against its revival in Vienna in 2022. He holds a PhD in philosophy from the [Imam Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini) Educational and Research Institute. Previously served in the 9th [Majlis](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly) (2012–2016).

nodes.label: Mahmoud Taleghani
nodes.role: Co-founder of Freedom Movement of Iran; revolutionary cleric

data.role: Co-founder of Freedom Movement of Iran; revolutionary cleric
data.bio: Liberal Shia cleric who co-founded the [Freedom Movement of Iran](/orgs/freedom-movement-of-iran) alongside [Mehdi Bazargan](/people/mehdi-bazargan) and [Yadollah Sahabi](/people/yadollah-sahabi). Imprisoned multiple times (4 arrests, over 11 years total) under the Pahlavi regime. Served in the provisional government after the [1979 revolution](/events/iranian-revolution-1979). Known for progressive interpretations of Islam and support for democratic governance. In August 1979, he was the highest vote-getter in Tehran Province in the election for the [Assembly of Experts for the Constitution](/orgs/assembly-of-experts-for-constitution), receiving over 2 million votes. He died on 9 September 1979, during the assembly's sessions, removing a key moderate voice from the constitutional debates.

nodes.label: Majid Khademi
nodes.role: Head of IRGC Intelligence Organisation (d. 2026)

data.role: Head of IRGC Intelligence Organisation (d. 2026)
data.bio: Majid Khademi served as head of the [IRGC Intelligence Organisation](/orgs/irgc-intelligence-organisation). During the [January 2026 massacres](/events/january-2026-massacres), he oversaw intelligence and surveillance operations against protesters. He publicly blamed "foreign agents" for instructing protesters to shoot each other, accusing US President Donald Trump of exacerbating the unrest as part of a "staged murder" strategy. Israeli intelligence accused Khademi of orchestrating overseas attacks and monitoring civilians during the domestic protests. He was killed on 6 April 2026 in an Israeli airstrike on Tehran during the 2026 Iran war.

nodes.label: Majid Sharif
nodes.role: Writer; translator; assassinated 1998

data.role: Writer; translator; assassinated 1998
data.bio: Majid Sharif was a writer, translator, and sociologist — a great-grandson of Ayatollah Habib Sharif Kashani. He held a Ph.D. in sociology. On 19 November 1998, Sharif disappeared during a morning jog. His body was found on 25 November; the official cause of death was listed as 'cardiac arrest,' but it was later established that he had been killed via potassium injection by agents of the [Ministry of Intelligence](/orgs/ministry-of-intelligence). His death, occurring just days before the murder of Dariush and Parvaneh Forouhar, was part of the [chain murders](/events/chain-murders-iran-1998) campaign.

nodes.label: Majid Takht-Ravanchi
nodes.role: Deputy Foreign Minister; Iran's UN Ambassador (2019–2023)

data.role: Deputy Foreign Minister; Iran's UN Ambassador (2019–2023)
data.bio: Majid Takht-Ravanchi is a career Iranian diplomat who served as deputy foreign minister for European and American affairs and was a member of Iran's nuclear negotiating team during the [JCPOA](/events/jcpoa-nuclear-deal-2015) talks. He later served as Iran's ambassador to the United Nations from 2019 to 2023. Leaked emails revealed that [Saeed Khatibzadeh](/people/saeed-khatibzadeh) sent his original proposal for the [Iran Experts Initiative](/events/iei-email-leak-2023) to Takht-Ravanchi alongside IPIS head [Mostafa Zahrani](/people/mostafa-zahrani).

nodes.label: Manouchehr Mottaki
nodes.role: Former Foreign Minister (2005–2010); MP for Tehran, 12th Majlis (2024–present)

data.role: Former Foreign Minister (2005–2010); MP for Tehran, 12th Majlis (2024–present)
data.bio: Manouchehr Mottaki served as Foreign Minister under [Mahmoud Ahmadinejad](/people/mahmoud-ahmadinejad) from 2005 to 2010, when he was abruptly dismissed by text message while on a foreign visit to Senegal. A career diplomat, he previously served as ambassador to Turkey and Pakistan and as a member of earlier [Islamic Consultative Assembly](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly) sessions. He ran for the 12th Majlis in 2024 heading the United Front principlist coalition list in Tehran.

nodes.label: Manucher Ghorbanifar
nodes.role: Iranian arms dealer; key intermediary in the Iran-Contra affair

data.role: Iranian arms dealer; key intermediary in the Iran-Contra affair
data.bio: Manucher Ghorbanifar is an expatriate Iranian arms dealer who served as the principal intermediary in the [Iran-Contra affair](/events/iran-contra-affair-1985). A former [SAVAK](/orgs/savak) associate under the Shah, he built a fortune in shipping before the [Iranian Revolution](/events/iranian-revolution-1979) and relocated to France after his assets were seized. In 1985, Ghorbanifar proposed the arms-for-hostages arrangement to U.S. and Israeli officials, brokering the initial shipments of TOW missiles and Hawk missile parts to Iran during the [Iran-Iraq War](/events/iran-iraq-war). He added a 41-percent markup to arms prices and was described by American officials as playing both sides for commercial advantage. The CIA issued a 'burn notice' on Ghorbanifar in 1984 after he failed multiple polygraph tests, labeling him an unreliable source. He re-emerged as a back-channel intelligence source to Pentagon officials during the lead-up to the 2003 Iraq War.

nodes.label: Maryam Rajavi
nodes.role: President-elect of NCRI (1993–present); co-leader of MEK

data.role: President-elect of NCRI (1993–present); co-leader of MEK
data.bio: Maryam Rajavi studied metallurgy at Sharif University of Technology and joined the [Mojahedin-e Khalq](/orgs/mojahedin-e-khalq) after two of her sisters were killed — one by SAVAK and one executed by [Ruhollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini)'s regime in 1982. She rose through MEK ranks, serving as joint leader (1985–1989) and Secretary General (1989–1993), before being elected President-elect of the [National Council of Resistance of Iran](/orgs/national-council-of-resistance-of-iran) in 1993. She married [Massoud Rajavi](/people/massoud-rajavi) in 1985. Under her leadership, women hold over half of senior positions in the organization. She is based in exile between France and Albania.

nodes.label: Mashallah Shamsolvaezin
nodes.role: Journalist and serial newspaper editor; editor of Kiyan, Jame'eh, Tous, Neshat, Asr-e Azadegan

data.role: Journalist and serial newspaper editor; editor of Kiyan, Jame'eh, Tous, Neshat, Asr-e Azadegan
data.bio: Mashallah Shamsolvaezin (also known by the pen name Mahmoud Shams) is the defining figure of the Khatami-era reformist press — a journalist who pioneered and perfected the 'serial newspaper' technique of keeping reformist journalism alive by immediately relaunching closed papers under new names. He began editing [Kian](/orgs/kian-magazine) in 1991, the intellectual magazine whose circle formed the intellectual core of the reform movement. After Kian was closed in 1998 he founded and edited [Jame'eh](/orgs/jameeh-newspaper), then [Tous](/orgs/tous-newspaper) after Jame'eh was closed, then [Neshat](/orgs/neshat-newspaper), then [Asr-e Azadegan](/orgs/asr-e-azadegan-newspaper) — four successive newspapers, each closed by the judiciary, each relaunched under a dormant license with the same editorial team. In November 2000 CPJ awarded him its International Press Freedom Award, calling him the 'dean of Iran's reformist press.' He was sentenced to 30 months in prison for [Neshat](/orgs/neshat-newspaper)'s capital-punishment article and spent time in Evin in solitary confinement. He serves as spokesman for the Iranian Committee for the Defense of Freedom of the Press and vice president of the Association of Iranian Journalists.

nodes.label: Masih Alinejad
nodes.role: Journalist and women's rights activist; prominent Iranian exile voice

data.role: Journalist and women's rights activist; prominent Iranian exile voice
data.bio:
Masih Alinejad is an Iranian journalist, author, and women's rights activist based in the United States. She is the founder of the My Stealthy Freedom and White Wednesdays campaigns against compulsory hijab in Iran.

Originally a parliamentary journalist in Iran, she was forced into exile and became one of the most visible voices of the Iranian diaspora opposition. During the 2022–2023 Woman, Life, Freedom protests, she was a leading international spokesperson for the movement.

She has been the target of multiple kidnapping and assassination plots by the Islamic Republic, including a 2021 FBI-foiled plot to abduct her from her Brooklyn home.

nodes.label: Masoud Pezeshkian
nodes.role: 9th President of Iran (2024–present)

data.role: 9th President of Iran (2024–present)
data.bio: Masoud Pezeshkian, a cardiac surgeon and long-serving MP for Tabriz, won the snap presidential election of July 2024 following Raisi's death. He ran on a moderate platform and was endorsed by reformist figures including [Khatami](/people/mohammad-khatami) and [Zarif](/people/javad-zarif). Ethnically Azerbaijani, he is the first non-Persian-speaking president. He previously served as Minister of Health under Khatami (2001–2005).

nodes.label: Massoud Rajavi
nodes.role: Leader of MEK (1979–2003); co-founder of NCRI

data.role: Leader of MEK (1979–2003); co-founder of NCRI
data.bio: Massoud Rajavi joined the [Mojahedin-e Khalq](/orgs/mojahedin-e-khalq) in 1967 and worked directly with founder [Mohammad Hanifnejad](/people/mohammad-hanifnejad). Arrested and sentenced to death by the Shah's regime, his sentence was commuted to life imprisonment thanks to international pressure led by his brother [Kazem Rajavi](/people/kazem-rajavi). Released in January 1979 during the revolution, he became the MEK's undisputed leader. After the [June 1981 crackdown](/events/june-20-uprising-1981) he fled to Paris with [Abolhassan Banisadr](/people/abolhassan-banisadr), where he co-founded the [National Council of Resistance of Iran](/orgs/national-council-of-resistance-of-iran). Expelled from France in 1986, he relocated to Iraq and established Camp Ashraf under Saddam Hussein's patronage. He launched the disastrous [Operation Forough-e Javidan](/events/operation-forough-e-javidan-1988) in July 1988. He disappeared in March 2003 during the US invasion of Iraq and has not been publicly seen since.

nodes.label: Mehdi Bazargan
nodes.role: Founder of Freedom Movement of Iran; Prime Minister of interim government (1979)

data.role: Founder of Freedom Movement of Iran; Prime Minister of interim government (1979)
data.bio: Engineer, academic, and political leader. First secretary-general of the [Freedom Movement of Iran](/orgs/freedom-movement-of-iran) (1961–1995). Co-founded the movement alongside [Mahmoud Taleghani](/people/mahmoud-taleghani) and [Yadollah Sahabi](/people/yadollah-sahabi) as a religiously oriented offshoot of the [National Front](/orgs/national-front-iran). Appointed by [Ruhollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini) as head of the provisional government after the [1979 revolution](/events/iranian-revolution-1979). Resigned on 5 November 1979 after the [US embassy seizure](/events/iran-hostage-crisis-1979), declaring he could not govern while revolutionary bodies operated outside his authority. Served in parliament (Majlis) 1980–1984. Died 20 January 1995.

nodes.label: Mehdi Hashemi
nodes.role: Head of IRGC Bureau for Liberation Movements; cleric executed for leaking Iran-Contra affair

data.role: Head of IRGC Bureau for Liberation Movements; cleric executed for leaking Iran-Contra affair
data.bio: Mehdi Hashemi was a Shia cleric who headed the [Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) Bureau for Liberation Movements, overseeing Iran's revolutionary export operations including support for [Hezbollah](/orgs/hezbollah) and Afghan mujahideen groups. A close associate of [Hossein-Ali Montazeri](/people/hossein-ali-montazeri) — his brother Hadi was Montazeri's son-in-law — Hashemi opposed Iran's secret arms dealings with the United States during the [Iran-Iraq War](/events/iran-iraq-war). He leaked details of the covert arrangement, including Robert McFarlane's May 1986 visit to Tehran, to the Lebanese magazine Ash-Shiraa, whose publication on 3 November 1986 triggered the [Iran-Contra affair](/events/iran-contra-affair-1985) scandal. Arrested in October 1986 on charges including treason, sedition, and murder, he was tried by a Special Clerical Court and executed on 28 September 1987. His prosecution and execution weakened the political position of his patron Montazeri and is widely seen as part of the power struggle between [Akbar Hashemi Rafsanjani](/people/akbar-hashemi-rafsanjani)'s pragmatist camp and the revolutionary-export faction.

nodes.label: Mehdi Karroubi
nodes.role: Speaker of Parliament (1989–1992, 2000–2004); Green Movement leader

data.role: Speaker of Parliament (1989–1992, 2000–2004); Green Movement leader
data.bio: Mehdi Karroubi served as Speaker of the [Islamic Consultative Assembly](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly) twice. A presidential candidate in 2009, he refused to accept the disputed official results and became a co-leader of the [Green Movement](/events/green-movement-protests-2009) alongside [Mir-Hossein Mousavi](/people/mir-hossein-mousavi). He has been held under house arrest in Tehran since February 2011 without trial, one of the most prominent political detainees in the Islamic Republic.

nodes.label: Mehdi Shabzendedar Jahromi
nodes.role: Clerical member of the Guardian Council (reappointed 2022)

data.role: Clerical member of the Guardian Council (reappointed 2022)
data.bio: Mehdi Shabzendedar Jahromi is a clerical member of the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council), reappointed by [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) in July 2022 alongside [Mohammad-Reza Modarresi Yazdi](/people/mohammad-reza-modarresi-yazdi). He is one of the six faqih members directly appointed by the Supreme Leader to serve on the Council.

nodes.label: Mir-Hossein Mousavi
nodes.role: Last Prime Minister of Iran (1981–1989); Green Movement co-leader

data.role: Last Prime Minister of Iran (1981–1989); Green Movement co-leader
data.bio: Mir-Hossein Mousavi served as Iran's last prime minister, managing the economy through the entire [Iran-Iraq War](/events/iran-iraq-war). An architect and painter by training, he was a founding figure of the Islamic Republic before gradually moving into opposition. His disputed loss in the 2009 presidential election triggered the [Green Movement](/events/green-movement-protests-2009). He has been under house arrest in Tehran since February 2011. In 2023 he called for a referendum to fundamentally change the constitution, a significant shift from his earlier reformist position. He is a distant relative of [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) through a shared ancestral line from Khameneh.

nodes.label: Mohammad Ali Jafari
nodes.role: Commander-in-Chief of the IRGC (2007–2019)

data.role: Commander-in-Chief of the IRGC (2007–2019)
data.bio: Mohammad Ali Jafari served as Commander-in-Chief of the [Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) from 2007 to 2019. He reorganised the IRGC's command structure and expanded its internal security role, particularly the [Basij](/orgs/basij) militia. He oversaw IRGC operations during the Syrian civil war and the expansion of Iran's ballistic missile programme. He was replaced by [Hossein Salami](/people/hossein-salami) in April 2019.

nodes.label: Mohammad-Ali Rajai
nodes.role: 2nd President of the Islamic Republic of Iran (August 1981)

data.role: 2nd President of the Islamic Republic of Iran (August 1981)
data.bio: Mohammad-Ali Rajai was Iran's second president, serving for less than one month after his election on 24 July 1981. A committed revolutionary and former teacher, he had previously served as prime minister. He and his prime minister [Mohammad-Javad Bahonar](/people/mohammad-javad-bahonar) were assassinated in a bombing at the prime minister's office on 30 August 1981, an attack attributed to the [Mojahedin-e Khalq](/orgs/mojahedin-e-khalq) (MEK).

nodes.label: Mohammad Aliabadi
nodes.role: Vice President under Ahmadinejad; head of Physical Education Organization; caretaker Oil Minister; linked to founding of Tat Bank

data.role: Vice President under Ahmadinejad; head of Physical Education Organization; caretaker Oil Minister; linked to founding of Tat Bank
data.bio:
Mohammad Aliabadi is an Iranian official who served as Vice President and head of the Physical Education Organization from 2005 to 2009 under President [Mahmoud Ahmadinejad](/people/mahmoud-ahmadinejad). He holds degrees in civil engineering and architecture from K.N. Toosi University of Technology.

Aliabadi served in the [Iran-Iraq War](/events/iran-iraq-war) and held earlier posts as deputy minister of Roads and Transportation and deputy director of the Bonyad-e Mostazafan (Foundation of the Oppressed). In June 2011, Ahmadinejad appointed him caretaker Minister of Petroleum after sacking the incumbent, a move widely criticised given Aliabadi's lack of energy sector experience. He was replaced after approximately two months by [Rostam Ghasemi](/people/rostam-ghasemi).

Aliabadi is linked to the establishment of Tat Bank, later restructured into [Ayandeh Bank](/orgs/ayandeh-bank). Reports indicate that sports federations under his authority, including indebted football clubs, appeared among Tat Bank's main shareholders, and that the league organisation directed funds to the bank. This facilitated banker [Ali Ansari](/people/ali-ansari-banker)'s entry into Iran's financial sector. Aliabadi also served as president of the National Olympic Committee of Iran from 2008 to 2014.

nodes.label: Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf
nodes.role: Speaker of Parliament (2020–present); Former Mayor of Tehran (2005–2017)

data.role: Speaker of Parliament (2020–present); Former Mayor of Tehran (2005–2017)
data.bio: Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf is a senior [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) figure who commanded the IRGC Aerospace Force and later the national police before serving as Mayor of Tehran for twelve years. He has run for president three times without success. Elected Speaker of Parliament in 2020 after the principlist sweep of that year's elections. He was one of 24 IRGC commanders who signed a 1999 letter threatening military action against student protesters, and has admitted to personally participating in street-level violence against them.

nodes.label: Mohammad Beheshti
nodes.role: First Chief Justice of the Islamic Republic; co-founder of the Islamic Republican Party; architect of the 1979 Constitution

data.role: First Chief Justice of the Islamic Republic; co-founder of the Islamic Republican Party; architect of the 1979 Constitution
data.bio:
Ayatollah Mohammad Beheshti was widely considered the second most powerful figure in Iran after [Ruhollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini) during the early years of the Islamic Republic. A cleric trained in Qom and holder of a doctorate in philosophy from the University of Tehran, he served as head of the Islamic Centre Hamburg from 1965 to 1970, where he built networks among Iranian students in Europe and published multilingual Islamic journals.

After the [Iranian Revolution](/events/iranian-revolution-1979), Beheshti became a member and then chairman of the [Islamic Revolutionary Council](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-council). He served as vice-chairman of the Assembly of Experts for the Constitution, where he was the principal architect of the new constitution, embedding the doctrine of velayat-e faqih (guardianship of the jurist) into Iran's governmental framework.

Beheshti co-founded the [Islamic Republican Party](/orgs/islamic-republican-party) alongside [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei), [Akbar Hashemi Rafsanjani](/people/akbar-hashemi-rafsanjani), [Mohammad-Javad Bahonar](/people/mohammad-javad-bahonar), and others, and served as its secretary-general. He was appointed the first head of the [Judiciary of Iran](/orgs/judiciary-of-iran) in 1979. He was also a co-founder of the [Haghani Seminary](/orgs/haghani-seminary) in Qom in the 1960s, alongside [Mohammad-Taqi Mesbah Yazdi](/people/mohammad-taqi-mesbah-yazdi) and [Ahmad Jannati](/people/ahmad-jannati).

Beheshti led the clerical establishment's campaign against President [Abolhassan Banisadr](/people/abolhassan-banisadr), whose impeachment in June 1981 he orchestrated through the IRP's parliamentary majority. On 28 June 1981, Beheshti was assassinated along with more than 70 other officials in the [Hafte Tir bombing](/events/hafte-tir-bombing-1981) at IRP headquarters, an attack attributed to the [Mojahedin-e Khalq](/orgs/mojahedin-e-khalq).

nodes.label: Mohammad Hanifnejad
nodes.role: Co-founder of Mojahedin-e Khalq (MEK)

data.role: Co-founder of Mojahedin-e Khalq (MEK)
data.bio: Mohammad Hanifnejad was an agricultural engineer and the principal co-founder of the [Mojahedin-e Khalq](/orgs/mojahedin-e-khalq) (MEK), established on 6 September 1965 alongside [Saeed Mohsen](/people/saeed-mohsen) and [Ali-Asghar Badizadegan](/people/ali-asghar-badizadegan). A former member of Mehdi Bazargan's Freedom Movement, he became convinced that a more radical approach was needed to challenge the Shah's regime. The three founders spent 1965–1969 recruiting cadres and developing the MEK's ideology, blending progressive Islam with anti-imperialist politics. He was arrested by SAVAK in 1971 and executed on 25 May 1972 along with his two co-founders.

nodes.label: Mohammad Hashemi
nodes.role: Head of IRIB (1980–1994)

data.role: Head of IRIB (1980–1994)
data.bio: Mohammad Hashemi is the younger brother of [Akbar Hashemi Rafsanjani](/people/akbar-hashemi-rafsanjani) and served as head of [IRIB](/orgs/irib) for fourteen years, the longest tenure of any IRIB director. Appointed in 1980, he oversaw the broadcasting monopoly through the [Iran–Iraq War](/events/iran-iraq-war) era and the post-war reconstruction period, expanding IRIB's reach from two television channels to a multi-channel network. Under his leadership IRIB served as the primary wartime propaganda organ, broadcasting footage from the front lines and promoting the war effort. He was replaced in 1994 when Supreme Leader [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) appointed [Ali Larijani](/people/ali-larijani) to bring a harder editorial line. Mohammad Hashemi later ran unsuccessfully for president in 2005 and remained active in pragmatist political circles.

nodes.label: Mohammad-Hassan Sadeghi Moghaddam
nodes.role: Jurist member of the Guardian Council; member, Central Committee for Election Supervision

data.role: Jurist member of the Guardian Council; member, Central Committee for Election Supervision
data.bio: Mohammad-Hassan Sadeghi Moghaddam is a jurist member of the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council) and a member of its Central Committee for Election Supervision. He was sanctioned by the US Treasury in February 2020 alongside [Ahmad Jannati](/people/ahmad-jannati), [Siamak Rahpeyk](/people/siamak-rahpeyk), and [Abbas-Ali Kadkhodaei](/people/abbas-ali-kadkhodaei) for preventing free and fair parliamentary elections in Iran.

nodes.label: Mohammad Jafar Pouyandeh
nodes.role: Author; translator; Iranian Writers' Association member; assassinated 1998

data.role: Author; translator; Iranian Writers' Association member; assassinated 1998
data.bio: Mohammad Jafar Pouyandeh was a prominent author and translator who, along with Mohammad Mokhtari, served on the organising committee of the [Iranian Writers' Association](/orgs/iranian-writers-association). He was a signatory of the 1994 'We Are the Writers!' open letter demanding an end to censorship. On 8 December 1998 — just days after Mokhtari's disappearance — Pouyandeh vanished. His body was found strangled in the Shahriar district west of Tehran on 11 December. He and Mokhtari were the last known victims of the [chain murders](/events/chain-murders-iran-1998). The subsequent investigation traced both killings to the same [Ministry of Intelligence](/orgs/ministry-of-intelligence) unit. Lawyer [Nasser Zarafshan](/people/nasser-zarafshan) represented Pouyandeh's family; his wife Sima Sahebi was herself arrested for publishing a letter criticising the authorities for blocking memorial services.

nodes.label: Mohammad-Javad Bahonar
nodes.role: Prime Minister of Iran (1981)

data.role: Prime Minister of Iran (1981)
data.bio: Mohammad-Javad Bahonar was a cleric and co-founder of the [Islamic Republican Party](/orgs/islamic-republican-party). He was elected to the [Assembly of Experts for the Constitution](/orgs/assembly-of-experts-for-constitution) in 1979, representing Kerman Province with over 80% of the vote. He served briefly as prime minister under President [Mohammad-Ali Rajai](/people/mohammad-ali-rajai) in 1981. He and Rajai were assassinated in a bombing at the prime minister's office on 30 August 1981, an attack attributed to the [Mojahedin-e Khalq](/orgs/mojahedin-e-khalq) (MEK). He had previously served as Minister of Education.

nodes.label: Mohammad Khamenei
nodes.role: Cleric; member of the Assembly of Experts for the Constitution; brother of Ali Khamenei

data.role: Cleric; member of the Assembly of Experts for the Constitution; brother of Ali Khamenei
data.bio: Mohammad Khamenei is a Shia cleric and the brother of Supreme Leader [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei). He was elected to the [Assembly of Experts for the Constitution](/orgs/assembly-of-experts-for-constitution) in 1979, representing Khorasan Province with over 60% of the vote. He was among the 53 assembly members who voted in favor of the velayat-e faqih provisions that granted sweeping powers to the Supreme Leader. He has maintained a relatively low political profile compared to his brother.

nodes.label: Mohammad Khatami
nodes.role: 5th President of Iran (1997–2005)

data.role: 5th President of Iran (1997–2005)
data.bio: Mohammad Khatami was elected president in a landslide in 1997, winning around 70% of the vote on a platform of civil society, rule of law, and a Dialogue Among Civilizations. His two terms represented the peak of the reformist movement, marked by a free press and attempts to improve relations with the West. His reform agenda was consistently blocked by the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council) and judiciary.

nodes.label: Mohammad Mokhber
nodes.role: Former First Vice President (2021–2024); former acting President (May–July 2024)

data.role: Former First Vice President (2021–2024); former acting President (May–July 2024)
data.bio: Mohammad Mokhber served as [Ebrahim Raisi](/people/ebrahim-raisi)'s First Vice President from 2021 and automatically became acting President upon Raisi's death on 19 May 2024, serving in that capacity until [Masoud Pezeshkian](/people/masoud-pezeshkian) was inaugurated in late July 2024. Before politics, Mokhber was the long-serving head of Setad Ejraiye Farmane Hazrat Emam (Setad), the vast conglomerate operating under the direct control of the [Office of the Supreme Leader](/orgs/office-of-the-supreme-leader), estimated to control assets worth tens of billions of dollars. His tenure at Setad made him one of Khamenei's most trusted operational executives. He is subject to US and EU sanctions.

nodes.label: Mohammad Mokhtari
nodes.role: Writer; poet; mythologist; Iranian Writers' Association member; assassinated 1998

data.role: Writer; poet; mythologist; Iranian Writers' Association member; assassinated 1998
data.bio: Mohammad Mokhtari was a writer, poet, and mythologist who served on the organising committee of the [Iranian Writers' Association](/orgs/iranian-writers-association). A leading figure in the effort to revive the association and defend freedom of expression in Iran, he was one of the 134 signatories of the landmark 1994 open letter 'We Are the Writers!' which demanded an end to censorship. On 3 December 1998, Mokhtari left his home and never returned. His body was found on 9 December in the town of Ray, south of Tehran — he had been strangled by agents of the [Ministry of Intelligence](/orgs/ministry-of-intelligence). His murder, together with that of Mohammad Jafar Pouyandeh days later and the earlier killing of Dariush and Parvaneh Forouhar, became the core cases of the [chain murders](/events/chain-murders-iran-1998) investigation.

nodes.label: Mohammad Mossadegh
nodes.role: Prime Minister of Iran (1951–1953); founder of National Front

data.role: Prime Minister of Iran (1951–1953); founder of National Front
data.bio: Mohammad Mossadegh was Prime Minister of Iran from 1951 to 1953. He founded the [National Front](/orgs/national-front-iran) in 1949 and led the campaign to nationalize Iran's oil industry, previously controlled by the Anglo-Iranian Oil Company. His democratically elected government was overthrown in the August 1953 coup d'état orchestrated by the CIA and MI6 (Operation Ajax/Boot). He was tried by a military tribunal, sentenced to three years in prison, and then placed under house arrest in his estate in Ahmadabad, where he remained until his death on 5 March 1967. His legacy shaped the ideology of the [Freedom Movement of Iran](/orgs/freedom-movement-of-iran), whose members described themselves as 'Mossadeghists.'

nodes.label: Mohammad Mousavi Khoeiniha
nodes.role: Publisher, Salam newspaper; leader of the 1979 US Embassy hostage-takers; reformist cleric

data.role: Publisher, Salam newspaper; leader of the 1979 US Embassy hostage-takers; reformist cleric
data.bio: Mohammad Mousavi Khoeiniha (born c. 1940) represents one of the most striking political transformations in the Islamic Republic: the spiritual patron and leader of the students who seized the US Embassy in November 1979 and held 52 American hostages for 444 days became, by the 1990s, the publisher of the reformist [Salam](/orgs/salam-newspaper) newspaper and a leading voice of the Khatami reform movement. Khoeiniha served as the student hostage-takers' clerical patron, giving the takeover religious legitimacy. He subsequently became Prosecutor-General of Iran. By 1991, when he founded Salam, he was a leading member of the reformist Association of Combatant Clerics (Majma'-e Rowhaniyoon-e Mobarez), the clerical wing of the reformist left. When Salam was closed in July 1999 Khoeiniha was tried by the Special Court for the Clergy — the court that handles clerics outside the regular judicial system.

nodes.label: Mohammad Rasoulof
nodes.role: Filmmaker and political dissident

data.role: Filmmaker and political dissident
data.bio: Mohammad Rasoulof is an Iranian filmmaker whose work frequently addresses political repression under the Islamic Republic. His film *There Is No Evil* (2020), which explored the death penalty in Iran, won the Golden Bear at the Berlin Film Festival. He was sentenced to prison and flogging in 2024 for his films and fled Iran before the sentence was carried out. On 28 January 2026, he co-signed a joint statement with other prominent political prisoners and civil society figures condemning the [January 2026 massacres](/events/january-2026-massacres).

nodes.label: Mohammad Reyshahri
nodes.role: Former Minister of Intelligence (1984–1989); head of Hajj and Pilgrimage Organisation

data.role: Former Minister of Intelligence (1984–1989); head of Hajj and Pilgrimage Organisation
data.bio: Mohammad Reyshahri was Iran's first dedicated Minister of Intelligence, serving under Prime Minister [Mir-Hossein Mousavi](/people/mir-hossein-mousavi) and President [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) from 1984 to 1989. He established the Ministry of Intelligence (MOIS) as an institutional entity and presided over it during the 1988 mass executions of political prisoners — the bloodiest period of internal repression in the Islamic Republic's history. He later headed the Hajj and Pilgrimage Organisation. He ran for president in 1997 but received less than 0.5% of the vote. He is regarded as a founding architect of Iran's security state.

nodes.label: Mohammad-Reza Aref
nodes.role: First Vice President (2024–present); former First Vice President (2013); former reformist presidential candidate

data.role: First Vice President (2024–present); former First Vice President (2013); former reformist presidential candidate
data.bio: Mohammad-Reza Aref is a reformist politician and electrical engineer who served briefly as First Vice President under [Hassan Rouhani](/people/hassan-rouhani) (2013) before stepping aside at [Mohammad Khatami](/people/mohammad-khatami)'s request to consolidate the reformist vote behind Rouhani in 2013. He had previously served as First Vice President under Khatami (2001–2005), where he oversaw technology and infrastructure policy. A former Minister of ICT under Khatami, he is now First Vice President under [Masoud Pezeshkian](/people/masoud-pezeshkian) (2024–present). He has been an MP for Tehran and heads the reformist parliamentary faction.

nodes.label: Mohammad Reza Ashtiani
nodes.role: Former Minister of Defense (2021–2024)

data.role: Former Minister of Defense (2021–2024)
data.bio: Mohammad Reza Ashtiani served as Minister of Defense under [Ebrahim Raisi](/people/ebrahim-raisi) from 2021 to 2024. A senior military officer, he previously served as Deputy Chief of Staff of the Iranian Armed Forces. He oversaw a period of accelerated missile and drone program development, including the April 2024 direct Iranian attack on Israel. He was replaced by [Aziz Nasirzadeh](/people/aziz-nasirzadeh) under [Masoud Pezeshkian](/people/masoud-pezeshkian).

nodes.label: Mohammad Reza Farzin
nodes.role: Governor of the Central Bank of Iran; oversaw dissolution of Ayandeh Bank

data.role: Governor of the Central Bank of Iran; oversaw dissolution of Ayandeh Bank
data.bio:
Mohammad Reza Farzin is an Iranian banker and economist who has served as Governor of the [Central Bank of Iran](/orgs/central-bank-of-iran) since January 2023. He was appointed by President [Ebrahim Raisi](/people/ebrahim-raisi) and retained in the role by President [Masoud Pezeshkian](/people/masoud-pezeshkian) after Raisi's death in May 2024.

Farzin's career spans three decades in Iran's banking sector, including senior positions at [Bank Melli Iran](/orgs/bank-melli-iran) and the Bank of Industry and Mine. Notably, he previously served as chairman of the board of [Ayandeh Bank](/orgs/ayandeh-bank).

As Central Bank governor, Farzin has pursued a programme of banking sector consolidation, targeting insolvent and unlicensed financial institutions. His most significant action was the October 2025 dissolution of Ayandeh Bank, whose operating licence he revoked after finding $5.1 billion in accumulated losses, $2.9 billion in overdrafts, and a capital adequacy ratio of negative 600 percent. The bank's assets and deposits were merged into Bank Melli Iran.

nodes.label: Mohammad-Reza Khatami
nodes.role: First Deputy Speaker of Parliament, 6th Majlis (2000–2004); Secretary-General, Islamic Iran Participation Front

data.role: First Deputy Speaker of Parliament, 6th Majlis (2000–2004); Secretary-General, Islamic Iran Participation Front
data.bio: Mohammad-Reza Khatami is a nephrologist and reformist politician, the younger brother of [President Mohammad Khatami](/people/mohammad-khatami). He entered politics after his brother's 1997 victory, becoming the top-polling candidate in Tehran in the March 2000 election. He served as Deputy Speaker of the reformist 6th [Majlis](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly) alternating with [Behzad Nabavi](/people/behzad-nabavi). He founded and served as Secretary-General of the [Islamic Iran Participation Front](/orgs/islamic-iran-participation-front), Iran's largest reformist party. He is married to Zahra Eshraghi, a granddaughter of [Ruhollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini). He was permanently disqualified from running for office by the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council).

nodes.label: Mohammad-Reza Modarresi Yazdi
nodes.role: Clerical member of the Guardian Council (reappointed 2022)

data.role: Clerical member of the Guardian Council (reappointed 2022)
data.bio: Mohammad-Reza Modarresi Yazdi is a clerical member of the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council), reappointed by [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) in July 2022 alongside Mehdi Shabzendedar Jahromi. A jurist from the Yazd clerical tradition, he is one of the six faqih members directly appointed by the Supreme Leader.

nodes.label: Mohammad Reza Shah Pahlavi
nodes.role: Shah of Iran (1941–1979)

data.role: Shah of Iran (1941–1979)
data.bio: Mohammad Reza Shah Pahlavi was the last Shah of Iran, ruling from 1941 until his overthrow in the [1979 revolution](/events/iranian-revolution-1979). His secret police, [SAVAK](/orgs/savak), suppressed political opposition including the [National Front](/orgs/national-front-iran), the [Freedom Movement of Iran](/orgs/freedom-movement-of-iran), and leftist groups. He was restored to power after the 1953 coup that overthrew Prime Minister [Mohammad Mossadegh](/people/mohammad-mossadegh). He fled Iran on 16 January 1979 and died in exile in Cairo on 27 July 1980. Father of [Reza Pahlavi](/people/reza-pahlavi).

nodes.label: Mohammad Sarafraz
nodes.role: Head of IRIB (2014–2016); Former head of Press TV

data.role: Head of IRIB (2014–2016); Former head of Press TV
data.bio: Mohammad Sarafraz served as head of [IRIB](/orgs/irib) from 2014 to 2016, appointed by Supreme Leader [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei). He had previously headed [Press TV](/orgs/press-tv), IRIB's English-language satellite news channel, where he oversaw the broadcast of forced confessions including that of Newsweek journalist Maziar Bahari, filmed inside Evin Prison in 2009. For his role in airing coerced confessions, Sarafraz was individually sanctioned by the European Union before his appointment as IRIB head. His tenure as IRIB director was relatively short and he was replaced by [Abdolali Ali-Asgari](/people/abdolali-ali-asgari) in 2016.

nodes.label: Mohammad-Taqi Mesbah Yazdi
nodes.role: Spiritual leader of the Front of Islamic Revolution Stability; co-founder of Haghani Seminary

data.role: Spiritual leader of the Front of Islamic Revolution Stability; co-founder of Haghani Seminary
data.bio: Mohammad-Taqi Mesbah Yazdi was the most influential ideologue of the Iranian ultra-right, described as 'a hardliner to terrify hardliners'. A student of [Ruhollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini) at the Qom seminary, he co-founded the [Haghani Seminary](/orgs/haghani-seminary) — the institution that trained the backbone of the Islamic Republic's clerical management class. He was the spiritual mentor of [Mahmoud Ahmadinejad](/people/mahmoud-ahmadinejad) and the guiding force behind the [Front of Islamic Revolution Stability](/orgs/front-of-islamic-revolution-stability), formally established in 2011 by his students. He endorsed Saeed Jalili in the 2013 presidential election and backed [Ebrahim Raisi](/people/ebrahim-raisi) in 2017. He repeatedly urged the [Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) to suppress the reform movement by any means. He died on 1 January 2021.

nodes.label: Mohammad Tavasoli
nodes.role: Secretary-general of Freedom Movement of Iran (2017–present)

data.role: Secretary-general of Freedom Movement of Iran (2017–present)
data.bio: Third secretary-general of the [Freedom Movement of Iran](/orgs/freedom-movement-of-iran) since 2017, succeeding [Ebrahim Yazdi](/people/ebrahim-yazdi). Imprisoned once under the Pahlavi regime (1 year) and five times under the Islamic Republic (4.3 years total).

nodes.label: Mohammad Yazdi
nodes.role: Chief Justice of Iran (1989–1999); chairman of the Assembly of Experts (2015–2016)

data.role: Chief Justice of Iran (1989–1999); chairman of the Assembly of Experts (2015–2016)
data.bio: Ayatollah Mohammad Yazdi was a senior hardline cleric who served as Chief Justice of Iran from 1989 to 1999, appointed by [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei). He was elected to the [Assembly of Experts for the Constitution](/orgs/assembly-of-experts-for-constitution) in 1979, representing Kermanshah Province.
