A member of the [Islamic Republican Party](/orgs/islamic-republican-party), he later served multiple terms on the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council) and in the [Assembly of Experts](/orgs/assembly-of-experts), briefly chairing the latter from 2015 to 2016. As head of the [Judiciary](/orgs/judiciary-of-iran) during the 1990s, he oversaw a period of political repression including crackdowns on the press and reformist activists. He was known as one of the most conservative figures in the clerical establishment.

nodes.label: Mohsen Armin
nodes.role: Reformist politician and journalist; member of the 6th Majlis

data.role: Reformist politician and journalist; member of the 6th Majlis
data.bio: Mohsen Armin is a reformist politician, journalist, and key figure in the [Mojahedin of the Islamic Revolution Organization](/orgs/mojahedin-of-islamic-revolution-organization). He served as a member of parliament in the reformist 6th [Majlis](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly). He has been active in political journalism and the reform movement, advocating for political openness and democratic governance within the Islamic Republic framework.

nodes.label: Mohsen Fakhrizadeh
nodes.role: Head, IRGC Amad Project (nuclear weapons programme); IRGC Brigadier General; assassinated 2020

data.role: Head, IRGC Amad Project (nuclear weapons programme); IRGC Brigadier General; assassinated 2020
data.bio: Mohsen Fakhrizadeh was Iran's foremost nuclear weapons scientist and the head of the Amad Project — the Islamic Republic's clandestine nuclear weapons research programme, which the IAEA documented as pursuing warhead miniaturisation, implosion tests, and delivery system integration. He held the rank of Brigadier General in the [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) and served simultaneously as Deputy Defence Minister for Research and Innovation. The IAEA identified him as the scientist 'orchestrating' Iran's military nuclear work in classified annex documents leaked in 2011. He was the target of [Amir Ali Hajizadeh](/people/amir-ali-hajizadeh)'s aerospace programme's delivery systems. He was assassinated on 27 November 2020 near Absard, east of Tehran, in a sophisticated remote-controlled ambush widely attributed to Israeli intelligence. His killing triggered calls in the Iranian Majlis to accelerate uranium enrichment and contributed to further JCPOA collapse. [Ali Akbar Salehi](/people/ali-akbar-salehi), then head of the [Atomic Energy Organisation of Iran](/orgs/atomic-energy-organisation-of-iran), described him as the 'shahid of science'.

nodes.label: Mohsen Kadivar
nodes.role: Dissident Shia cleric; philosopher of religion; professor at Duke University

data.role: Dissident Shia cleric; philosopher of religion; professor at Duke University
data.bio: Mohsen Kadivar is a Shia cleric and philosopher who became one of the most prominent religious critics of velayat-e faqih (guardianship of the jurist). Trained in the Qom seminaries, he studied under Grand Ayatollah Hossein-Ali Montazeri and earned a doctorate in philosophy from Tarbiat Modares University. In February 1999 he was sentenced to 18 months in prison by the Special Clergy Court for 'disseminating propaganda against the Islamic Republic' and 'disturbing public opinion' — his real offence being published critiques arguing that absolute clerical rule has no basis in Islamic jurisprudence. After his release he continued teaching at Tarbiat Modares University until 2008, when he left Iran for a visiting position at Duke University, where he remains as a research professor of Islamic studies. He co-authored the 2010 [Green Movement](/events/green-movement-protests-2009) manifesto with [Abdolkarim Soroush](/people/abdolkarim-soroush), [Akbar Ganji](/people/akbar-ganji), and others, calling for the resignation of Ahmadinejad and free elections.

nodes.label: Mohsen Kangarloo
nodes.role: Security Adviser to Prime Minister Mousavi (1982–1989); key Iranian negotiator in the Iran-Contra affair

data.role: Security Adviser to Prime Minister Mousavi (1982–1989); key Iranian negotiator in the Iran-Contra affair
data.bio: Mohsen Kangarloo served as security adviser to Prime Minister [Mir-Hossein Mousavi](/people/mir-hossein-mousavi) from 1982 to 1989 and was one of the key Iranian negotiators in the [Iran-Contra affair](/events/iran-contra-affair-1985). In February 1986, he met with Oliver North, [Manucher Ghorbanifar](/people/manucher-ghorbanifar), and Albert Hakim in Germany to discuss weapons transfers and hostage releases. Kangarloo pushed for the United States to provide Iran advanced air-to-air missiles for its fleet of F-14 fighters needed in the [Iran-Iraq War](/events/iran-iraq-war). He later stated in a 2018 interview that [Hezbollah](/orgs/hezbollah) took hostages 'because it can be useful for us,' confirming Iran's strategic leverage over the hostage situation. He died on 25 March 2020.

nodes.label: Mohsen Paknejad
nodes.role: Minister of Petroleum (2024–present)

data.role: Minister of Petroleum (2024–present)
data.bio: Mohsen Paknejad is Iran's current Minister of Petroleum, appointed by [Masoud Pezeshkian](/people/masoud-pezeshkian) in August 2024. A petroleum engineer, he previously served in various management roles within the national oil sector, including at the National Iranian Oil Company (NIOC). His appointment was seen as a technocratic choice aimed at improving production efficiency and attracting investment to the oil sector.

nodes.label: Mohsen Rezaei
nodes.role: Commander-in-Chief of the IRGC (1981–1997); Secretary of the Expediency Discernment Council (1997–2021); Vice President for Economic Affairs (2021–2024)

data.role: Commander-in-Chief of the IRGC (1981–1997); Secretary of the Expediency Discernment Council (1997–2021); Vice President for Economic Affairs (2021–2024)
data.bio: Mohsen Rezaei was the longest-serving commander of the [Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps), leading it from 1981 to 1997 — a period spanning the [Iran-Iraq War](/events/iran-iraq-war), the [Iran-Contra affair](/events/iran-contra-affair-1985), and the IRGC's transformation from a revolutionary militia into a major military-industrial force. Appointed by [Ruhollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini) at age 27, he oversaw the IRGC's wartime operations and its expansion into intelligence and economic activities. After leaving the IRGC, he served as Secretary of the [Expediency Discernment Council](/orgs/expediency-discernment-council) under [Akbar Hashemi Rafsanjani](/people/akbar-hashemi-rafsanjani). He ran unsuccessfully for president in 2005, 2009, and 2013. In 2021, he was appointed Vice President for Economic Affairs under [Ebrahim Raisi](/people/ebrahim-raisi). An Argentine court issued an international arrest warrant for him in 2006 in connection with the 1994 AMIA bombing in Buenos Aires. In March 2026, [Mojtaba Khamenei](/people/mojtaba-khamenei) appointed him as military adviser to the Supreme Leader.

nodes.label: Mojtaba Khamenei
nodes.role: 3rd Supreme Leader of the Islamic Republic of Iran (2026–present)

data.role: 3rd Supreme Leader of the Islamic Republic of Iran (2026–present)
data.bio: Mojtaba Khamenei is the second son of [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) and has been a shadowy but powerful figure in the Islamic Republic for decades. He long headed an intelligence bureau within the [Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps), functioning as a key conduit between his father's office and the IRGC's security apparatus. His wife is Zahra Haddad-Adel, daughter of [Gholam-Ali Haddad-Adel](/people/gholam-ali-haddad-adel), former Speaker of the [Islamic Consultative Assembly](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly). Following Ali Khamenei's death on 28 February 2026, the [Assembly of Experts](/orgs/assembly-of-experts) elected Mojtaba as the third Supreme Leader on 8 March 2026 — the second succession in the republic's history.

nodes.label: Monireh Gorji
nodes.role: Quran scholar; only woman elected to the 1979 Assembly of Experts for the Constitution

data.role: Quran scholar; only woman elected to the 1979 Assembly of Experts for the Constitution
data.bio: Monireh Gorji was an Islamic scholar and the only woman among the 73 members of the [Assembly of Experts for the Constitution](/orgs/assembly-of-experts-for-constitution) in 1979. Elected from Tehran Province on the [Islamic Republican Party](/orgs/islamic-republican-party) ticket with over 1.3 million votes, she was a Quran scholar and mujtahid. Despite her IRP affiliation, she raised objections during the constitutional debates about restrictions being imposed on women in the name of Islam. She continued her scholarly career in Quranic studies and Islamic jurisprudence.

nodes.label: Morteza Agha-Tehrani
nodes.role: MP; Chairman, Majlis Cultural Committee; former Secretary-General, Front of Islamic Revolution Stability

data.role: MP; Chairman, Majlis Cultural Committee; former Secretary-General, Front of Islamic Revolution Stability
data.bio: Morteza Agha-Tehrani is a senior figure of the [Front of Islamic Revolution Stability](/orgs/front-of-islamic-revolution-stability) who served as the party's founding Secretary-General before handing the role to [Sadegh Mahsouli](/people/sadegh-mahsouli) in late 2020. He chairs the [Islamic Consultative Assembly](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly)'s Cultural Committee — a key position that made him the primary legislative driver of the controversial mandatory hijab enforcement bill. He was also described as [Mahmoud Ahmadinejad](/people/mahmoud-ahmadinejad)'s personal ethics teacher, cementing the Paydari network's ties to the Ahmadinejad presidency.

nodes.label: Morteza Eshraqi
nodes.role: Former Tehran Prosecutor General; member, Death Committee (1988)

data.role: Former Tehran Prosecutor General; member, Death Committee (1988)
data.bio: Morteza Eshraqi was Tehran's Prosecutor General in 1988 and is one of two officials explicitly named in [Ruhollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini)'s fatwa as responsible for implementing the mass execution of political prisoners in Tehran — an event the UN classifies as crimes against humanity. His deputy, [Ebrahim Raisi](/people/ebrahim-raisi), also sat on the committee. His presence at the committee's 15 August 1988 meeting with Ayatollah Montazeri is captured on audio recordings published in 2016. He subsequently served as a judge on the Supreme Court from 1989 to 1998.

nodes.label: Morteza Motahhari
nodes.role: Co-founder of Combatant Clergy Association; Council of Islamic Revolution chairman

data.role: Co-founder of Combatant Clergy Association; Council of Islamic Revolution chairman
data.bio: Morteza Motahhari was one of the most influential clerical scholars of the Islamic Revolution. A devoted student of [Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini) in Qom, he co-founded the Hussainiyyah Ershad and the [Combatant Clergy Association](/orgs/combatant-clergy-association) (JRM). He served as chairman of Khomeini's Council of the Islamic Revolution in early 1979. He was assassinated on 1 May 1979 by the [Forqan Group](/orgs/forqan-group). His intellectual legacy underpins much of the Islamic Republic's ideological framework. He is the father-in-law of former [Majlis](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly) Speaker [Ali Larijani](/people/ali-larijani) and the father of MP [Ali Motahari](/people/ali-motahari).

nodes.label: Mostafa Chamran
nodes.role: First Minister of Defence of Islamic Republic; co-founder of Amal Movement in Lebanon

data.role: First Minister of Defence of Islamic Republic; co-founder of Amal Movement in Lebanon
data.bio: Engineer, guerrilla fighter, and political figure. Helped organize the [Freedom Movement of Iran](/orgs/freedom-movement-of-iran) from the United States. In 1971 he left the US for Lebanon, where he became the close associate and military right-hand man of [Musa al-Sadr](/people/musa-al-sadr). Together they co-founded the [Amal Movement](/orgs/amal-movement) in 1975 as the military wing of the Movement of the Deprived. Chamran established training camps in southern Lebanon near the Israeli border, where he trained hundreds of Shia fighters — and, by his own account, Iranian volunteers as well. His training infrastructure, fighter networks, and organizational methods in Lebanon laid critical groundwork for the later emergence of [Hezbollah](/orgs/hezbollah) after the 1982 Israeli invasion. After the [Iranian Revolution](/events/iranian-revolution-1979), Chamran returned to Iran and served as the first post-revolution Minister of Defence. He fought on the front lines in the [Iran–Iraq War](/events/iran-iraq-war) and was killed in action on 21 June 1981 in Dehlavieh, Khuzestan.

nodes.label: Mostafa Mohammad-Najjar
nodes.role: Former Minister of Defense (2005–2009); former Minister of Interior (2009–2013)

data.role: Former Minister of Defense (2005–2009); former Minister of Interior (2009–2013)
data.bio: Mostafa Mohammad-Najjar is an IRGC brigadier general who served as Defense Minister in [Mahmoud Ahmadinejad](/people/mahmoud-ahmadinejad)'s first term (2005–2009) and then transferred to the Interior Ministry in the second term (2009–2013). As Defense Minister he presided over the rapid advancement of Iran's missile programme. As Interior Minister he supervised the disputed 2009 presidential election that triggered the [Green Movement protests](/events/green-movement-protests-2009). He is subject to US and EU sanctions.

nodes.label: Mostafa Pourmohammadi
nodes.role: Former Minister of Justice (2013–2017); former Minister of Interior (2005–2008)

data.role: Former Minister of Justice (2013–2017); former Minister of Interior (2005–2008)
data.bio: Mostafa Pourmohammadi is a cleric who served as Interior Minister under [Mahmoud Ahmadinejad](/people/mahmoud-ahmadinejad) (2005–2008) and later as Justice Minister under [Hassan Rouhani](/people/hassan-rouhani) (2013–2017). His appointment by Rouhani was controversial and drew protests from human rights organisations, as Pourmohammadi had served as a member of the 'Death Committee' — the three-person panel in Tehran that approved the mass execution of thousands of political prisoners in 1988 following [Ruhollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini)'s fatwa. He later explicitly defended the executions. He ran in the 2024 presidential election.

nodes.label: Mostafa Tajzadeh
nodes.role: Reformist politician; former Deputy Interior Minister

data.role: Reformist politician; former Deputy Interior Minister
data.bio: Mostafa Tajzadeh is a prominent reformist politician and member of the [Mojahedin of the Islamic Revolution Organization](/orgs/mojahedin-of-islamic-revolution-organization). He served as deputy interior minister under [President Mohammad Khatami](/people/mohammad-khatami). He was arrested after the 2009 [Green Movement](/events/green-movement-protests-2009) protests and sentenced to six years in prison. He attempted to run for president in 2021 but was disqualified by the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council). He was re-arrested in 2022 during the Mahsa Amini protests.

nodes.label: Mostafa Zahrani
nodes.role: Director of IPIS (Institute for Political and International Studies)

data.role: Director of IPIS (Institute for Political and International Studies)
data.bio: Mostafa Zahrani served as head of the [Institute for Political and International Studies (IPIS)](/orgs/ipis), the [Ministry of Foreign Affairs](/orgs/ministry-of-foreign-affairs)' in-house think tank. He oversaw the [Iran Experts Initiative](/events/iei-email-leak-2023), serving as the main liaison between the network of Western-based academics and [Mohammad Javad Zarif](/people/javad-zarif)'s Foreign Ministry. Leaked emails showed that IEI members such as [Ali Vaez](/people/ali-vaez) shared article drafts with Zahrani before publication, and that [Ariane Tabatabai](/people/ariane-tabatabai) consulted him on conference attendance and engagements with Israeli and Saudi counterparts.

nodes.label: Moussa Khiabani
nodes.role: Deputy leader of MEK; commander of MEK armed wing

data.role: Deputy leader of MEK; commander of MEK armed wing
data.bio: Moussa Khiabani studied physics at the University of Tehran and joined the [Mojahedin-e Khalq](/orgs/mojahedin-e-khalq). He trained in guerrilla warfare in Lebanon and was sentenced to life imprisonment in 1972 for MEK activities. Released in January 1979 during the revolution, he became [Massoud Rajavi](/people/massoud-rajavi)'s deputy and commanded the MEK's armed wing inside Iran. On 8 February 1982, the [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) raided his safehouse in northern Tehran. After a three-hour firefight, Khiabani was killed along with [Ashraf Rabiei](/people/ashraf-rabiei).

nodes.label: Musa al-Sadr
nodes.role: Lebanese-Iranian Shia cleric; founder of the Amal Movement

data.role: Lebanese-Iranian Shia cleric; founder of the Amal Movement
data.bio: Musa al-Sadr was an Iranian-born Lebanese Shia cleric who became the most prominent leader of Lebanon's Shia community in the 1960s and 1970s. He founded the Movement of the Deprived in 1974 and co-founded its military wing, the [Amal Movement](/orgs/amal-movement), with [Mostafa Chamran](/people/mostafa-chamran). He worked closely with Chamran to establish training facilities for Shia fighters in southern Lebanon. His political mobilization of the Shia community laid the social and organizational groundwork from which [Hezbollah](/orgs/hezbollah) later emerged. He disappeared during a visit to Libya on 31 August 1978 under mysterious circumstances widely attributed to Muammar Gaddafi, and is presumed dead.

nodes.label: Narges Mohammadi
nodes.role: Human rights activist; Nobel Peace Prize laureate (2023); imprisoned in Evin Prison

data.role: Human rights activist; Nobel Peace Prize laureate (2023); imprisoned in Evin Prison
data.bio:
Narges Mohammadi is an Iranian human rights activist and the vice-president of the Defenders of Human Rights Center, founded by [Shirin Ebadi](/people/shirin-ebadi). She has been arrested and imprisoned multiple times by the Islamic Republic for her activism, particularly her campaigns against the death penalty and mandatory hijab.

She was awarded the Nobel Peace Prize in 2023 for her fight against the oppression of women in Iran and her efforts to promote human rights and freedom for all. She received the prize while imprisoned in Evin Prison.

During the 2025–2026 protests, she characterized [Reza Pahlavi](/people/reza-pahlavi)'s supporters as "the opposition against opposition," suggesting that his polarizing role alienated other opposition factions.

nodes.label: Naser Makarem Shirazi
nodes.role: Grand Ayatollah; Shia marja' (source of emulation)

data.role: Grand Ayatollah; Shia marja' (source of emulation)
data.bio: Grand Ayatollah Naser Makarem Shirazi is one of the most prominent Shia scholars and marjas (sources of emulation) in Iran. He was elected to the [Assembly of Experts for the Constitution](/orgs/assembly-of-experts-for-constitution) in 1979, representing Fars Province with over 84% of the vote. Based in Qom, he is the author of the influential Tafsir Nemooneh (Exemplary Commentary), a multi-volume Quranic exegesis. He has served as a member of the [Society of Seminary Teachers of Qom](/orgs/society-of-seminary-teachers-of-qom) and is known for issuing fatwas on social and economic matters. While broadly supportive of the Islamic Republic's system, he has occasionally criticized economic policies and corruption.

nodes.label: Nasrin Sotoudeh
nodes.role: Human rights lawyer and political prisoner

data.role: Human rights lawyer and political prisoner
data.bio: Nasrin Sotoudeh is an Iranian human rights lawyer known for defending political prisoners, women's rights activists, and minors facing the death penalty. She has been imprisoned multiple times by the [judiciary](/orgs/judiciary-of-iran) for her legal advocacy. In 2019, she was sentenced to 38 years in prison and 148 lashes on charges including espionage and insulting the Supreme Leader. She has defended clients involved in the 2009 Green Movement and the 2022 Mahsa Amini protests. On 28 January 2026, she co-signed a joint statement with other prominent political prisoners and civil society figures condemning the [January 2026 massacres](/events/january-2026-massacres). She received the European Parliament's Sakharov Prize for Freedom of Thought in 2012.

nodes.label: Nasrollah Pejmanfar
nodes.role: MP; Chairman, Article 90 Committee, 11th and 12th Majlis

data.role: MP; Chairman, Article 90 Committee, 11th and 12th Majlis
data.bio: Nasrollah Pejmanfar is a hardline MP and member of the [Front of Islamic Revolution Stability](/orgs/front-of-islamic-revolution-stability) who chairs the [Islamic Consultative Assembly](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly)'s Article 90 Committee — the parliamentary oversight body that investigates complaints against the judiciary, executive, and Majlis itself. His committee chairmanship gives the Paydari Front an institutional oversight lever in disputes with other state bodies.

nodes.label: Nasser Zarafshan
nodes.role: Human rights lawyer; represented chain murders victims' families; political prisoner

data.role: Human rights lawyer; represented chain murders victims' families; political prisoner
data.bio: Nasser Zarafshan is a human rights lawyer who represented the families of [Mohammad Mokhtari](/people/mohammad-mokhtari) and [Mohammad Jafar Pouyandeh](/people/mohammad-jafar-pouyandeh), two writers murdered in the [chain murders](/events/chain-murders-iran-1998). He publicly challenged the official narrative that the killings were the work of 'rogue agents,' arguing that the fatwa authorising the murders came from senior clerical figures. He stated: 'The problem is not to which faction the perpetrators belong, but to which faction the issuer of the fatwa belongs.' On 19 March 2002, Zarafshan was sentenced to five years in prison — two years for 'disseminating state secrets' and three years for allegedly possessing firearms — plus 70 lashes for possessing alcohol. His bail was set at $50,000, four times the bail of some of the accused murderers. His imprisonment was widely condemned by international human rights organisations and PEN International.

nodes.label: Noor Pahlavi
nodes.role: Eldest daughter of Reza Pahlavi; declared heir to Pahlavi succession (2019)

data.role: Eldest daughter of Reza Pahlavi; declared heir to Pahlavi succession (2019)
data.bio: Noor Pahlavi is the eldest daughter of [Reza Pahlavi](/people/reza-pahlavi) and [Yasmine Etemad-Amini](/people/yasmine-etemad-amini). In 2019, her father declared her heir to the Pahlavi succession, emphasizing equal rights between men and women — a break from traditional male-line primogeniture.

nodes.label: Parvaneh Forouhar
nodes.role: Political activist; co-leader of Nation of Iran Party; assassinated 1998

data.role: Political activist; co-leader of Nation of Iran Party; assassinated 1998
data.bio: Parvaneh Eskandari Forouhar was a political activist and co-leader of the [Nation of Iran Party](/orgs/nation-of-iran-party) alongside her husband [Dariush Forouhar](/people/dariush-forouhar). Active in Iranian politics since the 1950s, she was a fierce advocate for women's rights and democratic governance. On the night of 22 November 1998, [Ministry of Intelligence](/orgs/ministry-of-intelligence) agents entered the Forouhar home and murdered both husband and wife — she was stabbed 25 times. Their double assassination was one of the most shocking acts of the [chain murders](/events/chain-murders-iran-1998) and galvanised the reformist press and civil society into demanding accountability from the intelligence services.

nodes.label: Peyman Jebelli
nodes.role: Head of IRIB (2021–present)

data.role: Head of IRIB (2021–present)
data.bio: Peyman Jebelli has served as head of [IRIB](/orgs/irib) since 2021, appointed by Supreme Leader [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei). He previously headed IRIB's World Service, overseeing international broadcasting operations. His tenure has coincided with the [Mahsa Amini protests](/events/mahsa-amini-protests-2022), during which IRIB broadcast forced confessions of detained protesters and framed demonstrators as rioters backed by foreign powers. The European Union imposed fresh sanctions on IRIB leadership in October 2022 in response to this coverage.

nodes.label: Pirouz Davani
nodes.role: Leftist activist; former political prisoner; editor; assassinated 1998

data.role: Leftist activist; former political prisoner; editor; assassinated 1998
data.bio: Pirouz Davani was a leftist political activist, former political prisoner, and editor of the newspaper *Pirooz*. He disappeared on 29 August 1998. His body was never recovered, but he is believed to have been murdered around 20 September 1998 by agents of the [Ministry of Intelligence](/orgs/ministry-of-intelligence). His disappearance was one of the earliest of the 1998 wave of [chain murders](/events/chain-murders-iran-1998), preceding the more widely publicised killings of the Forouhars, Mokhtari, and Pouyandeh.

nodes.label: Qasem Soleimani
nodes.role: Commander of the IRGC Quds Force (1998–2020)

data.role: Commander of the IRGC Quds Force (1998–2020)
data.bio: Qasem Soleimani rose from a working-class background in Kerman to become the architect of Iran's network of regional proxies — [Hezbollah](/orgs/hezbollah), Shia militias in Iraq, the Houthis, and forces in Syria. As [Quds Force](/orgs/irgc-quds-force) commander from 1998 until his assassination, he was widely described as the most powerful military operative in the Middle East. He directed Iranian intervention in Syria from 2012 and was the primary coordinator of Iran's Axis of Resistance. He was killed on 3 January 2020 in a US drone strike at Baghdad International Airport, authorised by President Trump.

nodes.label: Qorbanali Dorri-Najafabadi
nodes.role: Former Minister of Intelligence (1997–1999); former Prosecutor General

data.role: Former Minister of Intelligence (1997–1999); former Prosecutor General
data.bio: Qorbanali Dorri-Najafabadi was [Mohammad Khatami](/people/mohammad-khatami)'s first Intelligence Minister, appointed in 1997 as a compromise candidate after hardliners blocked Khatami's preferred choice. His tenure coincided with the 1998 'chain murders' — the serial killing of reformist intellectuals, writers, and political activists by rogue elements of the [Ministry of Intelligence](/orgs/ministry-of-intelligence). When evidence implicated the ministry itself, Dorri-Najafabadi resigned in 1999. He later served as Iran's Prosecutor General (2008–2009) and as a member of the [Assembly of Experts](/orgs/assembly-of-experts). He was a member of the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council) before his intelligence appointment.

nodes.label: Reza Marashi
nodes.role: Former Research Director, NIAC

data.role: Former Research Director, NIAC
data.bio: Reza Marashi served as the first Research Director of the [National Iranian American Council (NIAC)](/orgs/niac), joining in 2010. Before NIAC, he spent four years at the U.S. State Department's Office of Iranian Affairs and worked at the Institute for National Strategic Studies on China-Middle East issues. He was also a Tehran-based private strategic consultant connected to the Atieh Bahar consulting ecosystem. His career path — from the State Department's Iran desk to NIAC — exemplifies the revolving door between government Iran policy positions and the NIAC network that critics have highlighted.

nodes.label: Reza Pahlavi
nodes.role: Crown Prince of Iran (in exile); opposition leader advocating secular democratic transition

data.role: Crown Prince of Iran (in exile); opposition leader advocating secular democratic transition
data.bio:
Reza Pahlavi is the eldest son of the late [Mohammad Reza Shah Pahlavi](/people/mohammad-reza-shah-pahlavi) and [Farah Pahlavi](/people/farah-pahlavi). He was designated Crown Prince in 1967 during his father's coronation. In August 1978, he left Iran to train as a fighter pilot at Reese Air Force Base in Texas and never returned after the [1979 Islamic Revolution](/events/iranian-revolution-1979). He enrolled at Williams College in September 1979 but dropped out in 1980, later obtaining a B.S. in political science from the University of Southern California by correspondence in 1985.

Following his father's death in Cairo on 27 July 1980, he declared himself Shah of Iran (Reza Shah II) on his 20th birthday at Koubbeh Palace in Cairo — a claim the U.S. State Department immediately disassociated itself from. He later shifted his public stance toward advocacy for a secular democratic republic, positioning himself as a unifying figure for the Iranian opposition rather than claiming the throne.

In 1982, he was involved in a coup d'état scheme alongside Israeli businessman Yaakov Nimrodi, Saudi businessman Adnan Khashoggi, and General Said Razvani. The plan reportedly had CIA and Israeli cabinet approval but was abandoned in 1983 when Israeli Prime Minister Menachem Begin resigned. He announced formation of a government-in-exile in May 1986.

He married [Yasmine Etemad-Amini](/people/yasmine-etemad-amini) in 1986. They have three daughters, including [Noor Pahlavi](/people/noor-pahlavi), whom he declared heir to the Pahlavi succession in 2019 — a break from traditional male-line primogeniture. His siblings include [Ali Reza Pahlavi](/people/ali-reza-pahlavi) (died 2011) and [Leila Pahlavi](/people/leila-pahlavi) (died 2001).

He co-founded the [Iran National Council](/orgs/iran-national-council) in 2013 as a platform for coordinating opposition activities, and launched the [Phoenix Project of Iran](/orgs/phoenix-project-of-iran) think tank in Washington, D.C. in February 2019, focused on post-regime transition planning.

During the [2022–2023 Woman, Life, Freedom protests](/events/mahsa-amini-protests-2022), he emerged as one of the most visible exile voices, calling for civil disobedience and international support. He organized a rally on 11 February 2023 that drew 80,000+ attendees in Los Angeles. A Change.org petition for Pahlavi as representative reached 460,000+ signatures. In March 2023, he spoke at the Oxford Union, and in April 2023 he visited Israel, meeting with President Isaac Herzog and Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu.

His father's former confidant, [Ahmad Ali Masoud Ansari](/people/ahmad-ali-ansari) (brother of historian [Ali M. Ansari](/people/ali-m-ansari)), has publicly alleged that Reza Pahlavi and his aides were funded by Saudi Arabia and the CIA — claims that Reza Pahlavi has not publicly addressed. Nobel Peace Prize laureate [Narges Mohammadi](/people/narges-mohammadi) has characterized his supporters as "the opposition against opposition," suggesting his role is polarizing within the broader movement. He resides in Potomac, Maryland.

nodes.label: Robert Malley
nodes.role: US Special Envoy for Iran (2021–2023); President of International Crisis Group (2018–2021)

data.role: US Special Envoy for Iran (2021–2023); President of International Crisis Group (2018–2021)
data.bio: Robert Malley is an American lawyer and diplomat who served as president and CEO of the [International Crisis Group](/orgs/international-crisis-group) from 2018 to 2021, and as the Biden administration's special envoy for Iran from January 2021 until his suspension in June 2023. While at ICG and the State Department, he employed or worked with at least three members of the [Iran Experts Initiative](/events/iei-email-leak-2023) — [Ariane Tabatabai](/people/ariane-tabatabai), [Ali Vaez](/people/ali-vaez), and [Dina Esfandiary](/people/dina-esfandiary). He recruited Tabatabai to join his Iran team at the State Department in February 2021. In June 2023, his security clearance was suspended and he was placed on unpaid leave amid an FBI investigation into his handling of classified information. The [IEI email leak](/events/iei-email-leak-2023) in September 2023 intensified congressional scrutiny of his Iran negotiating team.

nodes.label: Rostam Ghasemi
nodes.role: Former Minister of Petroleum (2011–2013); former Minister of Roads (2021–2023)

data.role: Former Minister of Petroleum (2011–2013); former Minister of Roads (2021–2023)
data.bio: Rostam Ghasemi is an IRGC brigadier general and oil sector executive who served as Minister of Petroleum in [Mahmoud Ahmadinejad](/people/mahmoud-ahmadinejad)'s second term (2011–2013) and then as Minister of Roads and Urban Development under [Ebrahim Raisi](/people/ebrahim-raisi) (2021–2023). He was previously managing director of the IRGC engineering arm Khatam al-Anbiya — the massive construction and contracting conglomerate that controls much of Iran's infrastructure market. He was replaced as Roads Minister in 2023. He is subject to US, EU, and UN sanctions.

nodes.label: Rouzbeh Parsi
nodes.role: Head of MENA Programme, Swedish Institute of International Affairs

data.role: Head of MENA Programme, Swedish Institute of International Affairs
data.bio: Rouzbeh Parsi is a Swedish-Iranian academic who headed the Middle East and North Africa programme at the Swedish Institute of International Affairs (UI). He was identified as a member of the [Iran Experts Initiative](/events/iei-email-leak-2023), attending the inaugural meeting at Vienna's Palais Coburg in May 2014 alongside other Western-based Iranian academics recruited by the Iranian [Foreign Ministry](/orgs/ministry-of-foreign-affairs) through [IPIS](/orgs/ipis) to promote Tehran's nuclear negotiating positions in Western policy circles.

nodes.label: Ruhollah Hosseinian
nodes.role: Hardline cleric; former MP; MOIS-linked; publicly justified chain murders

data.role: Hardline cleric; former MP; MOIS-linked; publicly justified chain murders
data.bio: Ruhollah Hosseinian is a hardline cleric who worked within the [Ministry of Intelligence](/orgs/ministry-of-intelligence) apparatus and later served as a member of parliament. He became notorious for publicly justifying the [chain murders](/events/chain-murders-iran-1998) on television, declaring: 'We have been a murderer ourselves! Murder of dissidents is not carried out the way that it has been described!' He attended Saeed Emami's memorial service. Hosseinian's open defence of the killings, at a time when the regime was attempting to portray them as the work of 'rogue elements,' was seen as revealing the true sentiment of the hardline establishment toward the assassination campaign.

nodes.label: Ruhollah Khomeini
nodes.role: Founder and first Supreme Leader of the Islamic Republic of Iran

data.role: Founder and first Supreme Leader of the Islamic Republic of Iran
data.bio: Ruhollah Khomeini was the Shia cleric and marja who led the 1979 [Iranian Revolution](/events/iranian-revolution-1979), overthrowing the Pahlavi monarchy and establishing the Islamic Republic. He served as Supreme Leader from 1979 until his death in June 1989. He developed the doctrine of Velayat-e Faqih (Guardianship of the Islamic Jurist), which forms the constitutional basis of clerical rule in Iran.

nodes.label: Ruhollah Zam
nodes.role: Dissident journalist; founder of Amad News

data.role: Dissident journalist; founder of Amad News
data.bio: Ruhollah Zam was an Iranian dissident journalist and the founder of the Amad News Telegram channel, which became one of the most influential opposition media outlets during the December 2017–January 2018 protests in Iran, with over one million followers. The son of reformist cleric Ahmad Zam, he had fled Iran and was living in exile in France. In October 2019, he was lured to Iraq by [IRGC Intelligence](/orgs/irgc-intelligence-organisation) operatives and captured. He was returned to Iran, where [IRIB](/orgs/irib) broadcast his forced confession. He was charged with corruption on earth, sentenced to death by a revolutionary court, and [executed](/events/execution-of-ruhollah-zam-2020) on 12 December 2020. His execution was widely condemned by France, the European Union, and international human rights organisations.

nodes.label: Sadegh Ghotbzadeh
nodes.role: First head of IRIB (1979–1980); Foreign minister (1979–1980)

data.role: First head of IRIB (1979–1980); Foreign minister (1979–1980)
data.bio: Sadegh Ghotbzadeh was a member of the [Freedom Movement of Iran](/orgs/freedom-movement-of-iran) and a close associate of [Ruhollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini) during the exile years in Paris. He played a key role in the [Iranian Revolution](/events/iranian-revolution-1979). He briefly served as the first head of [IRIB](/orgs/irib) in 1979, overseeing the transformation of the Shah-era National Iranian Radio and Television into the revolutionary broadcasting apparatus. He was subsequently appointed foreign minister, serving during the [Iran hostage crisis](/events/iran-hostage-crisis-1979) and the early months of the [Iran–Iraq War](/events/iran-iraq-war). Ghotbzadeh grew disillusioned with the clerical monopoly on power and was arrested in 1982 on charges of plotting a coup against [Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini) with the alleged involvement of [Kazem Shariatmadari](/people/kazem-shariatmadari). He was executed by firing squad on 15 September 1982.

nodes.label: Sadegh Larijani
nodes.role: Chairman of Expediency Discernment Council (2018–present); former Chief Justice (2009–2019); former Guardian Council member (2001–2021)

data.role: Chairman of Expediency Discernment Council (2018–present); former Chief Justice (2009–2019); former Guardian Council member (2001–2021)
data.bio: Sadegh Amoli Larijani is the younger brother of former Majlis Speaker [Ali Larijani](/people/ali-larijani) and one of the most powerful figures in Iranian jurisprudence. He served as Chief Justice for a decade (2009–2019) and simultaneously sat on the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council) from 2001. His tenure as Chief Justice was characterised by mass prosecutions of Green Movement figures, executions of political prisoners and juveniles, and widespread corruption allegations. He was removed from the judiciary in 2019 and replaced by [Ebrahim Raisi](/people/ebrahim-raisi). He resigned from the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council) in September 2021 in a public protest after the Council disqualified his brother [Ali Larijani](/people/ali-larijani) from the presidential race. He was appointed chairman of the [Expediency Discernment Council](/orgs/expediency-discernment-council) by [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) in December 2018, replacing the late Mahmoud Hashemi Shahroudi.

nodes.label: Sadegh Mahsouli
nodes.role: Secretary-General, Front of Islamic Revolution Stability; former Interior Minister (2008–2009)

data.role: Secretary-General, Front of Islamic Revolution Stability; former Interior Minister (2008–2009)
data.bio: Sadegh Mahsouli is a former [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) commander and the organisational kingmaker within the [Front of Islamic Revolution Stability](/orgs/front-of-islamic-revolution-stability), described by Iranian media as the 'Godfather of Paydari'. He became Secretary-General in 2020, replacing [Morteza Agha-Tehrani](/people/morteza-agha-tehrani). A political associate of [Mahmoud Ahmadinejad](/people/mahmoud-ahmadinejad) from their time as officials in Azerbaijan Province, he served as Interior Minister (2008–2009) and Welfare Minister (2009–2010) under Ahmadinejad. He commands significant IRGC-adjacent organisational networks across provincial Iran.

nodes.label: Sadegh Zibakalam
nodes.role: Political scientist, former professor at University of Tehran

data.role: Political scientist, former professor at University of Tehran
data.bio:
Sadegh Zibakalam is an Iranian political scientist and commentator known for publicly challenging the state line on sensitive topics including Iran's nuclear programme, relations with Israel, and political freedoms. He earned his PhD in political science from the [University of Bradford](/orgs/university-of-bradford) in 1989.

Zibakalam served as professor at the [University of Tehran](/orgs/university-of-tehran) and held faculty positions at [Islamic Azad University](/orgs/islamic-azad-university). He played a role in the [Iranian Cultural Revolution](/events/iranian-cultural-revolution-1980) in the early 1980s, for which he later publicly apologised and expressed regret.

He registered as a parliamentary candidate from Zanjan Province in the [2000 legislative election](/events/parliamentary-election-iran-2000) but was disqualified by the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council). In January 2014, he wrote an open letter criticising President [Hassan Rouhani](/people/hassan-rouhani) for failing to fulfil campaign promises, including freeing political prisoners [Mir-Hossein Mousavi](/people/mir-hossein-mousavi), [Zahra Rahnavard](/people/zahra-rahnavard), and [Mehdi Karroubi](/people/mehdi-karroubi). In February 2014, he made headlines for publicly recognising the State of Israel based on its UN membership.

In March 2018, an Islamic Revolutionary Court sentenced him to one year in prison and a two-year ban from political and social media activity for critical remarks in an interview with Deutsche Welle. He received the Deutsche Welle Freedom of Speech Award in 2018.

His notable students include [Saeed Hajjarian](/people/saeed-hajjarian) and [Fatemeh Hashemi Rafsanjani](/people/fatemeh-hashemi-rafsanjani). His bestselling books include *How Did We Become What We Are?* and *An Introduction to Islamic Revolution*. His brother [Saeid Zibakalam](/people/saeid-zibakalam) is also an academic at the University of Tehran, and [Ahmad Tavakkoli](/people/ahmad-tavakkoli) is his co-father-in-law.

nodes.label: Saeed Emami
nodes.role: Deputy Minister of Intelligence; ringleader of chain murders; died in custody 1999

data.role: Deputy Minister of Intelligence; ringleader of chain murders; died in custody 1999
data.bio: Saeed Emami (also known as Saeed Eslami) was a senior official in the [Ministry of Intelligence](/orgs/ministry-of-intelligence) who served as Deputy Minister for Security Affairs under [Ali Fallahian](/people/ali-fallahian). He masterminded and directed the [chain murders](/events/chain-murders-iran-1998) — a decade-long campaign of assassinations targeting Iranian intellectuals, writers, and dissidents from 1988 to 1998. Emami led a unit of operatives who carried out the killings of Dariush and Parvaneh Forouhar, Mohammad Mokhtari, Mohammad Jafar Pouyandeh, Ali Akbar Saidi Sirjani, and dozens of other victims. He also orchestrated the 1996 attempt to kill 21 writers by driving their bus off a cliff at Heyran Pass. He remained an advisor to Intelligence Minister [Qorbanali Dorri-Najafabadi](/people/qorbanali-dorri-najafabadi) at the time of his arrest in early 1999. On 20 June 1999, his death was announced — officially by suicide after drinking depilatory cream on 16 June. The claim was widely disbelieved; Shirin Ebadi stated it was 'impossible' for him to have died that way. No public photographs of his body were ever released, and no grave was registered in his name. He is widely believed to have been murdered to prevent him from revealing who gave the orders for the chain murders.

nodes.label: Saeed Hajjarian
nodes.role: Publisher, Sobh-e Emroz; chief political strategist for Khatami; former deputy Minister of Intelligence

data.role: Publisher, Sobh-e Emroz; chief political strategist for Khatami; former deputy Minister of Intelligence
data.bio: Saeed Hajjarian is the most consequential reformist strategist of the Khatami era and the publisher of the investigative newspaper [Sobh-e Emroz](/orgs/sobh-e-emroz-newspaper). He had an extraordinary trajectory: from revolutionary intelligence officer who helped storm the US Embassy in 1979, to deputy Minister of Intelligence (1984–1989), to reformist political architect who theorised 'pressure from below, negotiation from above' as the strategy for incremental democratisation. When [Mohammad Khatami](/people/mohammad-khatami) was elected president in 1997 Hajjarian became his chief political advisor; he was also elected to Tehran's City Council in 1999. His newspaper [Sobh-e Emroz](/orgs/sobh-e-emroz-newspaper) published [Akbar Ganji](/people/akbar-ganji) and [Emadeddin Baghi](/people/emadeddin-baghi)'s landmark investigations into the [chain murders of 1998](/events/chain-murders-iran-1998), forcing the government to admit Ministry of Intelligence responsibility. On 12 March 2000, he was shot in the face outside the Tehran City Council building by a Basij gunman (Saeed Asgar). The bullet lodged in his neck and left him permanently disabled — largely wheelchair-bound, with severe neurological damage. After the [2009 Green Movement protests](/events/green-movement-protests-2009) he was arrested despite his disability and held in deteriorating health in Evin Prison.

nodes.label: Saeed Jalili
nodes.role: Secretary of the Supreme National Security Council (2007–2013); presidential candidate

data.role: Secretary of the Supreme National Security Council (2007–2013); presidential candidate
data.bio: Saeed Jalili is an Iranian hardline politician who served as secretary of the [Supreme National Security Council](/orgs/supreme-national-security-council) from 2007 to 2013, appointed by [Mahmoud Ahmadinejad](/people/mahmoud-ahmadinejad). He was Iran's chief nuclear negotiator during that period, taking a confrontational stance toward Western powers. He ran unsuccessfully in the 2013 presidential election and was a leading candidate in the [2024 snap presidential election](/events/presidential-election-iran-2024-snap), finishing second to [Masoud Pezeshkian](/people/masoud-pezeshkian) in the runoff. He is closely aligned with the [Front of Islamic Revolution Stability](/orgs/front-of-islamic-revolution-stability) and was endorsed by [Mohammad-Taqi Mesbah-Yazdi](/people/mohammad-taqi-mesbah-yazdi). He lost part of his right leg during the [Iran–Iraq War](/events/iran-iraq-war).

nodes.label: Saeed Khatibzadeh
nodes.role: IPIS Director (2025–present); Former MFA Spokesman (2020–2021)

data.role: IPIS Director (2025–present); Former MFA Spokesman (2020–2021)
data.bio: Saeed Khatibzadeh is an Iranian diplomat who served as spokesman for the [Ministry of Foreign Affairs](/orgs/ministry-of-foreign-affairs) from 2020 to 2021 and was appointed head of the [Institute for Political and International Studies (IPIS)](/orgs/ipis) in January 2025. While stationed in Berlin in 2014, Khatibzadeh conceived and organised the [Iran Experts Initiative](/events/iei-email-leak-2023), a covert Foreign Ministry influence operation that recruited Western-based Iranian academics to promote Tehran's nuclear negotiating positions. In a March 2014 email to IPIS head [Mostafa Zahrani](/people/mostafa-zahrani), he proposed building a network of 'second-generation Iranians' affiliated with Western think tanks and offered them 'political support.' He recruited [Ariane Tabatabai](/people/ariane-tabatabai) and [Dina Esfandiary](/people/dina-esfandiary) as the core group following a meeting in Prague. In April 2015, he reported the IEI's media metrics to [Mohammad Javad Zarif](/people/javad-zarif) and the negotiating team. The operation was exposed when leaked emails were published by Iran International and Semafor in September 2023.

nodes.label: Saeed Mohsen
nodes.role: Co-founder of Mojahedin-e Khalq (MEK)

data.role: Co-founder of Mojahedin-e Khalq (MEK)
data.bio: Saeed Mohsen was an engineer and one of the three co-founders of the [Mojahedin-e Khalq](/orgs/mojahedin-e-khalq), established on 6 September 1965 alongside [Mohammad Hanifnejad](/people/mohammad-hanifnejad) and [Ali-Asghar Badizadegan](/people/ali-asghar-badizadegan). A former member of the Freedom Movement, he helped build the MEK's early organizational structure. He was arrested by SAVAK in 1971 and executed on 25 May 1972.

nodes.label: Saeed Mortazavi
nodes.role: Press Court judge; 'Press Murderer'; later Tehran Prosecutor-General; imprisoned 2018 for Kahrizak deaths

data.role: Press Court judge; 'Press Murderer'; later Tehran Prosecutor-General; imprisoned 2018 for Kahrizak deaths
data.bio: Saeed Mortazavi was the Press Court judge who became the primary instrument of the hardline judicial assault on the reformist press, earning the nickname 'Press Murderer' (ghattale matbooat). Beginning in April–May 2000, following Khamenei's 'enemy bases' speech, Mortazavi issued an order closing 16–21 reformist newspapers overnight — including [Sobh-e Emroz](/orgs/sobh-e-emroz-newspaper), [Mosharekat](/orgs/mosharekat-newspaper), and [Ham-Mihan](/orgs/ham-mihan-newspaper). By February 2004 he had personally overseen the closure or license revocation of approximately 90–120 publications. He was also the judge who closed [Neshat](/orgs/neshat-newspaper) in September 1999. Appointed Tehran Prosecutor-General in May 2003 — the first to hold that post since prosecutors were abolished in 1995 — Mortazavi was involved in the torture and death in custody of Canadian-Iranian photographer Zahra Kazemi in 2003. He was a leading figure in the suppression of the [2009 Green Movement protests](/events/green-movement-protests-2009), signing arrest warrants for hundreds of reformist figures including [Saeed Hajjarian](/people/saeed-hajjarian). In 2018 he was himself imprisoned for his role in the deaths of three detainees at the Kahrizak detention centre during the 2009 crackdown.

nodes.label: Saeid Zibakalam
nodes.role: Academic, faculty member at University of Tehran

data.role: Academic, faculty member at University of Tehran
data.bio:
Saeid Zibakalam is an Iranian academic and conservative political activist. He holds a PhD in philosophy from the University of Leeds (1990), where his dissertation focused on the social epistemology of the Edinburgh School. He is a faculty member at the [University of Tehran](/orgs/university-of-tehran), specialising in philosophy of science and political philosophy.

Zibakalam is a staunch supporter of [Mahmoud Ahmadinejad](/people/mahmoud-ahmadinejad) and has publicly criticised [Hassan Rouhani](/people/hassan-rouhani) and the [JCPOA nuclear deal](/events/jcpoa-nuclear-deal-2015). In 2014, he was summoned to court for criticising nuclear negotiators. He attempted to run in the [2020 legislative election](/events/parliamentary-election-iran-2020) but was disqualified by the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council).

He received Iran's Book of the Year Award in 2007 for his work *Social Epistemology*. His brother [Sadegh Zibakalam](/people/sadegh-zibakalam) is a prominent reformist commentator.

nodes.label: Shapour Bakhtiar
nodes.role: Last Prime Minister of Iran under the Shah (1979); opposition leader in exile

data.role: Last Prime Minister of Iran under the Shah (1979); opposition leader in exile
data.bio: Appointed as Iran's last Prime Minister by [Mohammad Reza Shah Pahlavi](/people/mohammad-reza-shah-pahlavi) in January 1979 in a last-ditch effort to quell the revolution. A member of the [National Front](/orgs/national-front-iran), his acceptance of the position led to his expulsion from the party. He fled to France after the [revolution](/events/iranian-revolution-1979) and was assassinated by Iranian government agents in his home near Paris on 6 August 1991.

nodes.label: Shirin Ebadi
nodes.role: Nobel Peace Prize laureate (2003); human rights lawyer; represented Forouhar family

data.role: Nobel Peace Prize laureate (2003); human rights lawyer; represented Forouhar family
data.bio: Shirin Ebadi is an Iranian lawyer and human rights activist who was awarded the Nobel Peace Prize in 2003 for her efforts to promote democracy and the rights of women and children. She was Iran's first female judge, serving on the Tehran city court from 1975 until she was forced to resign after the 1979 revolution. As a lawyer, she represented the family of [Dariush Forouhar](/people/dariush-forouhar) and Parvaneh Forouhar in the [chain murders](/events/chain-murders-iran-1998) case. During the investigation she discovered that she herself had been on the murder list — her assassination had been postponed until after Ramadan, and the unit was arrested in the interim. She stated unequivocally that Saeed Emami's claimed suicide in prison was impossible. She also represented the family of Canadian-Iranian photographer Zahra Kazemi, who was killed in Evin Prison in 2003. Ebadi left Iran in 2009 and lives in exile in the UK.

nodes.label: Shohreh Aghdashloo
nodes.role: Actress and political activist; vocal supporter of Reza Pahlavi and Iranian opposition

data.role: Actress and political activist; vocal supporter of Reza Pahlavi and Iranian opposition
data.bio:
Shohreh Aghdashloo is an Iranian-American actress and political activist. She received an Academy Award nomination for her role in *House of Sand and Fog* (2003) and has had a long career in both Iranian and Hollywood cinema.

She is a vocal supporter of [Reza Pahlavi](/people/reza-pahlavi) and the Iranian opposition movement, using her public platform to advocate for secular democracy in Iran. During the 2022–2023 Woman, Life, Freedom protests, she was among prominent Iranian-Americans who publicly backed the protest movement.

nodes.label: Siamak Namazi
nodes.role: Former managing director, Atieh Bahar Consulting; former political prisoner

data.role: Former managing director, Atieh Bahar Consulting; former political prisoner
data.bio:
Siamak Namazi is an Iranian-American businessman who managed Atieh Bahar Consulting, a Tehran-based firm founded by his mother Pari Namazi and Bijan Khajehpour that provided business advisory services and access to Iranian regime contacts for foreign enterprises. In 1999, Namazi and [Trita Parsi](/people/trita-parsi) co-authored a paper at a Cyprus conference proposing the creation of an Iranian-American lobby to influence U.S.-Iran policy — the conceptual blueprint for what became [NIAC](/orgs/niac). While Parsi led NIAC, he simultaneously worked as an analyst for Atieh Bahar writing newsletters, a dual role not publicly disclosed.

In October 2015, Namazi was arrested by the IRGC while visiting Iran and sentenced to 10 years in Evin Prison on espionage charges. His father, Baquer Namazi, was also arrested in February 2016. Both were held as de facto hostages and released in September 2023 as part of a U.S.-Iran prisoner exchange deal. Their imprisonment was widely seen as a hostage-taking by IRGC hardliners targeting individuals with Western connections, including those in the pragmatist/reformist network.

nodes.label: Siamak Rahpeyk
nodes.role: Vice Secretary of the Guardian Council; jurist member

data.role: Vice Secretary of the Guardian Council; jurist member
data.bio: Siamak Rahpeyk is a jurist member of the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council) and its Vice Secretary, responsible for overseeing the Council's executive and electoral affairs. Appointed to the position by Secretary [Ahmad Jannati](/people/ahmad-jannati), he has been the operational manager of Iran's candidate-vetting process. He publicly justified the mass disqualification of reformist candidates in the lead-up to the [2020 parliamentary election](/events/parliamentary-election-iran-2020). He was sanctioned by the US Treasury in February 2020 alongside [Ahmad Jannati](/people/ahmad-jannati), [Abbas-Ali Kadkhodaei](/people/abbas-ali-kadkhodaei), and others for denying Iranians free and fair elections.

nodes.label: Trita Parsi
nodes.role: Co-founder & Executive Vice President, Quincy Institute; founder, NIAC

data.role: Co-founder & Executive Vice President, Quincy Institute; founder, NIAC
data.bio:
Trita Parsi is an Iranian-Swedish political scientist who founded the [National Iranian American Council (NIAC)](/orgs/niac) in 2002 and served as its president until approximately 2018. Born in Iran and raised in Sweden, he holds a Ph.D. from Johns Hopkins SAIS. In 1997, he founded Iranians for International Cooperation (IIC) in Sweden, which lobbied U.S. Congress members for sanctions removal. In 1999, he and [Siamak Namazi](/people/siamak-namazi) co-authored a paper at a Cyprus conference proposing the creation of an Iranian-American lobby to influence U.S.-Iran policy — the conceptual foundation for NIAC.

During the 2008 Hassan Dai defamation lawsuit, discovery revealed extensive email exchanges between Parsi and [Javad Zarif](/people/javad-zarif) (then Iran's UN Ambassador) from 2002 to 2007, including arranging meetings between Zarif and members of Congress. In April 2006, Zarif provided Parsi with a copy of the alleged 2003 Iranian 'grand bargain' offer, which Parsi publicized. In August 2013, Zarif told the Iranian parliament that Parsi and NIAC had been his 'main partners' in exploiting American political divisions on Iran policy. While leading NIAC, Parsi simultaneously worked as an analyst for Atieh Bahar Consulting — a Tehran-based firm run by the Namazi family — writing newsletters, a dual role he did not publicly disclose.

In 2019, Parsi co-founded the [Quincy Institute for Responsible Statecraft](/orgs/quincy-institute), where he serves as Executive Vice President. The Hoover Institution's December 2023 report by Abbas Milani placed Parsi and NIAC at the center of a 'diaspora pillar' of Tehran's influence network in the West.

nodes.label: Vahid Haghanian
nodes.role: Executive Deputy of the Office of the Supreme Leader; US-sanctioned member of Khamenei's inner circle

data.role: Executive Deputy of the Office of the Supreme Leader; US-sanctioned member of Khamenei's inner circle
data.bio:
Vahid Haghanian is a senior Iranian security and political operative who served as executive deputy of the [Office of the Supreme Leader](/orgs/office-of-the-supreme-leader) for over three decades. The US Treasury described him as 'the Supreme Leader's right hand.'

Haghanian's relationship with [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) dates to 1979, when he served as Khamenei's personal driver. He joined the [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) in 1984, commanded Tehran security patrols under the Sarallah Corps, and was wounded in the [Iran-Iraq War](/events/iran-iraq-war) in 1987. He subsequently served as a commander in the [IRGC Quds Force](/orgs/irgc-quds-force) for approximately four years, focusing on Eastern Bloc operations.

As Khamenei's chief political fixer, Haghanian relayed the Supreme Leader's directives across military, economic, and political spheres. During the [2009 Green Movement](/events/green-movement-protests-2009), he served as the formal communication channel between [Mir Hossein Mousavi](/people/mir-hossein-mousavi)'s campaign and Khamenei's office. He was reportedly involved in the detention of [Mehdi Karroubi](/people/mehdi-karroubi) in 2011. In the 2021 presidential election, he openly orchestrated candidate withdrawals to favour [Ebrahim Raisi](/people/ebrahim-raisi).

Under Raisi's government, Haghanian held a dual role — simultaneously serving in the president's administration at a rank higher than minister while maintaining his position in Khamenei's office. In June 2024, he registered as a presidential candidate following Raisi's death but was disqualified by the Guardian Council.

The United States sanctioned Haghanian on 4 November 2019 alongside eight other members of Khamenei's inner circle, including [Mojtaba Khamenei](/people/mojtaba-khamenei).

nodes.label: Yadollah Sahabi
nodes.role: Co-founder of Freedom Movement of Iran

data.role: Co-founder of Freedom Movement of Iran
data.bio: Co-founder of the [Freedom Movement of Iran](/orgs/freedom-movement-of-iran) alongside [Mehdi Bazargan](/people/mehdi-bazargan) and [Mahmoud Taleghani](/people/mahmoud-taleghani) in 1961. Served in the post-revolution provisional government and the [Islamic Revolutionary Council](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-council). Father of [Ezzatollah Sahabi](/people/ezzatollah-sahabi).

nodes.label: Yasmine Etemad-Amini
nodes.role: Wife of Reza Pahlavi; Iranian exile community figure

data.role: Wife of Reza Pahlavi; Iranian exile community figure
data.bio:
Yasmine Etemad-Amini is the wife of [Reza Pahlavi](/people/reza-pahlavi), whom she married in 1986. They have three daughters, including [Noor Pahlavi](/people/noor-pahlavi), whom Reza declared heir to the Pahlavi succession in 2019.

She accompanied Reza Pahlavi on his visit to Israel in April 2023, where they met with President Isaac Herzog and Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu. She resides with her family in Potomac, Maryland.

nodes.label: Zahra Rahnavard
nodes.role: Political activist, academic, first female university chancellor post-revolution

data.role: Political activist, academic, first female university chancellor post-revolution
data.bio:
Zahra Rahnavard is an Iranian political activist, academic, and sculptor. She holds a PhD in political science from [Islamic Azad University](/orgs/islamic-azad-university) and degrees in art and architecture from the [University of Tehran](/orgs/university-of-tehran).

She served as chancellor of [Alzahra University](/orgs/alzahra-university) from 1998 to 2006, making her the first woman appointed as a university chancellor in Iran since the 1979 revolution. She also served as political adviser to President [Mohammad Khatami](/people/mohammad-khatami) and headed the Women's Social and Cultural Council.

Rahnavard is the wife of former Prime Minister [Mir-Hossein Mousavi](/people/mir-hossein-mousavi) and was active in his 2009 presidential campaign. Following the [Green Movement protests](/events/green-movement-protests-2009), she was placed under house arrest in February 2011, which reportedly lasted until May 2018.

Foreign Policy magazine named her one of the world's most distinguished thinkers in 2009. She is the author of 15 books and a proponent of Islamic feminism.

nodes.label: Alliance of Builders of Islamic Iran

data.name_en: Alliance of Builders of Islamic Iran
data.description: The Alliance of Builders of Islamic Iran (Abadgaran) was a conservative-principlist political coalition formed in 2003. Described as 'Iran's neocons', it was composed mainly of [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) and [Basij](/orgs/basij) veterans who had been marginalised under [Rafsanjani](/people/akbar-hashemi-rafsanjani) and [Khatami](/people/mohammad-khatami). It swept the 2003 municipal and 2004 parliamentary elections — helped enormously by the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council)'s mass disqualification of reformist candidates — and backed [Mahmoud Ahmadinejad](/people/mahmoud-ahmadinejad)'s successful 2005 presidential run. [Gholam-Ali Haddad-Adel](/people/gholam-ali-haddad-adel), the [Majlis](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly) Speaker from 2004–2008, was a key figure.

Alzahra University is a public women's university in Tehran, Iran. Founded in 1964, it is one of the few single-sex universities in the country. [Zahra Rahnavard](/people/zahra-rahnavard) served as its chancellor from 1998 to 2006, becoming the first woman to head a university in post-revolution Iran.

The Amal Movement (Afwaj al-Muqawama al-Lubnaniyya — Lebanese Resistance Regiments) is a Lebanese Shia political party and militia founded in 1975 by [Musa al-Sadr](/people/musa-al-sadr) and [Mostafa Chamran](/people/mostafa-chamran) as the military wing of the Movement of the Deprived. Chamran served as the movement's military commander, establishing training camps in southern Lebanon with support from Palestinian Fatah. In 1982, a faction led by Husayn al-Musawi split from Amal to form Islamic Amal, which later merged into [Hezbollah](/orgs/hezbollah). The movement remains a major political force in Lebanon, distinct from and at times a rival to Hezbollah.

Ansar-e Hezbollah (Helpers of the Party of God) is an Iranian vigilante paramilitary group with links to hardline factions within the state. Founded in the mid-1990s, the group is known for violently disrupting reformist gatherings, student protests, and intellectual lectures. It was behind a series of physical assaults on religious intellectual [Abdolkarim Soroush](/people/abdolkarim-soroush) and his audiences in the mid-1990s, and was instrumental in attacking student dormitories during the [July 1999 student uprising](/events/iran-student-uprising-july-1999). The group operates with impunity, widely understood to enjoy protection from elements within the security establishment and the [Office of the Supreme Leader](/orgs/office-of-the-supreme-leader).

Asr-e Azadegan (عصر آزادگان, 'Age of the Free People') was the fourth and final iteration of [Mashallah Shamsolvaezin](/people/mashallah-shamsolvaezin)'s serial newspaper chain, assembling staff from all three predecessors. [Akbar Ganji](/people/akbar-ganji) wrote landmark investigative columns on the chain murders of 1998 in its pages. Closed in the April 2000 crackdown. It was succeeded by the weekly *Gunagoun* under editor Fatemeh Farahmandpour — which the Press Court explicitly closed for 'closely resembling Jame'eh, Tous, Neshat, and Asr-e Azadegan.'

nodes.label: Assembly of Experts for the Constitution

data.name_en: Assembly of Experts for the Constitution
data.description: The Assembly of Experts for the Constitution (Majles-e Khobregan-e Qanun-e Asasi) was a 73-member body convened in 1979 to draft the Constitution of the Islamic Republic of Iran. Elected on 3 August 1979, the assembly was dominated by clerics aligned with the [Islamic Republican Party](/orgs/islamic-republican-party), who won approximately 55 of 73 seats. Chaired by [Hossein-Ali Montazeri](/people/hossein-ali-montazeri) with [Mohammad Beheshti](/people/mohammad-beheshti) as deputy speaker, the assembly met from 18 August to 15 November 1979 at the former Senate building in Tehran. Its most consequential act was embedding the doctrine of velayat-e faqih (Guardianship of the Islamic Jurist) into the constitution, granting sweeping powers to a Supreme Leader — a provision that went far beyond the original draft prepared by the provisional government. Opposition members, including figures from the [Freedom Movement of Iran](/orgs/freedom-movement-of-iran) and secular nationalists, were outnumbered and unable to block the clerical majority. The resulting constitution was approved in a national referendum on 2–3 December 1979 with 99.5% approval on 75% turnout, though the referendum was boycotted by leftists, Kurdish groups, and followers of Ayatollah Shariatmadari. This body is distinct from the [Assembly of Experts](/orgs/assembly-of-experts) (Majles-e Khobregan-e Rahbari), the permanent body established in 1982 to select and supervise the Supreme Leader.

The Assembly of Experts (Majles-e Khobregan-e Rahbari) is an 88-member body of Islamic scholars directly elected to eight-year terms by popular vote, with all candidates vetted by the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council). It holds the sole constitutional authority to appoint, supervise, and if necessary dismiss the Supreme Leader. Sessions are held at least twice per year and all records are confidential. It has convened twice to appoint a Supreme Leader: in June 1989 choosing [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) after [Ruhollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini)'s death, and on 8 March 2026 choosing [Mojtaba Khamenei](/people/mojtaba-khamenei). [Ahmad Jannati](/people/ahmad-jannati) served as its chairman from 2016 to 2024.

The Association of Combatant Clerics (Majma'-e Rowhaniyoun-e Mobarez, MRM) broke away from the conservative [Combatant Clergy Association](/orgs/combatant-clergy-association) in 1988. It is the reformist counterpart in clerical politics, associated with the Islamic left and later the reform movement. Key figures include [Mehdi Karroubi](/people/mehdi-karroubi) and [Mohammad Khatami](/people/mohammad-khatami) (the president). It supports political liberalisation within an Islamic framework.

nodes.label: Association of Families of Flight PS752 Victims

data.name_en: Association of Families of Flight PS752 Victims
data.description: Advocacy organisation established by families of the 176 victims of Ukraine International Airlines Flight 752, shot down by the IRGC Aerospace Force on 8 January 2020. The association campaigns for accountability, transparency, and justice through legal action and international advocacy. It has pursued cases in Canadian and international courts and has called for an independent international investigation into the shootdown.

The Atomic Energy Organisation of Iran (AEOI, Sazman-e Enerji-ye Atomi-ye Iran) is the state body overseeing Iran's civil nuclear programme, including uranium enrichment, fuel fabrication, and the Bushehr nuclear power plant. Founded under the Shah in 1974, it continued after the revolution and became the primary institutional interface with the IAEA for inspections and safeguards. Its head is a Vice President of Iran. Key heads include Gholam Reza Aghazadeh (1997–2011), [Ali Akbar Salehi](/people/ali-akbar-salehi) (2009–2013, 2015–2021), and Mohammad Eslami (2021–present). The AEOI administers the enrichment facilities at Natanz and Fordow, the heavy water reactor at Arak, and the uranium conversion facility at Isfahan. It was the formal counterpart in [JCPOA](/events/jcpoa-nuclear-deal-2015) negotiations and continues enriching uranium to 60% purity under the collapsed JCPOA framework. The Amad Project — the clandestine nuclear weapons research programme led by [Mohsen Fakhrizadeh](/people/mohsen-fakhrizadeh) — operated in parallel to and largely hidden from the AEOI's formal structures.

Ayandeh Bank was a private Iranian bank formed in 2012 through the merger of Tat Bank, Salehin Credit Institution, and ATI Credit Institution, orchestrated by founder [Ali Ansari](/people/ali-ansari-banker). Operations began in March 2015 and the bank grew to 270 branches across Iran.

The bank's lending was heavily concentrated in [Iran Mall](/orgs/iran-mall), with approximately 70 percent of its loans directed to entities linked to the mall's development. By 2024, the bank had accumulated $5.1 billion in losses, $2.9 billion in overdrafts from the [Central Bank of Iran](/orgs/central-bank-of-iran), and a capital adequacy ratio of negative 600 percent. Iranian authorities accused the bank of operating as a de facto Ponzi scheme.

On 23 October 2025, the Central Bank of Iran revoked Ayandeh Bank's operating licence and merged its assets and deposits into state-owned [Bank Melli Iran](/orgs/bank-melli-iran). The collapse contributed to broader public protests over Iran's banking sector. The [United Kingdom](/events/uk-sanctions-ali-ansari-2025) sanctioned founder Ali Ansari one week later for alleged [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) financing.

Bank Melli Iran is Iran's first national bank and one of the largest state-owned commercial banks in the country. Founded in 1927, it operates hundreds of branches domestically and maintains an international presence. In October 2025, it absorbed the assets and deposits of the dissolved [Ayandeh Bank](/orgs/ayandeh-bank) on the order of the [Central Bank of Iran](/orgs/central-bank-of-iran).

The Basij Resistance Force (Niroo-ye Moqavemat-e Basij) is a paramilitary volunteer militia subordinate to the [Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps). Founded by [Ruhollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini) in 1979, it played a prominent role in the [Iran-Iraq War](/events/iran-iraq-war) through human-wave assaults. It functions as an internal security and social control force, operating through mosques, universities, and government offices. It was centrally involved in the violent suppression of the [Green Movement](/events/green-movement-protests-2009) in 2009 and the Mahsa Amini protests in 2022.

Bonyan (بنیان, 'Foundation') absorbed journalists displaced by the April 2000 closures. Journalist Ahmad Zeidabadi received a 23-month sentence for an interview in Bonyan — charged as 'insulting the Supreme Leader' for condemning Palestinian suicide bombings. Closed 2002.

The British Institute of Persian Studies (BIPS) promotes scholarship and research on all aspects of Iran and the wider Persianate world — including Afghanistan, Central Asia, and the Caucasus. Founded in 1961 during a British state visit to Iran, it opened a purpose-built institute in northern Tehran in 1976, which remarkably remained open after the 1979 Revolution. Funded by the British Academy, it publishes the journal *IRAN* (since 1963) and awards research and travel grants. [Ali M. Ansari](/people/ali-m-ansari) was elected President in 2023.

The Central Bank of the Islamic Republic of Iran (Bank Markazi) is Iran's central bank and monetary authority, responsible for maintaining monetary and financial stability, regulating the banking sector, and managing foreign exchange reserves. In October 2025, it revoked the operating licence of [Ayandeh Bank](/orgs/ayandeh-bank) and ordered its merger into [Bank Melli Iran](/orgs/bank-melli-iran).

Chatham House, formally the Royal Institute of International Affairs, is a London-based independent policy institute. Founded in 1920, it is one of the world's leading think tanks for international affairs, with extensive programmes on the Middle East and North Africa, including regular analysis of Iranian politics, nuclear negotiations, and sanctions policy. Its Iran-related work has involved numerous scholars and analysts connected to Iranian affairs.

The Combatant Clergy Association (Jame'e-ye Rowhaniyat-e Mobarez, JRM) is a conservative clerical political organisation founded in 1977 by a group of clerics including [Morteza Motahhari](/people/morteza-motahhari). It is one of the oldest and most powerful political bodies in the Islamic Republic, consistently aligning with the conservative and principlist factions. Key affiliated figures include [Ali Larijani](/people/ali-larijani) and [Ali Akbar Nategh-Nouri](/people/ali-akbar-nategh-nouri). It split in 1988 when its left wing broke away to form the [Association of Combatant Clerics](/orgs/association-of-combatant-clerics) (MRM).

nodes.label: Council of Nationalist-Religious Activists of Iran

data.name_en: Council of Nationalist-Religious Activists of Iran
data.description: The Council of Nationalist-Religious Activists (Melli-Mazhabi) is a political organization in Iran established in 2000 by members closely associated with the [Freedom Movement of Iran](/orgs/freedom-movement-of-iran). It carries forward the nationalist-religious political tradition that blends Islamic values with democratic nationalism. The group faced a government crackdown shortly after its founding, with dozens of members arrested and tried.

The Democratic Party of Iranian Kurdistan (PDKI) is a Kurdish political party founded in 1945 in Mahabad. It played a central role in the short-lived Republic of Mahabad (1946) and has been a leading voice for Kurdish autonomy within Iran ever since. After the [Iranian Revolution](/events/iranian-revolution-1979), the party initially participated in the new political process — its leader [Abdul Rahman Ghassemlou](/people/abdul-rahman-ghassemlou) was elected to the [Assembly of Experts for the Constitution](/orgs/assembly-of-experts-for-constitution) in August 1979 with over 80% of the vote in West Azerbaijan — but the Islamic Republic banned the party, rejected Ghassemlou's credentials, and launched a military campaign against Kurdish regions. The party has operated in exile since the early 1980s.

Etemaad (اعتماد, 'Trust') was founded in 2002 and managed by former MP Elias Hazrati, who also co-founded [Mehdi Karroubi](/people/mehdi-karroubi)'s National Trust Party in 2005. Banned after the 2009 Green Movement, it resumed in 2011 and remains one of the few reformist papers still operating.

Ettelaat (اطلاعات, 'Information') is the oldest continuously-published Persian-language daily newspaper in the world, founded in 1926. Under the Shah it was the newspaper of record. On 6 January 1978 it published an article attacking [Ruhollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini) as a British agent — triggering the Qom protests that opened the revolutionary cycle. After the revolution it was seized and placed under the [Office of the Supreme Leader](/orgs/office-of-the-supreme-leader).

The European Council on Foreign Relations (ECFR) is a pan-European think tank focused on foreign and security policy, headquartered in Berlin. [Ellie Geranmayeh](/people/ellie-geranmayeh), a member of the [Iran Experts Initiative](/events/iei-email-leak-2023), served as deputy head of its Middle East and North Africa programme and senior policy fellow.

The Expediency Discernment Council of the System (Majma'-e Tashkhis-e Maslahat-e Nezam) was established by [Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini) in 1988 to resolve legislative disputes between the [Majlis](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly) and [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council). It also acts as an advisory body to the Supreme Leader. Membership is appointed by the Supreme Leader and typically includes former presidents, senior clerics, and military figures, making it a powerful gathering point for Iran's political elite.

Fath (فتح, 'Victory') was a short-lived reformist daily on whose editorial board [Akbar Ganji](/people/akbar-ganji) and [Emadeddin Baghi](/people/emadeddin-baghi) served. Closed in the April 2000 crackdown; both Ganji (22 April) and Baghi (29 May) were arrested for their Fath and [Sobh-e Emroz](/orgs/sobh-e-emroz-newspaper) articles.

The Forqan Group (Goruh-e Forqan) was a small militant Islamist organisation that carried out a series of assassinations in the chaotic months following the 1979 revolution. It assassinated [Morteza Motahhari](/people/morteza-motahhari) on 1 May 1979 and attempted to kill [Akbar Hashemi Rafsanjani](/people/akbar-hashemi-rafsanjani). The group opposed clerical rule, arguing for a return to the Quran without clerical intermediaries. Its members were arrested and executed by 1980.

Founded 17 May 1961 by [Mehdi Bazargan](/people/mehdi-bazargan), [Mahmoud Taleghani](/people/mahmoud-taleghani), and [Yadollah Sahabi](/people/yadollah-sahabi) as a religiously oriented offshoot of the [National Front](/orgs/national-front-iran). Oldest political party still active in Iran. Self-describes as "Muslims, Iranians, Constitutionalists and [Mossadegh](/people/mohammad-mossadegh)ists." Members of the Freedom Movement formed the core of the provisional government after the [1979 revolution](/events/iranian-revolution-1979), with [Bazargan](/people/mehdi-bazargan) as prime minister. Barred from elections since 1980 (with a brief exception in 2003 local elections). Despite being effectively outlawed, continues to exist as a "barely tolerated" opposition force. Splinter groups include the [Mojahedin-e Khalq](/orgs/mojahedin-e-khalq) (1965) and the [Council of Nationalist-Religious Activists](/orgs/council-of-nationalist-religious-activists) (2000).

The Front of Islamic Revolution Stability (Jebhe-ye Paydari-ye Enqelab-e Eslami), known as Paydari or the Steadfast Front, is the most hardline political faction operating within the Islamic Republic's official political space. Founded in 2011 by disciples of [Mohammad-Taqi Mesbah Yazdi](/people/mohammad-taqi-mesbah-yazdi), it advocates absolute velayat-e faqih and the total rejection of Western diplomacy. Key parliamentary figures include [Mahmoud Nabavian](/people/mahmoud-nabavian), [Hamid Rasaee](/people/hamid-rasaee), and [Amir-Hossein Sabeti](/people/amir-hossein-sabeti).
