The front outperformed [Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf](/people/mohammad-bagher-ghalibaf)'s SHANA coalition in Tehran in the 2024 parliamentary election.

The Guardian Council (Shura-ye Negahban) is a 12-member constitutional body with three mandates: vetting legislation for conformity with Islamic law and the constitution; supervising elections; and approving or disqualifying all candidates for parliament, presidency, and [Assembly of Experts](/orgs/assembly-of-experts). Six members are Islamic jurists appointed directly by the Supreme Leader; six are civil lawyers nominated by the Chief Justice (also appointed by the Supreme Leader) and confirmed by parliament. Members serve six-year terms in staggered cohorts.

The Haghani Seminary (Howzeh-ye Elmieh-ye Haqqani) is a hardline Shia seminary in Qom co-founded in the 1960s by [Mohammad-Taqi Mesbah Yazdi](/people/mohammad-taqi-mesbah-yazdi), [Mohammad Beheshti](/people/mohammad-beheshti), and [Ahmad Jannati](/people/ahmad-jannati). Described as 'a kind of École Nationale d'Administration for the Islamic Republic', its graduates form the backbone of the clerical management class in Iran's key political and security institutions. The seminary operates under a strict interpretation of velayat-e faqih and has been the primary incubator of the Haghani Circle ideology.

Ham-Mihan (همیهن, 'Compatriots') was a reformist daily operated by [Gholamhossein Karbaschi](/people/gholamhossein-karbaschi) after he was jailed in 1999 on politically-motivated corruption charges. Closed by Tehran's Press Court on 16 May 2000. Briefly relaunched in 2007.

Hamshahri (همشهری, 'Fellow Citizens') was founded in 1992 by Tehran Mayor [Gholamhossein Karbaschi](/people/gholamhossein-karbaschi) as Iran's first colour daily. Within three years it became Iran's best-selling newspaper at 400,000 copies daily — twice the circulation of [Kayhan](/orgs/kayhan-newspaper) and [Ettelaat](/orgs/ettelaat-newspaper). During the Khatami era it was reformist-aligned. After Karbaschi was jailed in 1999 on politically-motivated charges, Tehran Municipality under conservative control shifted its editorial line. In February 2006 its graphics editor launched the International Holocaust Cartoon Competition, drawing global controversy.
Hayat-e No (حیات نو, 'New Life') emerged from the wreckage of the April 2000 closures and continued through Khatami's second term under repeated suspensions.

Hezbollah (Party of God) is a Lebanese Shia Islamist political party and militant group formally established in 1982 during the Israeli invasion of Lebanon. Its organizational roots trace to the Shia military infrastructure built in Lebanon during the 1970s by [Mostafa Chamran](/people/mostafa-chamran) and [Musa al-Sadr](/people/musa-al-sadr) through the [Amal Movement](/orgs/amal-movement). The formal founding was actively supervised by [Ali Akbar Mohtashamipur](/people/ali-akbar-mohtashamipur), Iran's ambassador to Syria, who worked within the [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) framework to merge existing radical Shia factions — including the Islamic Amal splinter, the Lebanese Dawa Party, and other groups — into a unified organization. It is the most powerful of Iran's regional proxies and receives extensive financial, military, and logistical support from the [IRGC Quds Force](/orgs/irgc-quds-force). It operates as both a political party in Lebanese parliament and a military force with an arsenal that surpasses the Lebanese Armed Forces.

Hussainiyyeh Ershad is a religious and cultural institution in Tehran co-founded by [Morteza Motahhari](/people/morteza-motahhari). It became famous in the 1960s and 1970s as a centre for modernist Islamic lectures, notably by Ali Shariati, whose talks attracted large audiences and contributed to the intellectual groundwork of the Islamic Revolution. It was closed by the Shah's government in 1973 but resumed activity after the revolution.

The Institute for Iranian Studies at the University of St Andrews is one of the leading academic centres for the study of Iran in Europe. Founded in 2006 by [Ali M. Ansari](/people/ali-m-ansari), it provides expertise on Iran from late antiquity to the modern era. The university library holds over 6,000 Persian books. The institute offers an MLitt in Iranian Studies and hosts a regular seminar series featuring researchers from across Britain and Europe.

The International Crisis Group (ICG) is a Brussels-based transnational NGO that conducts field research on violent conflict and advocates for policy solutions. [Robert Malley](/people/robert-malley) served as its president and CEO from 2018 to 2021 before joining the Biden administration as special envoy on Iran. At least two members of the [Iran Experts Initiative](/events/iei-email-leak-2023) — [Ali Vaez](/people/ali-vaez) and [Dina Esfandiary](/people/dina-esfandiary) — were employed by ICG, with Vaez serving as Iran project director. After the [IEI email leak](/events/iei-email-leak-2023), ICG characterised the initiative as 'an informal platform' supported by European governments and institutions.

nodes.label: Institute for Political and International Studies

data.name_en: Institute for Political and International Studies
data.description: The Institute for Political and International Studies (IPIS) is the in-house think tank of Iran's [Ministry of Foreign Affairs](/orgs/ministry-of-foreign-affairs). Founded in 1983, it conducts research on foreign policy, regional affairs, and international relations, and trains diplomats. Under [Mostafa Zahrani](/people/mostafa-zahrani)'s directorship, IPIS oversaw the [Iran Experts Initiative](/events/iei-email-leak-2023), a covert influence operation that recruited Western-based Iranian academics to promote Tehran's nuclear negotiating positions in Western media and policy circles. [Saeed Khatibzadeh](/people/saeed-khatibzadeh), the initiative's mastermind, was appointed head of IPIS in January 2025.

Iran International is a Persian-language television news network launched in 2017, headquartered in London. Founded by Volant Media, the channel quickly became one of the most-watched Persian-language outlets outside Iran, covering Iranian politics, human rights, and diaspora affairs. The Islamic Republic has repeatedly accused the channel of ties to Saudi Arabia and the UK, and Iran's [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) designated it a terrorist organisation. In 2023 the Metropolitan Police warned two Iran International journalists of credible assassination threats from Iranian intelligence, prompting the channel to temporarily relocate operations. In September 2023 Iran International, together with [Semafor](/orgs/semafor), published leaked [Iranian Foreign Ministry](/orgs/ministry-of-foreign-affairs) emails that exposed the [Iran Experts Initiative](/events/iei-email-leak-2023), a covert influence operation targeting Western policy circles.

Iran Mall is the world's largest shopping mall by area, located in northwest Tehran by Chitgar Lake. Conceived by [Ali Ansari](/people/ali-ansari-banker) around 2000, construction began in 2012 and the first phase opened on 1 May 2018 with 267,000 square metres of gross leasable area and 708 retail units.

The project was financed primarily through [Ayandeh Bank](/orgs/ayandeh-bank), which directed approximately 70 percent of its total lending — some 97 thousand billion tomans — to Iran Mall and related entities. The majority of these loans became non-performing. In December 2020, the complex was announced for sale at $3.5 billion. Over 1,200 contractors and 25,000 workers participated in its construction.

The Iran National Council (INC) is an opposition political platform co-founded by [Reza Pahlavi](/people/reza-pahlavi) in 2013 to coordinate Iranian exile opposition activities. It was conceived as a broad-based coalition aiming to unite disparate opposition factions under a shared commitment to secular democracy and regime change in Iran.

The organization was active from 2013 to approximately 2017, when Pahlavi shifted his focus to other vehicles for opposition coordination.

Iran (ایران) is the primary government-owned daily, published since 1995 by the state news agency IRNA. It supports the presidency. English-language edition: *Iran Daily*. In September 2006 it was banned on the same day [Shargh](/orgs/shargh-newspaper) was closed; the Iran ban was quickly lifted while Shargh remained suspended.

nodes.label: Academy of Persian Language and Literature

data.name_en: Academy of Persian Language and Literature
data.description: The Academy of Persian Language and Literature (Farhangestan-e Zaban va Adab-e Farsi) is Iran's official language-regulatory body, tasked with preserving and developing the Persian language. It coins Persian equivalents for foreign terms and oversees linguistic standards. [Gholam-Ali Haddad-Adel](/people/gholam-ali-haddad-adel) has served as its president since 1995.

The Iranian Writers' Association (Kanun-e Nevisandegan-e Iran) is a literary and civil society organisation founded in 1968 to defend freedom of expression and the rights of writers in Iran. In 1994, 134 of its members signed the landmark open letter 'We Are the Writers!' demanding an end to censorship. Several members of its organising committee — including [Mohammad Mokhtari](/people/mohammad-mokhtari), [Mohammad Jafar Pouyandeh](/people/mohammad-jafar-pouyandeh), and [Ali Akbar Saidi Sirjani](/people/ali-akbar-saidi-sirjani) — were murdered as part of the [chain murders](/events/chain-murders-iran-1998). In August 1996, 21 writers travelling to a poetry conference were nearly killed when a [Ministry of Intelligence](/orgs/ministry-of-intelligence) operative attempted to drive their bus off a cliff at Heyran Pass. The association has been repeatedly suppressed by Iranian authorities but continues to operate informally.

The IRGC Aerospace Force (Niruy-e Hava-Faza-ye Sepah) is the branch of the [Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) responsible for Iran's ballistic missile programme, satellite launches, and UAV (drone) operations. Founded in 1985 as the IRGC Air Force, it was expanded from 1989 onward under Supreme Leader [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei). Its role was elevated dramatically by the appointment of [Amir Ali Hajizadeh](/people/amir-ali-hajizadeh) as commander in 2009. The force operates Iran's arsenal of short-, medium-, and long-range ballistic missiles — including the Shahab, Emad, and Fattah-1 hypersonic series — as well as a large UAV fleet including the Shahed loitering munitions. It directed the January 2020 strikes on US bases, the April and October 2024 direct strikes on Israel, and the Strait of Hormuz drone campaign. It was responsible for the accidental shoot-down of Ukrainian Airlines PS752 in January 2020. [Hajizadeh](/people/amir-ali-hajizadeh) was killed in an Israeli airstrike in June 2025; he was succeeded by Hossein Mousavi Eftekhari.

The IRGC Intelligence Organisation (Sazman-e Ettelaat-e Sepah, IRGC-IO) is a parallel intelligence service operating alongside the civilian [Ministry of Intelligence](/orgs/ministry-of-intelligence). Formally restructured in its current form in 2009 following the [Green Movement protests](/events/green-movement-protests-2009), it handles counter-intelligence within the IRGC, surveillance of political dissidents, arrests of dual nationals and foreign journalists, and covert operations abroad. It is distinct from the IRGC Quds Force's external operations wing but overlaps in function. It was headed by [Hossein Taeb](/people/hossein-taeb) from 2009 to 2022, then by General Mohammad Kazemi. The organisation has been sanctioned by the US, EU, and UK for its role in arresting and torturing activists during the 2009, 2019, and 2022 protest waves.

The Quds Force (Jerusalem Force) is the [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) branch responsible for extraterritorial operations, unconventional warfare, and support to non-state allies. It reports directly to the Supreme Leader. The Quds Force supports [Hezbollah](/orgs/hezbollah) in Lebanon, Hamas and Palestinian Islamic Jihad in Gaza, the Houthis in Yemen, and Shia militias in Iraq and Syria. It has been described by the US military as analogous to a combination of the CIA and JSOC.

IRIB (Seda va Sima) holds a constitutional monopoly on all radio and television broadcasting in Iran under Article 175 of the constitution, which grants the [Supreme Leader](/orgs/office-of-the-supreme-leader) sole authority to appoint and dismiss its director. Founded in 1979 after the [revolution](/events/iranian-revolution-1979) replaced the Shah-era National Iranian Radio and Television (NIRT), its first head was [Sadegh Ghotbzadeh](/people/sadegh-ghotbzadeh), followed by [Mohammad Hashemi](/people/mohammad-hashemi) (1980–1994), who ran IRIB through the [Iran–Iraq War](/events/iran-iraq-war) and expanded it from two channels to a multi-channel network. [Ali Larijani](/people/ali-larijani) (1994–2004) was appointed as a hardline counterweight to the reformist press during the Khatami era. [Ezzatollah Zarghami](/people/ezzatollah-zarghami) (2004–2014) oversaw coverage of the disputed [2009 election](/events/presidential-election-iran-2009) and the [Green Movement](/events/green-movement-protests-2009), during which IRIB declared [Ahmadinejad](/people/mahmoud-ahmadinejad) the winner before official certification and ignored mass protests. [Mohammad Sarafraz](/people/mohammad-sarafraz) (2014–2016), [Abdolali Ali-Asgari](/people/abdolali-ali-asgari) (2016–2021), and [Peyman Jebelli](/people/peyman-jebelli) (2021–present) followed. IRIB operates dozens of domestic television and radio channels, provincial stations, and international outlets including [Press TV](/orgs/press-tv) (English), Al-Alam (Arabic), HispanTV (Spanish), and iFilm. It also publishes the daily newspaper Jam-e Jam. IRIB has systematically broadcast forced confessions of political prisoners, journalists, and activists — a practice documented in over 500 cases by human rights organisations. The European Union sanctioned IRIB and director Zarghami in 2012, and Ofcom revoked Press TV's UK licence the same year for airing Newsweek journalist Maziar Bahari's coerced confession. Fresh EU sanctions were imposed in October 2022 over IRIB's role during the [Mahsa Amini protests](/events/mahsa-amini-protests-2022). Dissident journalist [Ruhollah Zam](/people/ruhollah-zam), whose forced confession was broadcast on IRIB, was [executed](/events/execution-of-ruhollah-zam-2020) in December 2020.

Islamic Azad University is the largest university system in Iran, with branches across the country. It was founded in 1982 by [Akbar Hashemi Rafsanjani](/people/akbar-hashemi-rafsanjani) and operates as a private, non-profit institution. It has played a significant role in expanding higher education access in Iran.

The Islamic Consultative Assembly (Majles-e Shura-ye Eslami), commonly known as the Majlis or Majles, is the unicameral legislature of the Islamic Republic of Iran. It currently has 290 seats, elected for four-year terms. All candidates must be vetted and approved by the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council). The Majlis can legislate, ratify treaties, and impeach the president, but all legislation must pass Guardian Council review for conformity with Islamic law and the constitution.

The Islamic Iran Participation Front (Jebhe-ye Mosharekat-e Iran-e Eslami), abbreviated IIPF, was the largest and most prominent reformist political party in post-revolutionary Iran. Founded in 1998 by [Mohammad-Reza Khatami](/people/mohammad-reza-khatami), it was the main vehicle for the reformist 2nd Khordad movement that swept the 6th [Majlis](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly) in 2000. The [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council) barred all IIPF candidates from running in the 2004 parliamentary election, effectively eliminating the party from parliament. It was formally suspended by the government in 2010 following the [Green Movement](/events/green-movement-protests-2009) protests. The party advocated political pluralism, civil society, rule of law, and engagement with the West.

The Islamic Republican Party (Hezb-e Jomhuri-ye Eslami, IRP) was the dominant political party of the early Islamic Republic, co-founded in 1979 by [Ruhollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini)'s inner circle including [Mohammad Beheshti](/people/mohammad-beheshti), [Akbar Hashemi Rafsanjani](/people/akbar-hashemi-rafsanjani), [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei), [Mohammad-Javad Bahonar](/people/mohammad-javad-bahonar), and [Abdolkarim Mousavi-Ardabili](/people/abdolkarim-mousavi-ardabili). It dominated the first three [Majlis](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly) sessions and the executive branch until its dissolution in 1987 by Khomeini's order, following irreconcilable internal factional disputes. Its dissolution led directly to the formation of the rival [Combatant Clergy Association](/orgs/combatant-clergy-association) and [Association of Combatant Clerics](/orgs/association-of-combatant-clerics) as successor factions.

The Islamic Revolutionary Council was a governing body established by [Ruhollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini) in January 1979 to oversee the transition of power during the [Iranian Revolution](/events/iranian-revolution-1979). Its members included [Mehdi Bazargan](/people/mehdi-bazargan), [Yadollah Sahabi](/people/yadollah-sahabi), [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei), [Abolhassan Banisadr](/people/abolhassan-banisadr), and others. It operated in parallel with Bazargan's provisional government and was dissolved in 1980 after the first Majlis was elected.

The Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC), known domestically as Sepah or Pasdaran, is a multi-service elite military force established by [Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini) in May 1979. It consists of five branches: Ground Forces, Aerospace Force, Navy, [Quds Force](/orgs/irgc-quds-force), and [Basij](/orgs/basij). Its commander-in-chief reports directly to the Supreme Leader. Beyond its military role, the IRGC controls a vast economic empire including Khatam al-Anbiya Construction, multiple banks, and extensive holdings in energy, telecommunications, and construction. Estimated at 125,000 personnel.

Jame'eh (جامعه, 'Society') was the first of the 'serial newspapers' — the chain of reformist dailies run by [Mashallah Shamsolvaezin](/people/mashallah-shamsolvaezin) and co-directed by [Hamid Reza Jalalipour](/people/hamid-reza-jalalipour) that were successively closed and immediately relaunched under new names. Founded in early 1998, it ran a headline quoting Khatami's challenge to the IRGC: 'Society cannot be moved forward by instilling fear.' After suspension and permanent closure, Shamsolvaezin and Jalalipour were jailed 35 days without charge. Upon release, Shamsolvaezin immediately relaunched as [Tous](/orgs/tous-newspaper).

Jomhouri-ye Eslami (جمهوری اسلامی) was founded on 30 May 1979 as the organ of the [Islamic Republican Party](/orgs/islamic-republican-party). Managing director: [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei). First editor-in-chief: [Mir-Hossein Mousavi](/people/mir-hossein-mousavi). The paper continued after the IRP's dissolution in 1987 and is classified as close to [Rafsanjani](/people/akbar-hashemi-rafsanjani)'s circle by the 2010s.

The Judiciary of Iran (Qovveh-ye Qaza'iyeh) is the judicial branch of the Islamic Republic, headed by a Chief Justice appointed directly by the [Office of the Supreme Leader](/orgs/office-of-the-supreme-leader) for five-year terms. The Chief Justice also nominates the six jurist members of the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council). The Judiciary has been a primary instrument of political repression, overseeing prosecutions of reformists, journalists, and human rights defenders. Chief Justices since 1989 include [Akbar Hashemi Rafsanjani](/people/akbar-hashemi-rafsanjani) (briefly), Mohammad Yazdi (1989–1999), Mahmoud Hashemi Shahroudi (1999–2009), [Sadegh Amoli Larijani](/people/sadegh-larijani) (2009–2019), [Ebrahim Raisi](/people/ebrahim-raisi) (2019–2021), and [Gholam-Hossein Mohseni-Ejei](/people/gholam-hossein-mohseni-ejei) (2021–present).

Kayhan (کیهان, 'Universe') is Iran's most prominent hardline daily newspaper, founded in 1941 as a private paper. After the revolution its owner Mostafa Mesbahzadeh fled abroad and the paper was seized and placed directly under the [Office of the Supreme Leader](/orgs/office-of-the-supreme-leader). Since 1993 the managing editor has been [Hossein Shariatmadari](/people/hossein-shariatmadari), a Khamenei appointee and former MOIS interrogator who is regarded as the Supreme Leader's direct mouthpiece in the press. Kayhan regularly calls for the prosecution of reformist politicians and journalists, denies the Holocaust, and attacks critical media. In the early 1990s it published *Kayhan-e Farhangi*, an intellectual magazine hosting Abdolkarim Soroush's circle, until Khatami's 1992 resignation from the Culture Ministry forced that group out to found [Kian](/orgs/kian-magazine).

Khatam al-Anbiya Construction Headquarters is the [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps)'s engineering and construction arm and one of Iran's largest conglomerates. Originally formed to rebuild infrastructure after the [Iran-Iraq War](/events/iran-iraq-war), it grew into a sprawling enterprise with interests in oil and gas, transportation, water management, and construction. It has been subject to US and EU sanctions for its role in Iran's nuclear and missile programmes and its ties to the IRGC.

Khordad (خرداد) was the flagship newspaper of the '2nd of Khordad' reformist movement, named for the date of Khatami's election. Published by the Islamic Iran Participation Front, editor-in-chief [Abbas Abdi](/people/abbas-abdi). Closed in 1999 as one of the most politically explicit press suppressions of the era.

Kian (کیان) was the most influential intellectual magazine of the pre-Khatami reform era, founded in November 1991 by publisher Mostafa Rokh-Sefat with [Mashallah Shamsolvaezin](/people/mashallah-shamsolvaezin) as editor. Its circle (*halgheh-ye Kian*) included Abdolkarim Soroush, [Saeed Hajjarian](/people/saeed-hajjarian), [Akbar Ganji](/people/akbar-ganji), and others who became the intellectual architects of the reform movement. Kian emerged when Khatami's 1992 resignation from the Culture Ministry forced Soroush's circle out of *Kayhan-e Farhangi*. Closed by judicial order in 1998.

The Law Enforcement Command of Iran (FARAJA, formerly NAJA) is the national police force of the Islamic Republic. Subordinate to the [Ministry of Interior](/orgs/ministry-of-interior), it is responsible for public security, border control, and crowd control. Under Commander-in-Chief [Ahmad-Reza Radan](/people/ahmad-reza-radan), police forces participated in the violent suppression of the [2025-2026 protests](/events/january-2026-massacres), using live ammunition and military-grade weapons against civilian demonstrators.

nodes.label: Ministry of Defense and Armed Forces Logistics

data.name_en: Ministry of Defense and Armed Forces Logistics
data.name_fa: وزارت دفاع و پشتیبانی نیروهای مسلح
data.description: Iran's Defense Ministry (Vezarat-e Defa va Poshtibani-ye Niruhaye Mosallah) manages defense procurement, arms production, and logistics for Iran's armed forces. It does not command operational forces; that function resides with the General Staff and IRGC command. Key ministers: [Ali Shamkhani](/people/ali-shamkhani) (1997–2005), [Mostafa Mohammad-Najjar](/people/mostafa-mohammad-najjar) (2005–2009), [Ahmad Vahidi](/people/ahmad-vahidi) (2009–2013), [Hossein Dehghan](/people/hossein-dehghan) (2013–2017), Amir Hatami (2017–2021), [Mohammad Reza Ashtiani](/people/mohammad-reza-ashtiani) (2021–2024), [Aziz Nasirzadeh](/people/aziz-nasirzadeh) (2024–present).

nodes.label: Ministry of Economic Affairs and Finance

data.name_en: Ministry of Economic Affairs and Finance
data.description: Iran's Ministry of Economic Affairs and Finance (Vezarat-e Omur-e Eqtesadi va Daraii) manages the state budget, tax policy, financial regulation, and oversees state-owned enterprises. Key ministers: Ali Tayyebnia (2013–2017), Masoud Karbasian (2017–2018), Farhad Dejpasand (2018–2021), Ehsan Khandouzi (2021–2024), [Abdolnaser Hemmati](/people/abdolnaser-hemmati) (2024, briefly).

Iran's Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Vezarat-e Omur-e Khareje) is the executive body responsible for conducting the country's foreign policy, managing diplomatic missions, and negotiating international agreements. In practice, strategic foreign policy is set by the [Office of the Supreme Leader](/orgs/office-of-the-supreme-leader) and coordinated through the [Supreme National Security Council](/orgs/supreme-national-security-council), with the Ministry implementing day-to-day diplomacy. Foreign Ministers since 1981: [Ali Akbar Velayati](/people/ali-akbar-velayati) (1981–1997), [Kamal Kharrazi](/people/kamal-kharrazi) (1997–2005), [Manouchehr Mottaki](/people/manouchehr-mottaki) (2005–2010), [Ali Akbar Salehi](/people/ali-akbar-salehi) (2010–2013), [Mohammad Javad Zarif](/people/javad-zarif) (2013–2021), [Hossein Amir-Abdollahian](/people/hossein-amir-abdollahian) (2021–2024), [Abbas Araghchi](/people/abbas-araghchi) (2024–present).

The Ministry of Intelligence (Vezarat-e Ettelaat, VAJA / formerly MOIS) is Iran's primary civilian intelligence service, established in 1983 from several post-revolution intelligence bodies. The Minister of Intelligence must be a qualified Islamic jurist (mojtahed). The Supreme Leader has historically exercised direct influence over this appointment. Ministers: Mohammad Reyshahri (1984–1989), [Ali Fallahian](/people/ali-fallahian) (1989–1997), [Qorbanali Dorri-Najafabadi](/people/qorbanali-dorri-najafabadi) (1997–1999), Ali Younessi (1999–2005), [Gholam-Hossein Mohseni-Ejei](/people/gholam-hossein-mohseni-ejei) (2005–2009), [Heydar Moslehi](/people/heydar-moslehi) (2009–2013), [Mahmoud Alavi](/people/mahmoud-alavi) (2013–2021), [Esmaeil Khatib](/people/esmaeil-khatib) (2021–present).

Iran's Ministry of Interior (Vezarat-e Keshvar) oversees provincial governance, domestic security coordination, civil registration, and the administration of elections. As the agency that supervises and certifies election results, it wields enormous political power in conjunction with the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council). Key ministers: Ali Akbar Natiq-Nouri (1981–1985), Mohammad Reyshahri briefly (1989), [Ali Fallahian](/people/ali-fallahian) briefly, Abdollah Nouri (1989–1993, 1997–1998), [Mostafa Pourmohammadi](/people/mostafa-pourmohammadi) (2005–2008), [Sadegh Mahsouli](/people/sadegh-mahsouli) (2008–2009), Mostafa Mohammad-Najjar (2009–2013), Abdolreza Rahmani Fazli (2013–2017), [Ahmad Vahidi](/people/ahmad-vahidi) (2021–2024), [Eskandar Momeni](/people/eskandar-momeni) (2024–present).

Iran's Ministry of Justice (Vezarat-e Dadgostari) manages the administrative interface between the executive and judiciary. The minister does not head the Judiciary — the Chief Justice is separately appointed by the Supreme Leader — but administers courts, prisons, and judicial coordination. Key ministers: [Mostafa Pourmohammadi](/people/mostafa-pourmohammadi) (2013–2017, implicated in 1988 massacre as deputy minister), Alireza Avaei (2017–2021, sanctioned for executions), [Amin Hossein Rahimi](/people/amin-hossein-rahimi) (2021–2024), Amin Hossein Rahimi (2024–present, reappointed by Pezeshkian).

Iran's Ministry of Petroleum (Vezarat-e Naft) manages the National Iranian Oil Company (NIOC) and oversees all upstream and downstream oil, gas, and petrochemical activities. Oil revenue has historically funded the majority of the state budget. Key ministers: Gholam Reza Aghazadeh (1985–1997), [Bijan Namdar Zanganeh](/people/bijan-namdar-zanganeh) (1997–2005, 2013–2021), Kazem Vaziri Hamaneh (2005–2008), Gholamhossein Nozari (2008–2009), Masoud Mir-Kazemi (2009–2011), [Rostam Ghasemi](/people/rostam-ghasemi) (2011–2013), [Javad Owji](/people/javad-owji) (2021–2024), [Mohsen Paknejad](/people/mohsen-paknejad) (2024–present).

The Moderation and Development Party is an Iranian centrist political party associated with former President [Hassan Rouhani](/people/hassan-rouhani). Founded in 1999, it advocates pragmatist economic and political reform within the framework of the Islamic Republic.

The Mojahedin-e Khalq (MEK), also known as the People's Mojahedin Organization of Iran (PMOI), originated as a splinter group from the [Freedom Movement of Iran](/orgs/freedom-movement-of-iran). It was co-founded in 1965 by [Mohammad Hanifnejad](/people/mohammad-hanifnejad), [Saeed Mohsen](/people/saeed-mohsen), and [Ali-Asghar Badizadegan](/people/ali-asghar-badizadegan), blending progressive Islam with anti-imperialist politics. After the three founders were executed by the Shah in 1972, [Massoud Rajavi](/people/massoud-rajavi) emerged as leader upon his release in 1979. The MEK participated in the revolution but clashed with [Ruhollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini)'s regime, organizing the [June 20 uprising](/events/june-20-uprising-1981) and carrying out the 1981 bombing that killed President [Mohammad-Ali Rajai](/people/mohammad-ali-rajai) and Prime Minister [Mohammad-Javad Bahonar](/people/mohammad-javad-bahonar). After a brutal crackdown, the MEK relocated to Iraq under Saddam Hussein's patronage and founded the [National Liberation Army](/orgs/national-liberation-army-of-iran), whose catastrophic [Operation Forough-e Javidan](/events/operation-forough-e-javidan-1988) in 1988 provided a pretext for the [mass executions](/events/mass-executions-1988) of thousands of prisoners. The [NCRI](/orgs/national-council-of-resistance-of-iran), led by [Maryam Rajavi](/people/maryam-rajavi), serves as its political umbrella. The MEK's intelligence network [revealed Iran's secret Natanz nuclear facility](/events/natanz-nuclear-revelation-2002) in 2002. Now based in Albania, it was removed from the US terrorist list in 2012.

nodes.label: Mojahedin of the Islamic Revolution Organization

data.name_en: Mojahedin of the Islamic Revolution Organization
data.description: The Mojahedin of the Islamic Revolution Organization (OMIIR) is a reformist party reconstituted in 1991 from the left wing of the original 1979 organisation of the same name. It is closely allied with the [Islamic Iran Participation Front](/orgs/islamic-iran-participation-front) within the reform movement. Key figures include [Behzad Nabavi](/people/behzad-nabavi), [Mohsen Armin](/people/mohsen-armin), and [Mostafa Tajzadeh](/people/mostafa-tajzadeh). The party emphasised the republican dimensions of the Islamic Republic over its theocratic elements. Several members were arrested and imprisoned after the 2009 [Green Movement](/events/green-movement-protests-2009) protests.

Mosharekat (مشارکت, 'Participation') was the newspaper of the Islamic Iran Participation Front — the party of Mohammad Reza Khatami, brother of President Khatami. Closed without stated justification on 27 April 2000, simultaneously with [Sobh-e Emroz](/orgs/sobh-e-emroz-newspaper), as part of the mass April 2000 crackdown following the reformists' parliamentary election landslide.

The Nation of Iran Party (Hezb-e Mellat-e Iran) is one of the oldest nationalist-democratic political parties in Iran, founded in 1951 during the oil nationalisation movement. Led for decades by [Dariush Forouhar](/people/dariush-forouhar), it advocated for secular democracy, free elections, and the rule of law. Forouhar and his wife [Parvaneh Forouhar](/people/parvaneh-forouhar) were assassinated in their home on 22 November 1998 as part of the [chain murders](/events/chain-murders-iran-1998). The party has been effectively banned since the early Islamic Republic but maintained an informal presence through the Forouhars' political salon.

nodes.label: National Council of Resistance of Iran

data.name_en: National Council of Resistance of Iran
data.description: The NCRI was co-founded by [Massoud Rajavi](/people/massoud-rajavi) and President [Abolhassan Banisadr](/people/abolhassan-banisadr) on 21 July 1981 as a broad coalition opposing the Islamic Republic. It functions as a parliament-in-exile comprising approximately 540 members, with the [Mojahedin-e Khalq](/orgs/mojahedin-e-khalq) as its principal member organization. [Maryam Rajavi](/people/maryam-rajavi) has served as its President-elect since 1993. Banisadr left the coalition in 1984 over disagreements with Rajavi's leadership. The NCRI is headquartered at Auvers-sur-Oise near Paris and advocates for the overthrow of the Islamic Republic and establishment of a secular democratic government.

Coalition of nationalist political parties founded by [Mohammad Mossadegh](/people/mohammad-mossadegh) in 1949. Led the oil nationalization movement and supported [Mossadegh](/people/mohammad-mossadegh)'s government (1951–1953). Reconstituted as the Second National Front (1960) and Third National Front (1964). The [Freedom Movement of Iran](/orgs/freedom-movement-of-iran) split from it in 1961. Members including [Karim Sanjabi](/people/karim-sanjabi) served in the post-revolution provisional government, but the organization was marginalized by the Islamic Republic.

The National Liberation Army of Iran (NLA) was the armed wing of the [Mojahedin-e Khalq](/orgs/mojahedin-e-khalq), founded in 1987 in Iraq with Saddam Hussein's support. It fielded approximately 7,000 fighters equipped with Iraqi-supplied weapons and armored vehicles. Its most significant operation was [Operation Forough-e Javidan](/events/operation-forough-e-javidan-1988) on 26 July 1988, a failed offensive into western Iran that was repelled by Iranian forces with devastating losses. The NLA was effectively disbanded after the 2003 US invasion of Iraq when its members were disarmed by American forces.

The National Trust Party (Hezb-e E'temad-e Melli) was founded in 2005 by [Mehdi Karroubi](/people/mehdi-karroubi) after he came third in the first round of the presidential election that year. It positioned itself as a reformist but pragmatic party advocating social freedoms, economic reform, and political pluralism. Karroubi ran for president again under the party's banner in 2009. After the disputed election result and the [Green Movement](/events/green-movement-protests-2009), the party's leadership was suppressed and Karroubi himself was placed under house arrest.

Neshat (نشاط, 'Joy/Happiness') was the third iteration of [Mashallah Shamsolvaezin](/people/mashallah-shamsolvaezin)'s serial chain, built on the dormant *Akhbar* license. Staff from [Tous](/orgs/tous-newspaper) and [Jame'eh](/orgs/jameeh-newspaper) formed the core. In September 1999 Press Court Judge [Saeed Mortazavi](/people/saeed-mortazavi) closed it after it published an open letter urging Khamenei to distance himself from hardliners, and [Emadeddin Baghi](/people/emadeddin-baghi)'s article criticising capital punishment. Shamsolvaezin was sentenced to 30 months — but founded [Asr-e Azadegan](/orgs/asr-e-azadegan-newspaper) before he could be imprisoned.

NIAC Action is the 501(c)(4) lobbying arm of the [National Iranian American Council (NIAC)](/orgs/niac), created in June 2015 to engage in direct political lobbying during the push for the [JCPOA nuclear deal](/events/jcpoa-nuclear-deal-2015). Led by [Jamal Abdi](/people/jamal-abdi), it conducts grassroots advocacy, maintains local chapters, and engages political candidates on Iran-related policy. Its creation formalized lobbying activities that NIAC had already been conducting through its 501(c)(3) — activities that were the subject of the Hassan Dai lawsuit controversy.

The National Iranian American Council (NIAC) is a Washington D.C.-based 501(c)(3) nonprofit founded in 2002 by [Trita Parsi](/people/trita-parsi). It describes itself as promoting the civic participation of Iranian Americans and diplomacy between the U.S. and Iran. Critics — including the Hoover Institution, members of Congress, and Iranian diaspora activists — allege it functions as a de facto lobby for the Iranian regime. During a 2008 defamation lawsuit filed by NIAC against journalist Hassan Dai, discovery revealed extensive email communications between Parsi and [Javad Zarif](/people/javad-zarif) (then Iran's UN Ambassador), including arranging meetings between Zarif and U.S. Congress members. In August 2013, Zarif told the Iranian parliament that Parsi and NIAC had been his 'main partners' in a campaign to exploit American political divisions on Iran policy. Internal NIAC documents showed employees referring to their work as 'lobbying,' and an employee was found to have altered 130 files changing 'lobbying' to 'legislative direct.' The case was dismissed in 2012, and NIAC was sanctioned $183,480 for discovery abuses in 2015. In 2020, Senators Cotton, Braun, and Cruz wrote to the Attorney General urging a FARA investigation of NIAC. NIAC created a 501(c)(4) sister organization, [NIAC Action](/orgs/niac-action), in 2015 to lobby for the [JCPOA nuclear deal](/events/jcpoa-nuclear-deal-2015). After leaving NIAC, Parsi co-founded the [Quincy Institute for Responsible Statecraft](/orgs/quincy-institute).

Norooz (نوروز, 'New Year') was a reformist daily of the Executives of Construction Party (Hezb-e Kargozaran-e Sazandegi), a technocratic reformist party linked to [Rafsanjani](/people/akbar-hashemi-rafsanjani). Closed 2003.

The Office of Prime Minister was the head of government position in the Islamic Republic from 1979 to 1989. Under the 1979 constitution, executive power was divided between the president and the prime minister. The role was abolished in the 1989 constitutional revision, which merged its powers into the presidency. [Mir-Hossein Mousavi](/people/mir-hossein-mousavi) was the last holder of the office.

The Presidency of the Islamic Republic of Iran is the second-highest office in the political system, below the Supreme Leader. The president is elected by popular vote for four-year terms, with a two-term consecutive limit. The president chairs the cabinet and is responsible for implementing the constitution. The post of prime minister was abolished in the 1989 constitutional revision, merging its powers into the presidency.

The Office of the Supreme Leader (Daftar-e Maqam-e Mo'azzam-e Rahbari) is the institutional apparatus of the Supreme Leader of Iran. The Supreme Leader holds ultimate authority over all branches of government, the armed forces, the judiciary, and state media under the doctrine of Velayat-e Faqih. The office encompasses numerous subsidiary bodies including the Supreme Leader's Inspection Office, his representatives in the provinces, and his representatives to the [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) and other military branches.

The Phoenix Project of Iran is a think tank launched by [Reza Pahlavi](/people/reza-pahlavi) in Washington, D.C. in February 2019. It focuses on post-regime transition planning for Iran, developing policy frameworks and governance proposals for a future secular democratic Iranian state.

The project has produced an Emergency Phase Booklet covering the first six months of a post-Islamic Republic transition, as well as the Iran Prosperity Project for long-term economic planning.

The Popular Mobilization Forces (PMF, or Hashd al-Sha'abi) is an Iraqi state-sponsored umbrella organisation of predominantly Shia paramilitary groups, many of which receive training, funding, and direction from Iran's [IRGC Quds Force](/orgs/irgc-quds-force). Its deputy commander Abu Mahdi al-Muhandis was killed alongside [Qasem Soleimani](/people/qasem-soleimani) in the January 2020 US drone strike at Baghdad International Airport.

Press TV is [IRIB](/orgs/irib)'s English-language 24-hour news channel, launched in 2007 to project Iran's perspective to international audiences. Under its former head [Mohammad Sarafraz](/people/mohammad-sarafraz), the channel broadcast forced confessions of political prisoners including Newsweek journalist Maziar Bahari, who was detained for 118 days in 2009 and coerced into a televised confession filmed inside Evin Prison. In January 2012 the UK media regulator Ofcom revoked Press TV's broadcasting licence for airing Bahari's confession without his consent. The channel was subsequently dropped from European satellite providers under EU sanctions. Press TV's website domain was among Iranian media domains seized by the US Department of Justice in 2021. The channel continues to broadcast via the internet and non-Western satellites.

The Quincy Institute for Responsible Statecraft is a Washington D.C.-based think tank co-founded in 2019 by [Trita Parsi](/people/trita-parsi) and Andrew Bacevich, advocating diplomatic engagement and military restraint in U.S. foreign policy. Parsi serves as Executive Vice President. The institute received initial funding from both the Charles Koch Foundation and George Soros's Open Society Foundations. Former [NIAC](/orgs/niac) staff, including Adam Weinstein, have moved to the Quincy Institute. Critics view it as a continuation of Parsi's advocacy for U.S.-Iran engagement under a broader anti-interventionist umbrella.

Resalat (رسالت, 'Mission') was founded in 1985 as the organ of traditional conservative Islamic politics, closely tied to the Tehran Bazaar and the Islamic Coalition Party (Hezb-e Mo'talefeh). Alongside [Kayhan](/orgs/kayhan-newspaper), it is one of the two leading hardline-conservative dailies but is editorially independent of direct Supreme Leader control.

The Royal United Services Institute (RUSI) is the world's oldest independent think tank on international defence and security. Based in London, it conducts research on defence, security, and foreign policy, including analysis of Iran's military capabilities, IRGC operations, sanctions enforcement, and regional security dynamics. [Ali M. Ansari](/people/ali-m-ansari) serves as a Senior Associate Fellow.

Salam (سلام, 'Hello/Peace') was a highly influential reformist daily published from 9 February 1991 to 7 July 1999. Its name was given by Ahmad Khomeini, son of the Supreme Leader. Publisher was reformist cleric Mohammad Mousavi Khoeiniha; editor-in-chief was [Abbas Abdi](/people/abbas-abdi). Salam built reformist momentum ahead of Khatami's 1997 election. Its closure on 7 July 1999 — after publishing a leaked MOIS document about plans to restrict the press — directly ignited the [July 1999 student uprising](/events/iran-student-uprising-july-1999), the largest protests in Iran since 1979. At least four students were killed when security forces attacked Tehran University dormitories.

SAVAK (Sāzemān-e Ettelā'āt va Amniyat-e Keshvar — Organization of Intelligence and National Security) was the secret police, domestic security, and intelligence service of Iran under [Mohammad Reza Shah Pahlavi](/people/mohammad-reza-shah-pahlavi). Established in 1957 with CIA and Mossad assistance, it suppressed political opposition including the [National Front](/orgs/national-front-iran), the [Freedom Movement of Iran](/orgs/freedom-movement-of-iran), leftist organizations, and Islamist groups. Known for systematic use of torture, it imprisoned thousands of political dissidents. Dissolved after the [1979 revolution](/events/iranian-revolution-1979).

Semafor is a global news platform launched in October 2022 by former BuzzFeed News editor-in-chief Ben Smith and former Bloomberg Media CEO Justin B. Smith. In September 2023, Semafor journalist Jay Solomon published a landmark investigation, jointly with [Iran International](/orgs/iran-international), that revealed the [Iran Experts Initiative](/events/iei-email-leak-2023) — a covert Iranian [Foreign Ministry](/orgs/ministry-of-foreign-affairs) influence operation in which Western-based academics were recruited to promote Tehran's nuclear negotiating positions in Western media and policy circles.

Shargh (شرق, 'East') is the most durable reformist daily of the post-Khatami era, founded in 2003. Suspended multiple times — most notably in September 2006 simultaneously with the lifting of the *Iran* newspaper ban, and after the [2009 Green Movement](/events/green-movement-protests-2009) — yet consistently resumed. Journalists Niloofar Hamedi and Elaheh Mohammadi of Shargh broke the Mahsa Amini story in September 2022 and were imprisoned 17 months for it.

Sobh-e Emroz (صبح امروز, 'This Morning') was founded by [Saeed Hajjarian](/people/saeed-hajjarian), Khatami's chief political strategist and a former deputy Minister of Intelligence. It was the investigative flagship of the reformist press, publishing [Akbar Ganji](/people/akbar-ganji) and [Emadeddin Baghi](/people/emadeddin-baghi)'s landmark series on the [chain murders of 1998](/events/chain-murders-iran-1998) using code names ('Excellency Red Garmented' for Rafsanjani, 'Master Key' for [Ali Fallahian](/people/ali-fallahian)). This reporting forced the government to admit Ministry of Intelligence 'rogue elements' were responsible. On 12 March 2000, Hajjarian was shot in the face outside Tehran City Council by a Basij gunman, leaving him permanently disabled. Sobh-e Emroz was among the 16 papers closed in the 24 April 2000 crackdown.

nodes.label: Society of Seminary Teachers of Qom

data.name_en: Society of Seminary Teachers of Qom
data.description: The Society of Seminary Teachers of Qom (Jame'eh-ye Modarresin-e Howzeh-ye Elmiyyeh-ye Qom) is an influential association of Shia clerics based in the Qom seminary. Founded in 1961, it became a key vehicle for [Ruhollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini)'s political movement and played a central role in the [Iranian Revolution](/events/iranian-revolution-1979). The society's members dominated the [Assembly of Experts for the Constitution](/orgs/assembly-of-experts-for-constitution) in 1979 and have maintained significant influence over the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council) and the [Assembly of Experts](/orgs/assembly-of-experts). It is considered a conservative-to-hardline clerical body closely aligned with the ruling establishment.

nodes.label: Supreme Council of the Cultural Revolution

data.name_en: Supreme Council of the Cultural Revolution
data.description: The Supreme Council of the Cultural Revolution is an Iranian state body created in December 1984 to succeed the original seven-member Cultural Revolution Committee (also known as the Cultural Revolution Headquarters) that [Ruhollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini) established in June 1980. The original committee was charged with closing universities, purging them of Western and secular influences, and reshaping academic curricula along Islamic lines during the [Iranian Cultural Revolution](/events/iranian-cultural-revolution-1980). The council continues to set cultural and educational policy for the Islamic Republic, including oversight of universities, regulation of the internet, and media policy.

The Supreme National Security Council (Shora-ye Ali-ye Amniat-e Melli, SNSC) was established by the 1989 constitutional revision to coordinate Iran's defence, intelligence, and foreign policy. The President chairs it; the Secretary — appointed with the Supreme Leader's approval — is effectively Iran's national security adviser and a direct representative of the [Office of the Supreme Leader](/orgs/office-of-the-supreme-leader). The SNSC has also been Iran's primary nuclear negotiating body. Key secretaries include [Hassan Rouhani](/people/hassan-rouhani) (1989–2005), [Ali Larijani](/people/ali-larijani) (2005–2007), and Saeed Jalili (2007–2013).

Tous (طوس) was the direct successor to [Jame'eh](/orgs/jameeh-newspaper), relaunched by [Mashallah Shamsolvaezin](/people/mashallah-shamsolvaezin) and [Hamid Reza Jalalipour](/people/hamid-reza-jalalipour) with the same staff under a new masthead and dormant license. Tous was also closed within months, and Shamsolvaezin immediately relaunched as [Neshat](/orgs/neshat-newspaper). The pattern — acquire a dormant license and restart with the same team — became the defining structural feature of Khatami-era reformist journalism.

The University of Bradford is a public university in Bradford, West Yorkshire, England. Founded in 1966, it is known for its programmes in peace studies, political science, and social sciences. Several Iranian academics and political commentators have studied there.

The University of Tehran is Iran's oldest and most prominent modern university. Founded in 1934, it played a central role in the [Iranian Cultural Revolution](/events/iranian-cultural-revolution-1980) of 1980–1983, during which it was closed along with all other Iranian universities and purged of secular and leftist faculty. It remains Iran's leading academic institution across the sciences, humanities, and social sciences.

Vatan-e Emroz (وطن امروز, 'Today's Homeland') is a hardline daily in the IRGC-linked neo-radical media ecosystem, alongside the Fars and Tasnim news agencies and the Mashregh and Raja news websites. It was a strong Ahmadinejad supporter.

Zan (زن, 'Woman') was a reformist daily founded by Faezeh Hashemi Rafsanjani, daughter of [Akbar Hashemi Rafsanjani](/people/akbar-hashemi-rafsanjani) and a reformist MP. Closed by Islamic Revolutionary Court in April 1999 for publishing a cartoon satirising blood money laws (which valued a woman's life at half a man's), and for reprinting a New Year's greeting from Empress Farah Pahlavi. Demonstrated that even publications with powerful family connections were not immune.

data.name: Algiers Accords
data.description: The Algiers Accords were agreements between Iran and the United States, mediated by Algeria, that resolved the Iran hostage crisis and secured the release of 52 American embassy staff held in Tehran for 444 days. [Behzad Nabavi](/people/behzad-nabavi) served as Iran's chief negotiator. The accords were signed on 19 January 1981, and the hostages were released on 20 January, minutes after Ronald Reagan's inauguration as US president.

data.name: Assassination of Kazem Rajavi
data.description: On 24 April 1990, [Kazem Rajavi](/people/kazem-rajavi) — professor of political science at the University of Geneva, human rights advocate, and elder brother of MEK leader [Massoud Rajavi](/people/massoud-rajavi) — was assassinated near his home in Coppet, Switzerland. He was shot at close range by multiple [Iranian intelligence](/orgs/ministry-of-intelligence) agents who had entered Switzerland on diplomatic passports. [Ali Fallahian](/people/ali-fallahian), then intelligence minister under [Akbar Hashemi Rafsanjani](/people/akbar-hashemi-rafsanjani), was charged by a Swiss court with masterminding the operation. In 2020, the Swiss case was reopened to be investigated as a crime against humanity.

data.name: Chain Murders of Iranian Intellectuals (1988–1998)
data.description:
The chain murders (قتل‌های زنجیره‌ای) were a decade-long campaign of state-sponsored assassinations carried out by a unit within the [Ministry of Intelligence](/orgs/ministry-of-intelligence), targeting Iranian intellectuals, writers, poets, political activists, and dissidents from 1988 to 1998. The campaign was led by MOIS deputy [Saeed Emami](/people/saeed-emami), who reported to Intelligence Minister [Ali Fallahian](/people/ali-fallahian). Victims numbered over 80 people. Methods included stabbing, strangulation, staged car accidents, lethal injections of potassium, and poisoning.

The murders came to public attention in late 1998 when five prominent figures were killed in quick succession: [Pirouz Davani](/people/pirouz-davani) (disappeared August 1998), [Hamid Hajizadeh](/people/hamid-hajizadeh) and his 9-year-old son (stabbed September 1998), [Majid Sharif](/people/majid-sharif) (potassium injection, November 1998), [Dariush Forouhar](/people/dariush-forouhar) and [Parvaneh Forouhar](/people/parvaneh-forouhar) (stabbed in their home, 22 November 1998), [Mohammad Mokhtari](/people/mohammad-mokhtari) (strangled, December 1998), and [Mohammad Jafar Pouyandeh](/people/mohammad-jafar-pouyandeh) (strangled, December 1998). Earlier victims included [Kazem Sami](/people/kazem-sami) (axed to death 1988, likely the first victim), [Ali Akbar Saidi Sirjani](/people/ali-akbar-saidi-sirjani) (killed in custody 1994), [Ahmad Tafazzoli](/people/ahmad-tafazzoli) (killed 1997), and [Ebrahim Zalzadeh](/people/ebrahim-zalzadeh) (arrested and killed 1997). [Ahmad Khomeini](/people/ahmad-khomeini), son of the revolution's founder, is widely believed to have been poisoned in 1995 as part of the same campaign. In August 1996, 21 members of the [Iranian Writers' Association](/orgs/iranian-writers-association) narrowly survived when a MOIS operative attempted to drive their bus off a cliff at Heyran Pass.

The MOIS issued an unprecedented admission on 6 January 1999 that its agents were responsible. Intelligence Minister [Qorbanali Dorri-Najafabadi](/people/qorbanali-dorri-najafabadi) resigned. [Saeed Emami](/people/saeed-emami) was arrested but died in custody in June 1999 — officially by suicide, widely believed murdered to protect those who gave the orders. Fifteen operatives were tried in 2000–2001; three were sentenced to death and others to prison terms, but the trial was widely regarded as a sham. Journalist [Akbar Ganji](/people/akbar-ganji) identified Fallahian as the 'Master Key' and was imprisoned for six years. [Saeed Hajjarian](/people/saeed-hajjarian), whose newspaper [Sobh-e Emroz](/orgs/sobh-e-emroz-newspaper) broke the story, was shot in the head in March 2000 and left paralysed for life. Lawyer [Nasser Zarafshan](/people/nasser-zarafshan) was imprisoned for representing victims' families. [Shirin Ebadi](/people/shirin-ebadi), who represented the Forouhar family, discovered she herself had been on the murder list.

data.name: 1979 Constitutional Assembly Election
data.description: The election for the [Assembly of Experts for the Constitution](/orgs/assembly-of-experts-for-constitution) was held on 3–4 August 1979, six months after the [Iranian Revolution](/events/iranian-revolution-1979). Voters chose 73 members to draft the new constitution. Of 428 registered candidates, the [Islamic Republican Party](/orgs/islamic-republican-party) and Khomeini-aligned clerics won approximately 55 seats. Turnout was 51.7% (10.8 million of 20.9 million eligible voters). The [Freedom Movement of Iran](/orgs/freedom-movement-of-iran) and secular nationalists won a small minority of seats. [Abdul Rahman Ghassemlou](/people/abdul-rahman-ghassemlou), leader of the [Democratic Party of Iranian Kurdistan](/orgs/democratic-party-of-iranian-kurdistan), won over 80% of the vote in West Azerbaijan but his credentials were rejected on [Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini)'s orders.

sources.title: The 1979 Assembly of Experts for the Drafting of the Constitution Election

data.name: 1979 Iranian Constitutional Referendum
data.description: The constitutional referendum held on 2–3 December 1979 submitted the draft constitution produced by the [Assembly of Experts for the Constitution](/orgs/assembly-of-experts-for-constitution) to a popular vote. The constitution, which embedded the doctrine of velayat-e faqih and granted sweeping powers to a Supreme Leader, was approved with 99.5% of votes cast. Turnout was 75.2% (15.7 million voters). The referendum was boycotted by leftist parties, secular nationalists, Kurdish autonomy movements including the [Democratic Party of Iranian Kurdistan](/orgs/democratic-party-of-iranian-kurdistan), followers of Ayatollah Shariatmadari, and the [Mojahedin-e Khalq](/orgs/mojahedin-e-khalq). Turnout was notably low in Kurdistan, Sistan-Baluchestan, and Azerbaijan provinces.

data.name: 1989 Constitutional Revision
data.description: The 1989 constitutional revision amended the Constitution of the Islamic Republic shortly before [Ruhollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini)'s death. Key changes included abolishing the position of prime minister and merging its powers into the presidency, establishing the [Supreme National Security Council](/orgs/supreme-national-security-council) and the [Expediency Discernment Council](/orgs/expediency-discernment-council) as constitutional bodies, removing the requirement that the Supreme Leader be a marja (senior religious authority), and expanding the Supreme Leader's powers. The revision was approved by referendum on 28 July 1989, the same day as the presidential election that brought [Akbar Hashemi Rafsanjani](/people/akbar-hashemi-rafsanjani) to office.

data.name: Death of Grand Ayatollah Montazeri
data.description: Grand Ayatollah [Hossein-Ali Montazeri](/people/hossein-ali-montazeri) died on 19 December 2009 at his home in Qom at the age of 87. As the most senior clerical critic of the Islamic Republic and the most authoritative religious voice supporting the [Green Movement](/events/green-movement-protests-2009), his death triggered massive mourning gatherings in Qom, Isfahan, and his hometown Najafabad that became political protests. The regime attempted to suppress the ceremonies but could not prevent them. The fortieth-day mourning coincided with Ashura (27 December 2009), leading to some of the most violent clashes of the Green Movement. His death removed the highest-ranking clerical opponent of [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei)'s leadership.

sources.title: Profile: Grand Ayatollah Hossein Ali Montazeri

data.name: Execution of Ruhollah Zam (2020)
data.description: Dissident journalist [Ruhollah Zam](/people/ruhollah-zam), founder of the Amad News Telegram channel, was executed on 12 December 2020 after being lured from exile in France to Iraq and captured by [IRGC Intelligence](/orgs/irgc-intelligence-organisation) operatives in October 2019. His forced confession was broadcast on [IRIB](/orgs/irib). A revolutionary court convicted him of 'corruption on earth' for his role in covering the December 2017 protests through Amad News, which had over one million followers. His execution was condemned by France, the European Union, and international human rights organisations. He was the son of reformist cleric Ahmad Zam.

data.name: Assassination of Mohsen Fakhrizadeh
data.description: Mohsen Fakhrizadeh, head of the IRGC's clandestine Amad nuclear weapons research programme and deputy Defence Minister, was assassinated on 27 November 2020 near Absard, east of Tehran. A sophisticated remote-controlled ambush used a weapon mounted on a truck to target his vehicle. Iran and Western intelligence attributed the killing to Israeli intelligence (Mossad), though Israel did not officially claim responsibility. Fakhrizadeh had been identified by the IAEA as the central figure in Iran's military nuclear work. His death was the most high-profile in a series of targeted killings of Iranian nuclear scientists and defence officials attributed to Israel since 2010. The Majlis responded by passing legislation requiring the government to increase enrichment to 20% and restrict IAEA inspector access — accelerating the nuclear programme and hastening the JCPOA's collapse. [Ali Akbar Salehi](/people/ali-akbar-salehi) at the [Atomic Energy Organisation of Iran](/orgs/atomic-energy-organisation-of-iran) described him as the 'shahid of science'.

data.name: Green Movement Protests
data.description: Following the disputed June 2009 presidential election in which incumbent [Mahmoud Ahmadinejad](/people/mahmoud-ahmadinejad) was declared the winner over reformist candidates [Mir-Hossein Mousavi](/people/mir-hossein-mousavi) and [Mehdi Karroubi](/people/mehdi-karroubi), millions of Iranians took to the streets in the largest protests since the 1979 revolution. The government response was violent; the [Basij](/orgs/basij) and [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) killed dozens of protesters. The movement was suppressed by late 2009, and its leaders Mousavi and Karroubi were placed under house arrest in 2011.

data.name: Hafte Tir Bombing
data.description: On 28 June 1981 (7 Tir 1360 in the Iranian calendar), a bomb exploded at the headquarters of the [Islamic Republican Party](/orgs/islamic-republican-party) in Tehran during a leadership meeting, killing over 70 people including [Mohammad Beheshti](/people/mohammad-beheshti), four cabinet ministers, 27 members of parliament, and other senior officials. It was one of the deadliest political assassinations in modern Iranian history. The attack is generally attributed to the [Mojahedin-e Khalq](/orgs/mojahedin-e-khalq) (MEK), though the group has denied responsibility and attribution remains disputed by some analysts.

data.name: Iran Experts Initiative Email Leak
data.description: In September 2023, [Iran International](/orgs/iran-international) and [Semafor](/orgs/semafor) published thousands of leaked Iranian [Foreign Ministry](/orgs/ministry-of-foreign-affairs) emails dating back to 2014, revealing the Iran Experts Initiative (IEI) — a covert influence operation run through the Ministry's think tank, the [Institute for Political and International Studies (IPIS)](/orgs/ipis). The emails showed that [Saeed Khatibzadeh](/people/saeed-khatibzadeh), a Berlin-based diplomat, conceived the initiative in March 2014 and recruited a core group of Western-based Iranian academics — including [Ariane Tabatabai](/people/ariane-tabatabai), [Dina Esfandiary](/people/dina-esfandiary), [Ali Vaez](/people/ali-vaez), [Adnan Tabatabai](/people/adnan-tabatabai), [Ellie Geranmayeh](/people/ellie-geranmayeh), and [Rouzbeh Parsi](/people/rouzbeh-parsi) — to promote Tehran's positions on the nuclear deal in Western media and policy circles. [Mostafa Zahrani](/people/mostafa-zahrani), then head of IPIS, oversaw the operation and served as the main liaison between the network and [Mohammad Javad Zarif](/people/javad-zarif)'s Foreign Ministry. The leak revealed that members shared article drafts with Iranian officials before publication, received guidance on conference attendance, and that one member offered to ghostwrite op-eds for Tehran. At least three IEI members — Tabatabai, Vaez, and Esfandiary — later worked for or advised [Robert Malley](/people/robert-malley), the Biden administration's special envoy on Iran, who was himself placed on leave and investigated by the FBI in 2023. The revelations triggered a Pentagon security review of Tabatabai and congressional demands for investigation.

data.name: Iran-Contra Affair
data.description: The Iran-Contra affair was a covert arrangement in which the United States secretly sold arms to Iran — then under an arms embargo and engaged in the [Iran-Iraq War](/events/iran-iraq-war) — in exchange for Iran's assistance in securing the release of American hostages held by [Hezbollah](/orgs/hezbollah) in Lebanon. Profits from the sales were illegally diverted to fund Contra rebels in Nicaragua. On the Iranian side, [Akbar Hashemi Rafsanjani](/people/akbar-hashemi-rafsanjani), then Speaker of the Majlis, was the principal interlocutor whom U.S. officials believed led a 'moderate' faction open to rapprochement. [Manucher Ghorbanifar](/people/manucher-ghorbanifar), an expatriate Iranian arms dealer, served as the initial intermediary, while [Hassan Karoubi](/people/hassan-karoubi) met with Ghorbanifar and U.S. officials in Geneva in October 1985 to propose the missile-for-hostage exchange. [Mohsen Kangarloo](/people/mohsen-kangarloo), security adviser to Prime Minister [Mir-Hossein Mousavi](/people/mir-hossein-mousavi), negotiated arms transfers in Germany in February 1986. Between August 1985 and October 1986, Iran received over 2,000 TOW anti-tank missiles and Hawk anti-aircraft missile parts through Israeli and then direct U.S. channels. In May 1986, former U.S. National Security Adviser Robert McFarlane led a secret delegation to Tehran but failed to reach agreement with Iranian officials. From August 1986, [Ali Hashemi Bahramani](/people/ali-hashemi-bahramani), Rafsanjani's nephew and an [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) officer, replaced Ghorbanifar as the primary Iranian contact — the 'second channel.' The affair was exposed on 3 November 1986 when the Lebanese magazine Ash-Shiraa published details leaked by [Mehdi Hashemi](/people/mehdi-hashemi), an [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) official aligned with [Hossein-Ali Montazeri](/people/hossein-ali-montazeri) who opposed the secret dealings. Hashemi was arrested and executed in September 1987. The scandal damaged both the Reagan administration and the credibility of Iranian 'moderates,' and contributed to Montazeri's eventual removal as Khomeini's designated successor.

data.name: Iran Hostage Crisis
data.description: On 4 November 1979, a group of Islamist students calling themselves 'Muslim Student Followers of the Imam's Line,' led by [Mohammad Mousavi Khoeiniha](/people/mohammad-mousavi-khoeiniha), stormed the US Embassy in Tehran and took 52 American diplomats and citizens hostage. The crisis lasted 444 days, ending with the release of the hostages on 20 January 1981 under the [Algiers Accords](/events/algiers-accords-1981). [Abbas Abdi](/people/abbas-abdi) was among the prominent student participants. The crisis cemented the revolutionary regime's anti-American identity and led to the severing of US-Iran diplomatic relations.

data.name: Iran-Iraq War
data.description: The Iran-Iraq War (1980–1988) was an eight-year conflict initiated by Iraq's invasion of Iran in September 1980. It was the longest conventional war of the twentieth century, killing an estimated 500,000 to 1 million people on both sides. The war shaped a generation of Iranian military and political leaders, particularly those who went on to lead the [Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps). [Mir-Hossein Mousavi](/people/mir-hossein-mousavi) served as prime minister throughout the conflict. The war ended with UN-brokered ceasefire Resolution 598, which [Ruhollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini) accepted, comparing it to 'drinking poison'.

data.name: Iran Press Crackdown — April 2000
data.description: On 24 April 2000, the day after Iranian reformists celebrated their landslide victory in February's parliamentary elections (taking three-quarters of seats), Press Court Judge [Saeed Mortazavi](/people/saeed-mortazavi) executed an order closing 16 to 21 reformist newspapers overnight — the single largest assault on the Iranian press in the Islamic Republic's history. The action directly followed a speech by [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) branding reformist newspapers 'enemy bases' that 'appear to be directed from the same centre' to 'make the people pessimistic about the system.' Mortazavi cited a 1960 crime-prevention law to justify mass closures without individual hearings. Papers closed included [Sobh-e Emroz](/orgs/sobh-e-emroz-newspaper), [Mosharekat](/orgs/mosharekat-newspaper), [Ham-Mihan](/orgs/ham-mihan-newspaper), [Asr-e Azadegan](/orgs/asr-e-azadegan-newspaper), and many others. The same week, [Saeed Hajjarian](/people/saeed-hajjarian) was shot in the face and severely disabled. [Akbar Ganji](/people/akbar-ganji) was arrested on 22 April; [Emadeddin Baghi](/people/emadeddin-baghi) on 29 May. When the reformist-dominated Sixth Majlis tried to pass a press freedom bill in August 2000, Khamenei personally ordered the bill removed from the parliamentary agenda. By February 2004 Mortazavi had personally overseen the closure or license revocation of approximately 90–120 publications.

sources.title: Freedom of the press in Iran

data.name: Iran Student Uprising — July 1999
data.description: The July 1999 student uprising was the largest protests in Iran since the 1979 revolution, directly triggered by the closure of the reformist newspaper [Salam](/orgs/salam-newspaper). On 7 July 1999, the Special Court for the Clergy closed Salam after it published a leaked MOIS document suggesting the Ministry of Intelligence had recommended restricting the press and may have been involved in the [chain murders of 1998](/events/chain-murders-iran-1998). That night, riot police and Basij attacked the dormitories at Tehran University where students were protesting the closure, forcing entry at 1am and beating students in their beds. At least four students were killed and hundreds injured over six days of protests that spread to multiple cities. Among those who played central roles in the dormitory attacks were three IRGC commanders: Brigadier General Mohammad Bagher Zolghadr, Brigadier General Mohammad Reza Naghdi (later commander of the Basij), and Brigadier General Hossein Nejat. The protests were suppressed within six days. None of the commanders responsible for the dormitory attacks were prosecuted during the Khatami era, a failure that became a defining symbol of reformist political impotence.

data.name: Iranian Cultural Revolution
data.description: In June 1980, Ayatollah Khomeini ordered the closure of all Iranian universities to purge them of Western and secular influences. The Cultural Revolution lasted until approximately 1983, during which thousands of students and faculty were expelled. A Cultural Revolutionary Headquarters was established, later replaced by the Supreme Council of the Cultural Revolution in 1984. The campaign resulted in the expulsion of all Baháʼí students and professors, bans on Western cultural influences, and the emigration of thousands of academics and technocrats.

data.name: Iranian Revolution
data.description: The Islamic Revolution of 1979 overthrew the Pahlavi monarchy and established the Islamic Republic of Iran. Following months of mass protests, Shah Mohammad Reza Pahlavi fled Iran on 16 January 1979. [Ayatollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini) returned from exile on 1 February 1979. The revolutionary government took power on 11 February 1979, the date now commemorated as the Day of Victory of the Islamic Revolution. A constitutional referendum in March confirmed the Islamic Republic, and a new theocratic constitution was approved in December 1979.

nodes.label: Israeli Strikes on Iran — Operation Rising Lion (June 2025)

data.name: Israeli Strikes on Iran — Operation Rising Lion (June 2025)
data.description: On 13 June 2025, Israel launched large-scale airstrikes on Iran targeting military command centres, nuclear infrastructure, and senior IRGC leadership — an operation Israel called 'Operation Rising Lion'. The strikes, described as pre-emptive, killed approximately 30 IRGC generals in the opening attack, including [Hossein Salami](/people/hossein-salami) (IRGC Commander-in-Chief), [Amir Ali Hajizadeh](/people/amir-ali-hajizadeh) (Aerospace Force commander), [Esmail Qaani](/people/esmail-qaani) (Quds Force commander), and Mohammad Bagheri (Chief of Staff of the Armed Forces). US airstrikes simultaneously damaged nuclear facilities at Natanz, Isfahan, and Fordow. The strikes initiated what became known as the 'Twelve-Day War'. [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) was killed in a subsequent strike on 28 February 2026, triggering the [succession crisis](/events/mojtaba-khamenei-supreme-leader-2026) that resulted in [Mojtaba Khamenei](/people/mojtaba-khamenei) becoming Supreme Leader.

data.name: January 2026 Iran Massacres
data.description: Between 8 and 25 January 2026, Iranian security forces killed thousands of civilians during a crackdown on the 2025-2026 protests. [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) ordered the [Supreme National Security Council](/orgs/supreme-national-security-council) to crush the protests by any means necessary on 9 January. [Ali Larijani](/people/ali-larijani), secretary of the SNSC, was identified as the chief planner. The [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps), [Basij](/orgs/basij), and [Law Enforcement Command](/orgs/law-enforcement-command) carried out the killings using live ammunition, military-grade weapons, and snipers. Iraqi and Lebanese militias including the [Popular Mobilization Forces](/orgs/popular-mobilization-forces) and [Hezbollah](/orgs/hezbollah) were reported to have participated. Death toll estimates range from HRANA's verified count of over 7,000 to unverified estimates exceeding 30,000. The UN Special Rapporteur stated the killings may constitute crimes against humanity.
